en.content:
The four inevitable stages of grocery shopping as a couple:

Stage one: "We're just here for milk." — the pre-entry innocence.

Stage two: the cart now contains cookies, frozen meals, two bottles of soy sauce we don't need, and a snack he "won't forgive himself for not buying." The milk is still on the shelf.

Stage three: I turn around. He's gone. I text: "where are you?" He replies: "I thought you were behind me." — Getting lost in the same supermarket is the most absurdly romantic thing modern love offers.

Stage four: we reunite in the produce section. He's holding something completely unrelated to the original plan.
"It's on sale."
"We don't need it."
"But it's on sale."
"...Fine."

At checkout, we both stare at the total. Five seconds of silence.
"We were only here for milk."
"Yeah."
"...Next time, a list."
"Okay."

Neither of us will write one. We both know.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who loses you in the supermarket — caption it "hold my hand next time." They'll reply: "you let go first." The grocery store is a permanent relationship testing ground
variations: Advanced: we actually wrote the list. Three minutes after entering the store he texts: "list is on the kitchen table." That's a relationship — fully prepared, and still entirely at the mercy of luck.

items.sourceNote: 情侶在家裡互相傳訊息的懶惰行為，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
交往超過兩年的情侶，傳訊息的距離分佈：

沙發到廚房（三公尺）：「幫我倒一杯水。」
房間到客廳（五公尺）：「電視遙控器在你那邊嗎？」
廁所到房間（兩公尺）：「衛生紙沒了。」
床的這頭到那頭（八十公分）：「你睡了嗎？」

我們不是懶——我們是在用科技維繫感情。

那天我朋友來家裡作客，看到我們各自坐在沙發兩端傳訊息，她說：「你們不能直接講嗎？」
我說：「講話要抬頭。」
她沉默了三秒，然後拿出手機傳訊息給她男友：「我懂了。」
他在隔壁房間。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個在家裡也會傳訊息給你的另一半——附上「下次直接喊」。對方一定回你『喊太累了』，然後繼續傳訊息
variations: 進階版：有一次我傳訊息問他要不要喝水，他在隔壁房間，沒有回我。我走過去看，他睡著了——手機還亮著，停在我的訊息上。我幫他關掉螢幕，自己倒了水。那一刻我突然懂了：所謂的家，就是不用每一句話都要被回應。

en.content:
Texting distances for couples past the two-year mark:

Couch to kitchen (3 meters): "Bring me water?"
Bedroom to living room (5 meters): "Is the remote with you?"
Bathroom to bedroom (2 meters): "Out of toilet paper."
One side of the bed to the other (80 centimeters): "You asleep?"

We're not lazy. We're using technology to nurture intimacy.

A friend came over once and watched us text each other from opposite ends of the couch. She said: "Why don't you just talk?"
I said: "Talking requires looking up."
Three seconds of silence. Then she pulled out her phone and texted her boyfriend: "I get it now."
He was in the next room.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner you text from the same house — caption it "next time just yell." They'll reply "yelling is exhausting" and keep texting
variations: Advanced: I once texted him asking if he wanted water. He was in the next room. No reply. I walked over — he was asleep. Phone still glowing, my message on the screen. I turned it off and got my own water. That's when I understood: home is the place where not every sentence needs a response.

items.sourceNote: 情侶共用密碼與已讀焦慮，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
情侶感情有三個階段，由「已讀」這個功能精準量測：

第一階段（一個月內）：訊息傳出去三十秒內已讀，回覆速度比 ChatGPT 還快，因為他根本沒在做別的事，只是在等你傳訊息。

第二階段（半年到一年）：已讀變成「等一下回」，等一下變成「忘了」，忘了變成「啊我以為我回了」。但你並沒有生氣，因為你也對他做過一樣的事。

第三階段（兩年以上）：訊息已讀不回三小時你完全不焦慮，因為你知道他不是不回——他只是在打遊戲、在洗澡、在跟主管講電話、或是手機放在桌上他在客廳吃水果。已讀不回從威脅變成生活背景音。

感情走到最後，已讀不再是一種懸念，而是一種默契。
你知道他會回，他知道你會等。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個已讀你三小時你卻完全不會生氣的另一半——附上「謝謝你訓練我的心臟」。對方一定回你『不客氣，我也被你訓練過』
variations: 番外篇：交往八年那天，他傳訊息給我，我已讀了三天才回。回完之後我才發現——他根本沒發現我隔了三天。我們對彼此的時間感，早就同步成同一台時鐘了。

en.content:
Relationship stages, as measured precisely by the "Read" receipt:

Stage one (first month): read within thirty seconds, reply faster than ChatGPT — because he isn't doing anything else, he's just waiting for your message.

Stage two (six months to a year): "Read" becomes "reply in a sec," which becomes "forgot," which becomes "wait, I thought I replied." You don't get mad. You've done the exact same thing to him.

Stage three (two years and up): three hours of "Read" with no reply, zero anxiety. You know he isn't ignoring you — he's playing a game, in the shower, on a call with his boss, or his phone is on the table while he eats fruit in the living room. "Read" stopped being a threat. It's just background noise now.

In the end, the "Read" receipt isn't suspense anymore. It's trust.
You know he'll reply. He knows you'll wait.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who leaves you on read for three hours without it bothering you — caption it "thanks for training my nervous system." They'll reply "you trained mine too"
variations: Bonus: on our eighth anniversary he sent me a message. I left it on read for three days before replying. After I sent it, I realized — he never noticed the gap. Our internal clocks had synced into the same time zone years ago.

items.sourceNote: 情侶睡覺搶棉被與佔床位的戰爭，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
情侶睡同一張床，會經歷四個必然戰役：

戰役一：棉被分配。你蓋一半，他蓋一半——理論上。實際上凌晨三點你會冷醒，發現整條棉被都在他身上，他像捲春捲一樣把自己捲進去，臉上還掛著無辜的睡相。

戰役二：床位侵略。明明是一張雙人床，他卻能用一個對角線斜躺的姿勢佔掉七成空間，把你擠到床邊三十公分的位置，你睡得像在懸崖邊緣。

戰役三：冷氣溫度。你要二十四度，他要二十六度。妥協方案是二十五度——結果兩個人都覺得不舒服。這就是民主。

戰役四：誰先關燈。「你關。」「你比較近。」「你剛剛動過。」「你是男生。」最後是手機亮度調到最低，當作小夜燈用，沒有人關燈。

隔天早上醒來，棉被在地上，他在床的另一端，你在最邊邊，冷氣已經自動關掉——而你竟然覺得這就是幸福。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個會在睡夢中把你擠下床的另一半——附上「今晚我要奪回棉被」。對方一定回你『試試看』。然後當晚棉被還是會在他身上
variations: 深夜版：有一次我醒來發現他在睡夢中把棉被蓋在我身上，自己縮成一團。我把棉被分一半回去給他，他迷迷糊糊地說了一句『不用，我不冷』，然後又把棉被推回我這邊。那一刻我才懂——有些愛是醒著的時候不會說的，是睡著了才會自己跑出來。

en.content:
Sharing a bed as a couple means surviving four inevitable battles:

Battle one: blanket allocation. You take half, he takes half — in theory. In practice, at 3 a.m. you wake up freezing to find the entire blanket has migrated. He's rolled into it like a burrito, sleeping with the innocent face of someone who has done nothing wrong.

Battle two: territorial invasion. It's a double bed, yet he manages to claim seventy percent of it by lying on a perfect diagonal, leaving you a thirty-centimeter strip on the edge. You sleep like someone clinging to a cliff.

Battle three: thermostat warfare. You want 24°C. He wants 26°C. The compromise is 25°C — and both of you are uncomfortable. This is democracy.

Battle four: who turns off the light. "You." "You're closer." "You moved last." "You're the guy." Eventually someone dims their phone to the lowest brightness and uses it as a night light. Nobody turns off the lamp.

Next morning: blanket on the floor, him on the far edge, you clinging to the other edge, AC auto-shut off hours ago. And somehow you think: this is happiness.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who pushes you off the bed in their sleep — caption it "tonight I take back the blanket." They'll reply "good luck." The blanket will still end up on them
variations: Late-night version: I woke up once to find he had draped the blanket over me and curled up cold on his own side. I split it back over him. Half-asleep he mumbled "I'm not cold" and pushed it back to me. That's when I got it — some kinds of love only come out when you're asleep, because awake, you'd never admit them.

items.sourceNote: 情侶共用浴室與牙刷的日常，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
同居後才會發現的浴室真相：

一、牙刷會自動換位置。你明明把它放在右邊，隔天就出現在左邊。沒有人承認，但兩個人都覺得是對方移動的。

二、洗髮精永遠在你頭上抹滿泡沫的那一刻用完。「欸——你那邊還有沒有？」「沒有。」「那你昨天用什麼？」「你的。」

三、毛巾的所有權是流動的。你以為這條是你的，他以為那條是他的，最後變成「比較乾的那條就是我的」。

四、馬桶座的位置永遠是錯的。男生掀起來會被念，放下來自己又忘記要掀。女生坐下去發現是冷的瓷器那一刻，整段感情都會被檢討。

五、最後兩個人會默默買兩支一樣顏色的牙刷，然後永遠分不出哪支是誰的。從那天起，這件事就不再是問題了——因為你們已經不在意了。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給剛同居的朋友，附上「歡迎來到地獄」——他三個月後會回你「比我想的還好笑」
variations: 進階版：交往十年後，浴室裡的東西已經沒有「你的」「我的」之分。連護髮素都是兩個人輪流用，洗面乳也是一支共用。這不是浪漫，這是極簡主義——以及徹底的疲憊。

en.content:
Truths about sharing a bathroom you only learn after moving in:

One: toothbrushes migrate. You leave yours on the right, next morning it's on the left. Nobody confesses. Both of you suspect the other.

Two: shampoo always runs out the exact moment your head is fully lathered. "Hey — do you have any?" "No." "What did you use yesterday?" "Yours."

Three: towel ownership is fluid. You think this one's yours, he thinks that one's his. Eventually the rule becomes: whichever is drier is mine.

Four: the toilet seat is always wrong. Up — you get scolded. Down — you forget to lift it. The moment she sits on cold porcelain, the entire relationship goes on trial.

Five: eventually you both buy identical toothbrushes in the same color and can never tell them apart. From that day on, it stops being an issue — because you stop caring.
en.editorNote: Send to a friend who just moved in with their partner — caption: "welcome to hell." They'll reply three months later: "funnier than I expected"
variations: Advanced version: ten years in, nothing in the bathroom is labeled 'his' or 'hers' anymore. You share conditioner. You share face wash. It's not romance — it's minimalism. And total exhaustion.

items.sourceNote: 老夫老妻的訊息日常——浪漫不再，效率至上，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
情侶訊息的進化史：

交往一個月：
「寶貝你今天有沒有想我❤️」
「想到不行😘😘😘」
「我也是嗚嗚嗚」
（連續發送二十三則）

交往三年：
「下班要不要去吃那家？」
「好。」
（對話結束）

交往七年：
「衛生紙。」
「收到。」
（沒有主詞、沒有句子、沒有表情——但兩個人都知道對方在說什麼）

結婚十年：
「。」
「我也是。」

他們不是不愛了，他們是把所有要說的話都變成了暗號，連標點符號都能傳達整段話。這就是時間給感情的禮物：你不用說，他就懂。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個跟你只用句點交流的另一半——附上「我們已經進化到不用打字了」。對方一定回你一個「嗯」
variations: 極限版：他傳「？」我回「。」就解決了一場可能要吵兩小時的架。我們連吵架都不需要動用完整句子了。

en.content:
The evolution of couple texting:

Month one:
"Babe are you thinking of me ❤️"
"Constantly 😘😘😘"
"Me too 🥺🥺"
(twenty-three consecutive messages)

Year three:
"Dinner at that place after work?"
"Sure."
(end of conversation)

Year seven:
"Toilet paper."
"Got it."
(no subject, no sentence, no emoji — yet both of you know exactly what's happening)

Year ten of marriage:
"."
"Same."

They haven't fallen out of love. They've just compressed every conversation into code — a single punctuation mark can carry a paragraph. That's what time gives a relationship: you don't have to say it. They already know.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner you only communicate with via single periods — caption: "we've evolved past typing." They will reply with "yep"
variations: Extreme version: he texted "?" and I replied "." and we resolved a two-hour argument before it started. We don't even need full sentences to fight anymore.

items.sourceNote: 情侶搶手機充電線大戰，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
情侶之間最大的戰爭，不是錢，不是家事，是充電線。

家裡明明有三條，但每次要用的時候只剩一條，而那一條一定插在對方的手機上。

「我手機快沒電了。」
「我也是。」
「我只剩百分之五。」
「我百分之三。」
「你怎麼可能比我低，你剛剛才在充。」
「我剛剛拔掉了。」
「為什麼拔掉？」
「因為我以為我充飽了。」
「那你現在在幹嘛？」
「在跟你吵充電線。」

最後解決方案：兩個人各自抱著手機，看著電量慢慢往下掉，比賽誰能撐到對方先去買一條新的。
通常都是同一個人先妥協。也通常那個人不是你。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給每次都搶你充電線的另一半——附上「我已經買第四條了」。對方一定回「那剛好我的壞了」
variations: 升級版：搬到一起住之後，我們家有七條充電線。每次需要用的時候，七條都不見了。後來才知道——它們都在沙發底下，跟襪子住在一起。

en.content:
The biggest war between couples isn't money or chores. It's the phone charger.

There are three in the apartment. The moment you need one, only one exists — and it's plugged into their phone.

"My phone's about to die."
"Mine too."
"I'm at five percent."
"I'm at three."
"How are you lower than me? You were just charging."
"I unplugged."
"Why?"
"Thought I was full."
"What are you doing right now?"
"Arguing with you about the charger."

Final resolution: both of you cradle your phones, watch the battery tick down, and silently compete to see who breaks first and goes to buy a new one.
It's always the same person. It's usually not you.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who steals your charger every single time — caption: "I've bought four now." They will reply: "perfect, mine just broke"
variations: Upgrade: after moving in together we owned seven chargers. The moment we needed one, all seven vanished. We finally found them — under the couch, living with the missing socks.

items.sourceNote: 情侶最後一口食物的禮讓戰爭，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
情侶共食時，最危險的不是搶菜，是「最後一口」。

盤子裡剩下最後一塊雞排，兩個人都看著它，都不夾。

「你吃啦。」
「我吃不下了。」
「你不是說很好吃？」
「對啊但我飽了。」
「那我吃喔？」
「吃啊。」
「真的可以嗎？」
「真的。」
「你不要等下又說我都不留給你？」
「不會啦。」
（你夾起來，咬了一口）
「……其實我也想吃。」
「你剛剛說你飽了！」
「我以為你會留一半給我。」

從此你學會了：所有「你吃啦」的背後，都藏著「你最好分我一半」。
真正的愛情，是在夾起最後一口之前，先用筷子把它切成兩半。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給總是把最後一口讓給你卻又偷偷期待你分她的另一半——附上「下次我直接切兩半」。對方一定回「你終於懂了」
variations: 進階：交往十年後，我們連菜上桌的那一刻就已經默默協議好——我吃這邊，你吃那邊，中間那條線是邊界。最後一口從來不存在，因為它從一開始就被劃分好了。

en.content:
When couples share food, the real danger isn't the fight over dishes — it's the last bite.

One piece of chicken left on the plate. Both of you stare at it. Neither moves.

"You take it."
"I'm full."
"You said it was great."
"It is, I'm just done."
"So I can have it?"
"Go ahead."
"You sure?"
"I'm sure."
"You won't say later that I never leave you anything?"
"I won't."
(You pick it up. You take a bite.)
"...Actually I did want some."
"You said you were FULL."
"I thought you'd leave me half."

That's how you learn: every "you take it" comes with a hidden "you'd better share."
Real love is cutting the last bite in half with your chopsticks before either of you reaches for it.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who always offers you the last bite but secretly hopes you'll split it — caption: "next time I'm cutting it in half." They'll reply: "FINALLY"
variations: Advanced: ten years in, we silently negotiated the moment the food hit the table — I eat this side, you eat that side, the line in the middle is the border. There is no last bite, because it was divided from the start.

items.sourceNote: 情侶超市走散的內部暗號文化，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
情侶逛超市走散的三種找人方式：

第一種：傳訊息「你在哪？」
對方回：「我在這裡。」
等於沒講。

第二種：打電話。
「我在零食區。」
你走到零食區，沒人。
「啊我剛剛走到水果區了。」
你走到水果區，沒人。
「我又回零食區了。」
你們在這間超市玩了二十分鐘鬼抓人。

第三種：交往三年以上的進階版——
你站在原地不動，閉上眼睛。
三十秒後睜開，對方就在你旁邊，手上拿著你最愛的那款餅乾。
沒講話，他就知道你在哪。
這叫做：超市雷達已啟用。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給總是在超市走丟的另一半——附上「下次直接傳定位」，對方一定回「我以為你會自己找到我」
variations: 終極版：兩個人乾脆從推車旁分開行動，講好「結帳區見」。結果一個先到的人會等十五分鐘，另一個還在堅持要找那款限定口味的乳酪。最後在結帳的不是食物，是耐心。

en.content:
Three ways couples find each other after splitting up in a supermarket:

First: text "where are you?"
They reply: "here."
Useless.

Second: phone call.
"I'm in the snack aisle."
You walk to the snack aisle. Empty.
"Oh I just moved to produce."
You walk to produce. Empty.
"I'm back in snacks now."
You play hide and seek for twenty minutes in a grocery store.

Third: the advanced version, three years in —
You stand still. You close your eyes.
Thirty seconds later, you open them. They're next to you, holding the snack you love.
No words. They just knew.
That's called: supermarket radar, activated.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who always gets lost in the store — caption: "next time just share your location." They'll reply: "I thought you'd find me."
variations: Final form: you give up, split at the cart, agree to meet at checkout. One of you waits fifteen minutes while the other hunts down a limited-edition cheese. What gets checked out at the register isn't food — it's patience.

items.sourceNote: 情侶「你應該知道」溝通迷思，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
情侶溝通最大的謊言，叫做：「你應該知道。」

「我心情不好。」
「怎麼了？」
「你應該知道。」
「我真的不知道。」
「你想想看。」
（你開始想——昨天的話？早上的訊息？上週的事？三年前的承諾？）
「我想不到。」
「你看吧，你根本不在乎。」

後來你才明白：交往這麼久，對方期待的不是你猜對答案，是你「願意陪她想」這件事本身。
猜不到沒關係，認真猜了，就贏了一半。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個總是要你猜的另一半——附上「我猜不到但我很願意陪你想」。她不會說，但會偷偷把這句存起來
variations: 反向版：「你應該知道我喜歡什麼禮物。」——後來他真的買了，你卻說「這不是我想要的。」答案永遠是動態的，這就是為什麼讀心術會失業。

en.content:
The biggest lie in couple communication is: "You should know."

"I'm upset."
"What's wrong?"
"You should know."
"I genuinely don't."
"Think about it."
(You start thinking — yesterday's conversation? This morning's text? Last week? A promise from three years ago?)
"I can't figure it out."
"See, you don't care."

Eventually you realize: after all this time, they don't expect you to guess right. They expect you to be willing to guess.
Missing the answer is fine. Trying counts for half the score.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who always makes you guess — caption: "I can't read your mind but I'll keep trying." They won't say anything, but they'll quietly save the message.
variations: Reverse version: "You should know what gift I want." Then you buy it and they say: "This isn't what I wanted." The right answer keeps moving. That's why mind reading went out of business.

items.sourceNote: 情侶冰箱裡誰的食物之爭，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
情侶同居後，冰箱裡會出現一條看不見的線。

左邊是你的優格。右邊是他的啤酒。
中間那層放共用的食物。

「我那盒布丁呢？」
「我吃了。」
「那是我的耶。」
「冰箱裡的不是都可以吃嗎？」
「不是！中間那層才可以！我那盒在左邊！」
「左邊跟中間差在哪？」
「差在那是我的！」

後來你們發明了一套標籤系統。
貼了三天就沒人遵守了。
第七天，他又吃了你的布丁。
第八天，你把他的啤酒喝掉一罐當作報復。
第九天，你們和解，買了兩盒布丁。
第十天，又吵了起來，因為他連你的那盒也吃了。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給總是偷吃你食物的另一半——附上「下次寫名字也沒用」。他一定回「那盒我以為是共用的」
variations: 終極解法：兩個人買兩個小冰箱，各放各的。聽起來合理，但三個月後你會發現——他的冰箱永遠是空的，因為他都在吃你的。

en.content:
After moving in together, an invisible line appears inside the fridge.

Left side: your yogurt. Right side: their beer.
Middle shelf: shared food.

"Where's my pudding?"
"I ate it."
"That was mine."
"Isn't everything in the fridge fair game?"
"No! Only the middle shelf! Mine was on the LEFT!"
"What's the difference?"
"The difference is it was MINE."

You invent a labeling system.
Three days later nobody follows it.
Day seven: they eat your pudding again.
Day eight: you drink one of their beers as revenge.
Day nine: you make up, buy two puddings.
Day ten: another fight, because they ate yours too.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who always eats your food — caption: "writing my name on it won't work either." They'll reply: "I thought that one was shared."
variations: Final solution: buy two mini-fridges, one each. Sounds reasonable. Three months later you'll notice — their fridge is always empty. Because they're still eating yours.

items.sourceNote: 情侶睡前滑手機儀式，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
情侶睡前的標準流程：

十一點：「我們今天早點睡。」
十一點半：兩個人躺在床上，各自滑手機。
十二點：「你看這個影片好好笑。」（傳給對方）
十二點半：對方笑出聲。「欸這個你看過了沒？」（再傳一個）
一點：兩個人明明躺在同一張床上，卻在用 LINE 互傳貓咪影片。
一點半：「真的要睡了。」
兩點：「最後一個我發誓。」
三點：「為什麼我們每天都這樣？」

隔天早上：「昨天怎麼又這麼晚才睡？」
互看一眼。
沒人有答案。
但你們都知道，今晚還會再發生一次。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給每天說好要早睡卻一起滑到三點的另一半——附上「今晚我們真的早睡」。對方一定回「好，最後一個影片我先傳你」
variations: 進階：兩個人約好「十一點手機放客廳」。執行了一週。第八天有人偷拿回房間，從此這個規定就消失了，跟新年的健身計畫一樣。

en.content:
The standard pre-sleep routine for couples:

11:00 PM: "Let's sleep early tonight."
11:30 PM: both in bed, both scrolling their phones.
12:00 AM: "Look at this video, it's hilarious." (sends it over)
12:30 AM: they laugh out loud. "Have you seen this one?" (sends another)
1:00 AM: lying in the same bed, sending each other cat videos via text.
1:30 AM: "Okay, sleeping for real now."
2:00 AM: "Last one, I swear."
3:00 AM: "Why do we do this every night?"

Morning: "Why did we stay up so late again?"
You look at each other.
Neither of you has an answer.
But both of you know it'll happen again tonight.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner you scroll until 3 AM with every night — caption: "tonight we actually sleep early." They'll reply: "okay, but watch this one first."
variations: Advanced: you agree to leave phones in the living room at 11. It lasts a week. On day eight someone sneaks one back into the bedroom, and the rule dies — right alongside the New Year gym plan.

items.sourceNote: 情侶共用棉被的世紀戰爭，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
情侶睡同一張床、蓋同一條棉被——聽起來很浪漫，但只有試過的人知道那叫做：人生最漫長的拔河比賽。

半夜三點，你冷醒。
往身邊一看——棉被全部捲在對方身上，捲成壽司的形狀。
你輕輕拉了一下。沒動。
再拉。對方翻身，把剩下那角也帶走了。

你坐起來，看著躺在棉被裡像蠶寶寶的他，思考了三十秒人生。
最後選擇：起床去客廳拿備用棉被。

隔天早上他問：「你昨天怎麼睡那麼差？」
你說：「沒事。」
但心裡記了一筆。

後來你們學乖了——買兩條棉被。
從此世界和平。婚姻顧問都該推薦這招。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給每晚把棉被捲成壽司的另一半——附上「下週直接買兩條」。對方一定回「我哪有捲走，是你自己踢開的」
variations: 進階版：你以為買兩條就沒事？錯。對方會開始捲他自己那條，然後伸手過來搶你的當補充包。棉被自治的盡頭，是棉被外交。

en.content:
Sharing a bed and one blanket sounds romantic. Only people who've actually tried it know it's the longest tug-of-war of your life.

3 AM. You wake up freezing.
You look over — the entire blanket is wrapped around your partner, in the shape of sushi.
You tug gently. Nothing.
You tug again. They roll over, taking the last corner with them.

You sit up. You stare at the human burrito next to you. You contemplate your life choices for thirty seconds.
Then you get up and grab the spare blanket from the couch.

Next morning: "Why'd you sleep so badly?"
You say: "No reason."
But you make a mental note.

Eventually you learn — buy two blankets.
World peace, achieved. Every marriage counselor should prescribe this.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who turns into a blanket burrito every night — caption: "buying two blankets next week." They'll reply: "I don't steal it, YOU kick it off"
variations: Advanced: think two blankets solves it? Wrong. They'll wrap up in theirs and still reach over to grab yours as a top-up. The end of blanket independence is blanket diplomacy.

items.sourceNote: 情侶冷氣溫度大戰，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
情侶同居後最持久的戰爭，不是錢，不是家事，是——冷氣溫度。

他覺得二十四度剛好。
你覺得二十四度是冰庫。

你偷偷把遙控器調到二十七度。
十分鐘後他經過，「咦怎麼那麼熱？」按回二十四度。
你再偷偷調回二十七度。
他再調回二十四度。

一個晚上，你們在沙發兩端，用同一支遙控器，下了一盤無聲的西洋棋。

後來你買了一條小毯子，他買了一台小電風扇。
問題沒有解決，只是各自找到了存活方式。
這叫做：成熟的伴侶關係。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給跟你搶遙控器的另一半——附上「下次我直接把電池拔掉」。對方一定回「那我就去開窗」
variations: 終極版：兩個人乾脆裝兩台冷氣，一個房間一台。聽起來解決了，但晚上睡覺還是同一張床——你會發現，戰場只是換了地點，沒有消失。

en.content:
The longest war after moving in together isn't money or chores — it's the thermostat.

He thinks 75°F is perfect.
You think 75°F is a freezer.

You sneak the remote and turn it up to 79.
Ten minutes later he walks past: "Why is it so hot?" Sets it back to 75.
You sneak it back to 79.
He sets it back to 75.

One entire evening, on opposite ends of the couch, you played silent chess with one remote.

Eventually you bought a small blanket. He bought a small fan.
The problem wasn't solved. You both just found ways to survive.
This is called: a mature relationship.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who keeps fighting you over the remote — caption: "next time I'm removing the batteries." They'll reply: "then I'll just open the window"
variations: Final form: you give up and install two AC units, one per room. Sounds like a fix. But you still share one bed at night — and you'll realize the battlefield just moved. It never disappeared.

items.sourceNote: 情侶 Netflix 偷看下一集出軌，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
情侶之間最隱密的背叛，叫做：Netflix 偷跑。

你們一起追的劇，講好「明天晚上才看下一集」。
當天晚上，他洗澡的時候，你偷偷打開電視。
「就看五分鐘看看下一集在演什麼。」

五分鐘變成一集。
一集變成兩集。
等他洗完澡走出來，你已經看到第四集，正擦著眼淚。

「你在看什麼？」
「沒有，重看一遍前面。」
「你眼睛紅紅的。」
「我打哈欠。」
「劇情有發展嗎？」
「沒有沒有，跟昨天一樣。」

隔天兩個人坐下來看，你只能在他驚訝的時候，配合表演驚訝。
當他大喊「天啊我沒想到會這樣」的時候，你心裡想：我也沒想到，我前一天就沒想到。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給偷跑進度的另一半——附上「我都看出你在演了」。對方一定回「我以為我演得很好」
variations: 和解版：被抓包之後，兩個人乾脆從頭再看一遍。你坐在那裡，知道每個轉折、每個梗，但你選擇閉嘴，看著他第一次的反應。那一刻你發現——原來愛情就是，把驚喜留給對方，假裝自己也是第一次。

en.content:
The most secret betrayal between couples is called: Netflix cheating.

You agreed to watch the next episode together tomorrow night.
That evening, while he's in the shower, you sneak the TV on.
"Just five minutes. Just to see where it goes."

Five minutes becomes one episode.
One becomes two.
By the time he walks out of the bathroom, you're four episodes in, wiping your eyes.

"What are you watching?"
"Nothing. Rewatching the earlier part."
"Your eyes are red."
"I yawned."
"Anything happen in the plot?"
"No no, exact same as yesterday."

Next day you sit down together. You have to fake-gasp at every twist. When he yells "oh my god I didn't see that coming," you think to yourself: yeah, neither did I — yesterday.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who watches ahead — caption: "I can tell you're acting." They'll reply: "I thought I was pretty convincing"
variations: Resolution: after getting caught, you start the whole series over together. You sit there knowing every twist, every joke, every death. But you stay quiet and watch their first reaction. That's when you realize — love is keeping the surprise for someone else, and pretending it's new for you too.

items.sourceNote: 情侶代買清單被擴張的災難，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
情侶託對方買東西，永遠遵守一條神祕定律：清單上的東西一定買不齊，清單上沒有的東西一定多出來。

清單：「牛奶、麵包、雞蛋。」

他回家後拎了五個袋子。

「牛奶呢？」
「沒有了。」
「麵包呢？」
「我買了類似的吐司。」
「雞蛋呢？」
「忘了。」
「那這五袋是什麼？」
「啊我看到限時特價，就順便買了一些。」

你打開袋子：兩包洋芋片、一盒冰淇淋、一罐辣椒醬、一支拖把、一隻會發光的玩具鴨。

「我們不需要拖把。」
「那個在打折！」
「我們也不需要鴨子。」
「你不覺得很可愛嗎？」

隔天你親自去買牛奶、麵包、雞蛋。
冰箱旁邊的鴨子，亮著燈看著你。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給每次代買都買錯的另一半——附上「下次我自己去」。對方一定回「但你會錯過特價」
variations: 進階：清單上寫「番茄醬」，他買回來「番茄」跟「醬油」。他說：「合起來就是番茄醬啊。」你發現愛情會讓人變笨，而且是一起變笨。

en.content:
When couples ask each other to grocery shop, one mysterious law always applies: nothing on the list comes home, and everything not on the list does.

List: "Milk, bread, eggs."

He comes home with five bags.

"Where's the milk?"
"Out of stock."
"Bread?"
"I got a similar loaf."
"Eggs?"
"Forgot."
"What's in the five bags then?"
"Saw some sales, picked up a few things."

You open the bags: two bags of chips, one tub of ice cream, a bottle of hot sauce, a mop, and a glowing rubber duck.

"We don't need a mop."
"It was on sale!"
"We don't need a duck either."
"Isn't it cute though?"

Next day you go yourself. Milk. Bread. Eggs.
The duck sits next to the fridge, glowing, watching you.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who always brings home the wrong groceries — caption: "next time I'm going myself." They'll reply: "but you'll miss the deals"
variations: Advanced: the list said 'ketchup.' He brought back tomatoes and soy sauce. He said: 'put them together, same thing.' That's when you realized — love makes you dumber, and it makes you both dumber together.

items.sourceNote: 情侶「我沒生氣」三大字真假測謊，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
情侶關係裡，最危險的三個字不是「我愛你」，是——「我沒事」。

「你怎麼了？」
「沒事。」
「真的沒事？」
「真的。」
「那你看起來不太開心。」
「我說沒事就沒事。」

你閉嘴。十分鐘後她說：「你竟然真的就不問了。」

你打開求生指南，發現「沒事」分成三個等級：

第一級：語氣平穩——真的沒事，你可以繼續滑手機。
第二級：聲音降半度——有事，但她想看你會不會主動發現。
第三級：尾音上揚加一個句點——已經爆炸了，你只是還沒聽到聲音。

後來你學會了一招：什麼都不問，但默默泡一杯熱茶放在她旁邊。
她不一定會喝，但她會記得。
這比任何問句都有效。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給說「沒事」其實有事的另一半——附上「我都聽得出哪個等級」。對方一定回「那你怎麼還不過來」
variations: 反向版：男生說「沒事」的時候，真的就是沒事。他不是在演，他是真的什麼都沒在想。這就是為什麼她問十次，他答十次「沒事」，最後她崩潰了——不是因為他在隱瞞，是因為她不能相信有人腦子可以這麼空。

en.content:
The most dangerous three words in a relationship aren't "I love you." They're: "I'm fine."

"What's wrong?"
"Nothing."
"You sure?"
"Sure."
"You look upset."
"I said I'm fine."

You shut up. Ten minutes later: "I can't believe you actually stopped asking."

You open the survival manual. Turns out "I'm fine" has three levels:

Level 1: steady tone. Actually fine. Resume scrolling.
Level 2: voice drops half a step. Not fine. She wants to see if you'll figure it out.
Level 3: rising tone with a hard period at the end. Already exploded. You just haven't heard the boom yet.

Eventually you learn the move: don't ask anything. Just quietly make a cup of hot tea and set it next to her.
She might not drink it. But she'll remember.
More effective than any question.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner whose "I'm fine" is never fine — caption: "I can tell which level it is now." They'll reply: "so why aren't you over here yet"
variations: Reverse version: when a guy says "nothing," he actually means nothing. He's not hiding. His head is genuinely empty. That's why after asking ten times and getting ten "nothings," she finally loses it — not because he's lying, but because she can't accept a brain can be that quiet.

items.sourceNote: 情侶同一張沙發傳訊息，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
情侶交往滿一年後，最神奇的現象是：明明坐在同一張沙發上，距離不到三十公分，還是用 LINE 聊天。

「冰箱還有可樂嗎？」
（坐在你旁邊的人傳的）

「你不會自己看？」
（你回的，懶得開口）

「我手機在身上，腳沒在身上。」

「合理。」

後來你起身去冰箱拿，順便幫他帶一罐。
他沒說謝謝，只傳了一個讚的貼圖。
你也沒回，只傳了一個OK的貼圖。

隔壁的單身朋友看著這一切，覺得不可思議。
你只是覺得：這樣很省力。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個明明在你旁邊還傳訊息給你的另一半——附上「下次直接說話會少十秒嗎」。對方一定回一個貼圖
variations: 進階版：兩個人並排躺床上，一個發限動，另一個按愛心。沒有對話，但這就是溝通。在一起久了，連已讀都是一種陪伴。

en.content:
After a year together, the strangest thing happens: you're sitting on the same couch, less than a foot apart, texting each other.

"Any Coke left in the fridge?"
(Sent by the person literally next to you)

"Can't you check yourself?"
(You reply, too lazy to speak)

"My phone's on me. My legs aren't."

"Fair."

You get up. Grab one for him too.
He doesn't say thanks — just sends a thumbs-up sticker.
You don't reply — just send an OK sticker.

The single friend watching this is baffled.
You just think: this is efficient.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who texts you from two feet away — caption: "would speaking save us ten seconds?" They'll reply with a sticker
variations: Advanced version: lying side by side in bed, one posts a story, the other likes it. No conversation. But that's communication too. Long enough together and even a 'seen' counts as company.

items.sourceNote: 情侶搶遙控器戰爭，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
情侶交往的真實考驗，從來不是吵架，是——晚上九點，沙發上，誰拿遙控器。

「我想看那個推理劇。」
「我想看綜藝。」
「上次你也看你的。」
「上次你看了三集你的。」
「那就猜拳。」
「你每次都贏。」
「那不然怎麼辦。」
「……我們一起看 YouTube。」

結果兩個人滑了四十分鐘短影片，誰也沒看到自己想看的。
但很奇怪，兩個人都覺得：「今天晚上很爽。」

後來你發現一個真相：
情侶最深的默契，不是看一樣的東西，是一起浪費時間也不覺得可惜。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個老是搶遙控器的另一半——附上「下次直接讓我吧」。對方一定回「你才要讓我」
variations: 終極版：兩個人最後決定一人看一隻耳機。她聽她的劇，他看他的賽事。沒有聲音，只有兩個人並排坐著，偶爾一笑，偶爾一聲「欸這個你看」。這也叫一起看電視。

en.content:
The real test of a relationship isn't arguments. It's: 9pm, on the couch, who gets the remote.

"I want to watch the mystery show."
"I want the variety show."
"You got your pick last time."
"You got three episodes of yours last time."
"Rock paper scissors."
"You always win."
"Then what?"
"...Let's just scroll YouTube together."

Forty minutes of short videos later, neither has watched what they wanted.
But somehow, both think: "great night."

Later you realize the truth:
The deepest couple sync isn't watching the same thing — it's wasting time together without feeling like it was wasted.
en.editorNote: Send to the remote-hogging partner — caption: "next time you let me pick." They'll reply: "no you let me"
variations: Ultimate version: you end up sharing one earbud each. Her show on her side, his game on his. No shared audio, just two people on a couch, occasionally laughing, occasionally saying "hey look at this." That counts as watching TV together too.

items.sourceNote: 情侶搶充電線戰爭，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
同居情侶最常吵架的事，不是錢，不是家事，是——「我的充電線呢？」

「我充電線又不見了。」
「我沒拿。」
「家裡只有我們兩個。」
「那可能是貓。」
「我們沒養貓。」
「……那可能是風。」

你打開抽屜，發現你買的第七條充電線正在床的另一邊，纏在另一半的耳機線上，像兩條相愛多年的小蛇。

「你看，就是你拿的。」
「我不記得了。」
「那這條跟你耳機線纏在一起？」
「它們自由戀愛。」

後來你直接買兩條，一人一條，貼標籤。
結果三天後，標籤被撕掉，兩條線又纏在一起，分不出誰是誰的。
你決定不再追究——這就是同居。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個老是借走你充電線不還的另一半——附上「下次我藏起來」。對方一定回「藏哪你說一下」
variations: 反向版：你買了無線充電盤，以為從此世界和平。結果發現只有一個，兩個人開始搶充電盤。問題不在線，在人。

en.content:
The number one fight for cohabiting couples isn't money or chores. It's: "where's my charger?"

"My charger's missing again."
"Didn't take it."
"It's just us in this apartment."
"Maybe the cat."
"We don't have a cat."
"...Then the wind."

You open the drawer. The seventh charger you've bought is on the other side of the bed, tangled with their earbuds, like two snakes in a long-term relationship.

"See, you took it."
"I don't remember."
"Then why is it tangled with your earbuds?"
"They fell in love on their own."

Eventually you buy two. Label them. One each.
Three days later, the labels are gone. The cables are tangled. Nobody knows whose is whose.
You stop investigating. This is cohabitation.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who keeps stealing your charger — caption: "next time I'm hiding it." They'll reply: "hiding it where, tell me"
variations: Reverse version: you buy a wireless pad, thinking peace has arrived. Then you realize there's only one. Now you fight over the pad. The problem was never the cable. It was you two.

items.sourceNote: 情侶最後一口食物之爭，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
情侶共食一份食物，最後一口的歸屬，是一場無聲的心理戰。

第一階段（剛交往）：
「你吃。」
「不，你吃。」
「真的，你吃。」
「不行，你吃。」
——最後雞排放到涼掉。

第二階段（交往一年）：
「最後一口你要嗎？」
「你呢？」
「我都可以。」
——對切，一人一半。

第三階段（交往三年）：
你看著她。她沒看你。
你說：「我吃了喔。」
她說：「嗯。」
——你吃了。沒有任何感情糾葛。

第四階段（交往五年）：
你伸手要吃，發現已經被吃了。
你問：「最後一口呢？」
她說：「我以為你不要。」
你也沒生氣，因為下次她點的鬆餅，你也會這樣對她。

這就是公平。也是愛情。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給共食很久的另一半——附上「下次最後一口我要」。對方一定回「下次再說」然後下次又吃掉
variations: 終極版：你們學會了一招——點兩份一樣的。從此世界和平。但你很快發現，少了搶最後一口的緊張感，這頓飯莫名其妙地少了一點什麼。原來爭最後一口不是為了吃，是為了確認對方還在乎。

en.content:
When a couple shares food, the last bite is a silent psychological war.

Phase 1 (just dating):
"You have it."
"No, you."
"Really, you."
"No way, you."
— The fried chicken goes cold.

Phase 2 (one year in):
"You want the last piece?"
"Do you?"
"Either way."
— Split in half. One each.

Phase 3 (three years in):
You look at her. She doesn't look at you.
You say: "I'm taking it."
She says: "Mm."
— You take it. No drama.

Phase 4 (five years in):
You reach for it. It's already gone.
You ask: "Where's the last bite?"
She says: "I thought you didn't want it."
You're not even mad. Next time she orders waffles, you'll do the same to her.

That's fairness. That's love.
en.editorNote: Send to your long-term food-sharing partner — caption: "next time the last bite is mine." They'll reply: "we'll see" — then eat it again
variations: Ultimate version: you finally figure it out — order two of the same thing. World peace achieved. But you quickly notice: without the tension of fighting for the last bite, the meal feels slightly emptier. Turns out fighting for the last bite was never about food. It was about confirming the other person still cared.

items.sourceNote: 情侶網購包裹話題，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
「你又買東西？」
「沒有啊。」
（門口三個包裹）
「那這些是？」
「喔那個……是之前下的單，現在才到。」
「上禮拜你也是這樣說。」
「因為上禮拜的也是更之前下的啊。」

——這套說法已經連續沿用八個月。
包裹永遠來自一個遙遠的、誰也查不到的「之前」。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個購物車永遠清不完的另一半，看他敢不敢說「我才沒有」
variations: 進階版：「這個我用得到。」三個月後那樣東西原封不動躺在櫃子最深處，連標籤都還在。

en.content:
"Did you buy stuff again?"
"Nope."
(Three packages at the door.)
"Then what are these?"
"Oh those… I ordered them a while ago, they're just arriving now."
"You said that last week too."
"Because last week's were from even earlier."

— This explanation has been in continuous use for eight months.
The packages always come from a distant, untraceable 'a while ago.'
en.editorNote: Send to the partner whose cart is never empty and watch them try to say 'I did not'
variations: Advanced version: 'I'll totally use this.' Three months later it's still in the back of the closet, tag attached, untouched.

items.sourceNote: 情侶冷氣溫度爭奪戰，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
我們家的冷氣遙控器有兩種狀態：

我設的：二十四度，剛剛好。
他/她設的：二十七度，他/她說剛剛好。

半夜醒來，溫度總是莫名其妙變成二十七度。
我調回二十四度。
再醒來，又是二十七度。

我們從來沒有當面討論過這件事。
這是一場在黑暗中、無聲進行、持續三年的戰爭。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個總是偷偷調溫度的另一半，附註「我知道是你」
variations: 和平協議版：最後買了兩條不同厚度的被子，各蓋各的，世界終於和平了。
升級版：冬天換成搶電熱毯的遙控器，劇情一模一樣，只是攻守互換。

en.content:
Our AC remote has two states:

When I set it: 75°F, perfect.
When they set it: 80°F, which they call perfect.

I wake up at 3 a.m. and somehow it's back to 80.
I set it to 75.
Wake up again — 80.

We have never once discussed this out loud.
It's a silent war, fought in the dark, ongoing for three years.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who keeps secretly changing the temperature, with the caption 'I know it's you'
variations: Peace treaty version: we bought two blankets of different thickness, each our own. World peace, achieved.
Winter edition: same plot, but now it's the electric blanket remote, and the roles are reversed.

items.sourceNote: 情侶暱稱演變史，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
情侶之間怎麼叫對方，可以看出交往幾年：

第一個月：「寶貝」「親愛的」，每句話都黏黏的。
第一年：「欸」。
第三年：直接喊全名，連姓都帶上。
第五年：「那個誰，幫我拿一下遙控器。」

稱呼越來越短，但人還在旁邊。
其實這才是最穩的版本。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給交往多年、現在互相只剩一個「欸」字的另一半
variations: 回歸版：吵架冷戰時反而會突然叫回「親愛的」，因為要裝沒事，這時候肉麻就是白旗。

en.content:
You can tell how long a couple's been together by what they call each other:

Month one: "babe," "sweetheart" — every sentence drips.
Year one: "hey."
Year three: full name, including the last name.
Year five: "you — grab the remote for me."

The names get shorter, but the person's still right there.
Honestly, that's the most stable version.
en.editorNote: Send to the long-term partner you now address exclusively as 'hey'
variations: Throwback version: mid-argument, someone suddenly busts out 'sweetheart' again — because pretending nothing's wrong means the cheesy name becomes the white flag.

items.sourceNote: 情侶共用一個外送帳號決策，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
晚上九點，兩個人盯著外送 App 半小時。

「這家？」「上次吃過。」
「那這家？」「評價只有三顆星。」
「這家四點五。」「但要等六十分鐘。」
「不然算了，自己煮？」
「……冰箱只有一顆蛋。」

最後關掉 App，泡了兩碗泡麵。
配料是：彼此的失望，以及一種奇異的滿足。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個每次點外送都要研究半小時最後吃泡麵的另一半
variations: 結局二：最後還是點了第一家「上次吃過」的那家。繞了一大圈，宇宙早就決定好了。

en.content:
9 p.m. Two people staring at the delivery app for half an hour.

"This place?" "We had it last time."
"That one?" "Only three stars."
"This one's 4.5." "But it's a 60-minute wait."
"Forget it, should we just cook?"
"…There's one egg in the fridge."

We closed the app and made two cups of instant noodles.
Garnished with: mutual disappointment, and a strange contentment.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who researches delivery for 30 minutes and then eats instant noodles
variations: Ending two: we ordered from the first place — the 'we had it last time' one. Big loop, and the universe had decided all along.

items.sourceNote: 情侶共用密碼依賴，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
交往久了，最危險的不是知道對方的密碼，
是兩個人的密碼開始互相滲透：

我的手機密碼裡有他的生日。
他的提款卡密碼裡有我們的紀念日。
串流平台帳號是我的，密碼是他設的。
外送的會員是他的，但都是我在用。

如果哪天分開，第一個崩潰的不是心，
是所有需要密碼才能登入的東西。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個帳號密碼早就跟你混在一起、分都分不開的另一半
variations: 現實版：吵架吵到一半，有一方說「我先去把 Netflix 密碼改掉」，另一方立刻軟化——有些威脅比道歉更有效。

en.content:
The longer you're together, the real danger isn't knowing each other's passwords —
it's that the passwords start bleeding into each other:

My phone PIN has their birthday in it.
Their bank PIN has our anniversary.
The streaming account is mine, but they set the password.
The delivery membership is theirs, but I'm the only one using it.

If we ever split, the first thing to collapse isn't the heart —
it's everything that requires a login.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner whose logins are now hopelessly tangled with yours
variations: Real-life version: mid-argument, one person says 'I'm changing the Netflix password' and the other instantly softens — some threats work better than apologies.

items.sourceNote: 長期關係妥協笑話，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
別人問我們：「在一起這麼久，吵架都怎麼解決的？」

我說：我們很懂妥協。
每次吵架，我負責承認自己錯了，
他負責同意我真的錯了。

一個願意低頭，一個願意接受，
這就是關係長久的祕訣。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個吵架永遠不會輸、但你還是離不開的另一半，附註「說的就是我們」
variations: 進階版：吵到最後其實兩個人都忘記在吵什麼，只記得「反正是你的錯」這個結論。結論先有，原因後補。

en.content:
People ask us: "You've been together so long — how do you settle fights?"

I tell them: we're great at compromise.
Every argument, I admit I was wrong,
and he agrees that I really was.

One of us bows down, the other graciously accepts.
That's the secret to lasting love.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who never loses an argument but somehow you still can't leave, captioned 'this is literally us'
variations: Advanced version: by the end neither of us remembers what the fight was about — only the conclusion that 'it was your fault anyway.' Conclusion first, reasons filled in later.

items.sourceNote: 情侶打呼半夜被吵醒，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
凌晨三點，他打呼像一台發不動的機車。
我用手肘輕輕推他，
他翻了個身，停了五秒，
然後用更大的音量重新啟動。

我躺在黑暗裡認真思考：
當初到底是喜歡他哪一點？

想了想，還是把棉被往他那邊蓋了過去。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個半夜打呼吵到你、但你還是會幫他蓋被子的另一半
variations: 現實版：最後我戴上耳塞，他打他的，我睡我的，這叫做各退一步、互不打擾的成熟愛情。

en.content:
3 a.m. His snoring sounds like a motorcycle that won't start.
I nudge him gently with my elbow.
He rolls over, goes quiet for five seconds,
then reboots at an even higher volume.

Lying there in the dark, I genuinely wonder:
what did I see in him in the first place?

Then I think about it, and pull the blanket back over his side.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner whose snoring keeps you up but you still tuck them in
variations: Real-life version: I put in earplugs, he snores his snore, I sleep my sleep — mature love is two people each taking a step back and leaving each other alone.

items.sourceNote: 情侶搶最後一口食物，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
一盤鹽酥雞，最後剩下一塊。

「你吃。」「不行，你吃。」
「我真的吃不下了。」「我也是。」

那塊鹽酥雞就這樣孤單地躺在盤子裡，
放到涼掉，放到變硬，
見證了兩個人最後的禮讓與克制。

半小時後，有一方默默把它吃了，
另一方說：「我就知道你還想吃。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個跟你互讓最後一口、最後還是被其中一人吃掉的另一半
variations: 另一種結局：兩個人都不吃，最後它進了垃圾桶。為了風度，犧牲了一塊鹽酥雞，值得嗎？深夜想起來還是會痛。

en.content:
A plate of fried chicken, one piece left.

"You have it." "No, you have it."
"I really can't eat another bite." "Me neither."

So that last piece lies alone on the plate,
going cold, going stiff,
a monument to two people's mutual restraint.

Half an hour later, one of us quietly eats it,
and the other says: "I knew you still wanted it."
en.editorNote: Send to the partner you keep offering the last bite to, until one of you finally caves
variations: Alternate ending: neither of us eats it, and it ends up in the trash. We sacrificed a perfectly good piece of chicken for the sake of manners. Was it worth it? Still hurts at midnight.

items.sourceNote: 情侶交往太久變家人感笑話，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
交往第一年：他傳訊息問「在幹嘛？想你了。」

交往第五年：他傳訊息問「衛生紙還有嗎？回家順便買。」

以前的訊息是情書，
現在的訊息是採購清單。

但說真的，
會記得家裡衛生紙快沒了，
好像也是一種浪漫。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個訊息內容從情話變成代辦清單、但你一點也不介意的另一半
variations: 升級版：交往第八年，訊息只剩一個已讀，因為兩個人正坐在同一個沙發上，根本不用傳。

en.content:
Year one: he texts "What are you up to? Miss you."

Year five: he texts "Are we out of toilet paper? Grab some on your way home."

The old messages were love letters.
The new ones are shopping lists.

But honestly,
remembering we're almost out of toilet paper
is kind of romantic too.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner whose texts went from sweet nothings to errand reminders — and you don't mind one bit
variations: Upgrade: year eight, the text is just a 'read' receipt with no reply — because you're both sitting on the same couch and don't need to text at all.

items.sourceNote: 情侶導航吵架梗，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
情侶開車最危險的時刻，不是高速公路，是導航說「五百公尺後右轉」的那一刻。

副駕：「欸右轉。」
駕駛：「我知道。」
副駕：「就是這個路口。」
駕駛：「我知道。」
副駕：「你過頭了。」
駕駛：「……我知道。」

導航重新規劃路線只要三秒。
車內的氣氛重新規劃，要三十分鐘。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給每次開車都要被另一半「人工導航」的朋友，或是那個總是忍不住多嘴的副駕本人
variations: 進階版：兩個人都不會看地圖，最後是導航小姐用最溫柔的語氣說了第七次「請迴轉」，才終於沒有人再說話。

en.content:
The most dangerous moment in a couple's car ride isn't the highway. It's the second the GPS says "turn right in five hundred meters."

Passenger: "Turn right."
Driver: "I know."
Passenger: "This is the turn."
Driver: "I know."
Passenger: "You missed it."
Driver: "...I know."

The GPS recalculates in three seconds.
The mood in the car takes thirty minutes.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who gets 'manually navigated' every time they drive — or to the backseat driver who just can't help themselves
variations: Advanced version: neither of you can read a map, and peace only returns after the GPS lady says 'please make a U-turn' for the seventh time in her gentlest voice.

items.sourceNote: 情侶分帳梗，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
情侶之間的金錢觀，會隨交往時間進化。

第一次約會：搶著付，搶到差點翻臉。
交往半年：「這餐我請，下餐你請。」記得清清楚楚。
交往兩年：「誰手機在桌上誰付。」
交往五年：「反正都是同一個戶頭，誰付有差嗎。」

從你的、我的，到我們的。
帳算到最後，算不清了，也就不用算了。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給已經分不清錢包是誰的那對情侶，或是還在認真記帳的新手情侶——兩種都會心一笑
variations: 反向版：交往五年，搶著付的理由變成「這家我有會員點數」——浪漫退場，精打細算上場，但那也是另一種一起過日子。

en.content:
A couple's relationship with money evolves over time.

First date: both fight to pay, nearly fall out over it.
Six months in: "I've got this one, you get the next." Tracked precisely.
Two years in: "Whoever's phone is on the table pays."
Five years in: "It's the same account anyway. Does it matter?"

From yours and mine, to ours.
Eventually the math gets too tangled to do — so you just stop doing it.
en.editorNote: Send to the couple who can no longer tell whose wallet is whose — or the new couple still splitting every receipt. Both will smile.
variations: Reverse version: five years in, the reason to grab the check becomes 'I have points at this place' — romance exits, frugality enters, and that's its own kind of building a life together.

items.sourceNote: 情侶分享食物梗，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
「我不餓，你吃就好。」

這句話，是情侶之間最大的謊言。

翻譯：「我等你點完，然後吃你的。」

所以聰明的人都會多點一份。
不是因為對方真的會吃，
是因為對方一定會吃，
而且還會說：「欸這個比我的好吃。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個每次都說不餓、最後吃掉你半盤的另一半，附註「我都知道」
variations: 終極版：你多點了一份防著，結果對方還是吃你那份，理由是「多點的那份是要一起分的啊」。邏輯無懈可擊。

en.content:
"I'm not hungry, you go ahead and eat."

This is the single biggest lie between any couple.

Translation: "I'll wait for you to order, then eat yours."

So the wise ones order an extra portion.
Not because their partner will actually eat it —
but because their partner will absolutely eat it,
and then say, "Hey, yours tastes better than mine."
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who always claims they're not hungry, then eats half your plate. Caption it 'I'm onto you.'
variations: Ultimate version: you order an extra portion as defense, and they eat yours anyway — reasoning, 'the extra one was meant to share.' Airtight logic.

items.sourceNote: 情侶搶被子梗，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
情侶同床睡的真相：

床有兩公尺寬。
你只佔了三十公分。
對方橫躺成一個大字，把你擠到床邊，一隻腳還壓在你身上。

你深吸一口氣，準備把人推回去——
結果對方睡夢中嘟囔了一句：「不要走。」

然後你就，繼續睡在那三十公分。
一句話的事，連被子都不敢搶了。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個睡相豪邁、卻又在夢裡黏人的另一半，看他清醒時死不承認
variations: 升級版：你乾脆買了一張更大的床，結果對方的睡眠範圍也等比例放大，你還是只有三十公分。問題從來不在床的大小。

en.content:
The truth about a couple sharing a bed:

The bed is two meters wide.
You occupy thirty centimeters of it.
They're sprawled diagonally like a starfish, pinning you to the edge, one leg flung across you.

You take a deep breath, ready to shove them back —
then they mumble in their sleep: "Don't go."

And you just... stay in your thirty centimeters.
Two words, and you don't even dare reclaim the blanket.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who sleeps like a starfish but turns clingy in their dreams — and flatly denies it when awake
variations: Upgrade: you buy a bigger bed, and their sleeping radius expands proportionally. You still get thirty centimeters. The problem was never the bed size.

items.sourceNote: 情侶已讀梗，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
感情裡最恐怖的三個字，不是「分手吧」。

是「已讀」。

後面沒有任何字。

你開始回想自己上一句說了什麼，
是不是哪裡惹到對方，
要不要再傳一句，
還是再等一下，
等到你把整段對話來回讀了五遍——

對方傳來：「抱歉我剛在洗澡。」

你瞬間原地復活，剛剛那十分鐘像是沒發生過。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個一被已讀就開始腦補小劇場的朋友，或是那個總是洗很久澡的另一半
variations: 進階版：你決定不要先傳，要等對方先開口——結果對方也在等你，於是兩個人各自盯著手機，把同一段對話讀到天荒地老。

en.content:
The scariest words in a relationship aren't "we need to talk."

It's "Read."

With nothing after it.

You start replaying your last message,
wondering if you said something wrong,
debating whether to send another,
or wait a little longer,
until you've reread the whole thread five times —

Then they text: "Sorry, was in the shower."

You resurrect on the spot, and those ten minutes never happened.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend whose brain writes a whole drama the second they get left on read — or the partner who takes suspiciously long showers
variations: Advanced version: you decide not to text first and wait for them to break the silence — except they're waiting on you too, so you both just stare at your phones rereading the same thread into eternity.

items.sourceNote: 情侶搶遙控器梗，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
情侶之間最大的權力鬥爭，不是誰洗碗，也不是誰倒垃圾。

是遙控器。

誰拿著遙控器，誰就掌握了今晚兩個人的命運。

你以為你贏了，因為遙控器在你手上——
結果對方說：「你選啊，我都可以。」
你選了一部，他說：「這部我看過了。」
你又選一部，他說：「這部好像有點悶。」

最後你把遙控器放回他手上：「還是你選吧。」

他笑了。
他從來沒想要遙控器。
他要的是你心甘情願地把它交出來。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個嘴上說「你選就好」卻把每個選項都否決掉的另一半，看他怎麼狡辯
variations: 升級版：你們裝了串流平台，選擇變多了，於是你們花在「選要看什麼」的時間，正式超過了「真正在看」的時間。

en.content:
The biggest power struggle in a relationship isn't who does the dishes or who takes out the trash.

It's the remote.

Whoever holds the remote holds both people's fate for the night.

You think you've won because the remote is in your hand —
then they say, "You pick, I'm fine with anything."
You pick something. "Seen it."
You pick again. "Eh, kind of slow."

Finally you hand the remote back. "You pick."

They smile.
They never wanted the remote.
They wanted you to surrender it willingly.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who says 'you choose' and then vetoes every single option — watch them try to explain themselves
variations: Upgrade: you add more streaming services, the options multiply, and the time you spend 'choosing what to watch' officially surpasses the time you spend actually watching.

items.sourceNote: 情侶共用冰箱信任梗，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
共用一個冰箱，是情侶之間最大的信任測試。

你把昨天沒吃完的半塊蛋糕，特地放在最顯眼的位置，心裡盤算著明天當早餐。

隔天打開冰箱——
蛋糕還在。
但只剩四分之一。

你看著對方。
對方一臉無辜：「我以為那是要分享的。」

冰箱裡沒有「你的」和「我的」。
只有「還沒被對方發現的」和「已經沒了的」。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個總是默默吃掉你最後一口珍藏的另一半，附上一張空盤子的照片更有殺傷力
variations: 進階版：你學乖了，把想留的東西藏在蔬菜抽屜最底層——一個對方這輩子都不會主動打開的地方。這招有效率達百分之百。

en.content:
Sharing one fridge is the ultimate trust exercise in a relationship.

You place the leftover half of yesterday's cake right at eye level, mentally reserving it for tomorrow's breakfast.

Next morning you open the fridge —
the cake is still there.
But only a quarter of it.

You look at your partner.
They look back, innocent: "I thought that was for sharing."

There's no 'yours' and 'mine' in a shared fridge.
There's only 'not discovered yet' and 'already gone.'
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who quietly finishes the last bite of whatever you were saving — a photo of the empty plate adds extra damage
variations: Advanced version: you wise up and hide your treasures at the very bottom of the vegetable drawer — a place they will never voluntarily open in their entire life. Hundred percent success rate.

items.sourceNote: 情侶冷氣溫度之爭，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
情侶住在一起後才發現，兩個人的體感溫度，活在兩個不同的星球。

一個人覺得二十四度剛剛好。
另一個人裹著棉被，發抖著說：「你是不是想把我冰起來？」

你把冷氣調高兩度。
換你開始流汗。

最後的解決方案是：一個人吹冷氣，一個人蓋棉被，兩個人同框，各自過著不同的季節。

愛情不能改變一個人的體質。
只能讓你買第二條棉被。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個夏天還要蓋棉被、或是冬天還要開電扇的另一半，這就是你們家的日常溫差
variations: 升級版：你們終於買了雙控的電熱毯，左邊一個溫度，右邊一個溫度。科技解決了三年來的戰爭，你們都後悔沒有早點買。

en.content:
Only after moving in together do couples discover that their two body thermostats live on two different planets.

One thinks seventy-five degrees is perfect.
The other, wrapped in a blanket, shivers: "Are you trying to freeze me?"

You bump the AC up two degrees.
Now you're the one sweating.

The final solution: one person under the AC, one person under the blanket, sharing the same frame while living in different seasons.

Love can't change a person's body temperature.
It can only make you buy a second blanket.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who needs a blanket in summer or a fan in winter — this is the daily temperature gap in your home
variations: Upgrade: you finally buy a dual-zone heated blanket, one temperature on the left, another on the right. Technology ends a three-year war, and you both regret not buying it sooner.

items.sourceNote: 情侶猜心遊戲梗，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
情侶之間有一個從來沒有贏家的遊戲，叫做「猜我在想什麼」。

「你猜我為什麼不開心？」
「因為今天太累了？」
「不是。」
「因為我忘記回你訊息？」
「不是。」
「那是因為……」
「算了，你不懂。」

問題是——對方自己也不一定知道為什麼不開心。
他只是需要你陪他一起把答案找出來，
找不到也沒關係，
重點是你有沒有陪他一起找。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個常常要你猜心思的另一半，或是那個總是猜不中卻很努力在猜的你
variations: 進階版：你終於猜中了一次，對方愣了一下說：「對，就是這個。」然後你默默把這次的正確答案記在心裡——直到下次發現，同樣的情況，答案已經換了。

en.content:
There's a game couples play that has never had a winner. It's called "guess what I'm thinking."

"Guess why I'm upset."
"Because you're tired today?"
"No."
"Because I forgot to text back?"
"No."
"Then it's because—"
"Forget it, you don't get it."

The thing is — they don't always know why they're upset either.
They just need you to help them find the answer.
Not finding it is okay.
What matters is whether you searched for it together.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who always makes you guess — or to the you who never guesses right but keeps trying anyway
variations: Advanced version: you finally guess right once. They pause and say, 'Yes, that's it.' You quietly file the correct answer away — until next time, when the same situation has a completely different answer.

items.sourceNote: 情侶一起下廚梗，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
「我們一起煮飯吧，很浪漫。」

這句話，是所有情侶廚房災難的開頭。

一個人說「鹽要再多一點」，另一個說「這樣會太鹹」。
一個人說「火開大一點」，另一個說「會燒焦」。
十分鐘後，煙霧偵測器加入了你們的合作。

最後兩個人站在一片狼藉的廚房裡，看著那盤不知道算成功還是失敗的料理，
相視而笑，默默打開了外送App。

一起煮飯不一定能煮出一頓飯。
但一定能煮出一個回憶。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個跟你一起把廚房搞得像戰場、最後還是叫外送的另一半，附上那盤黑暗料理的照片
variations: 升級版：你們達成共識——一個人負責煮，一個人負責讚美。從此廚房和平了，飯也終於能準時上桌了。

en.content:
"Let's cook together, it'll be romantic."

That sentence is the opening line of every couple's kitchen disaster.

One says "add more salt," the other says "that's too salty."
One says "turn up the heat," the other says "it'll burn."
Ten minutes later, the smoke alarm joins your collaboration.

In the end you both stand in the wreckage of a kitchen, staring at a dish that may or may not count as a success,
exchange a look, smile, and quietly open the delivery app.

Cooking together doesn't always produce a meal.
But it always produces a memory.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who turns the kitchen into a battlefield with you and then orders takeout anyway — attach a photo of the cursed dish
variations: Upgrade: you reach an agreement — one person cooks, one person compliments. Peace returns to the kitchen, and dinner finally makes it to the table on time.

items.sourceNote: 已讀不回的恐慌梗，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
一段感情的組成：
50% 是愛，
49% 是「早安」「晚安」「吃飽沒」，
剩下 1%，是看到對方已讀你訊息卻沒回的那三分鐘，
你在腦中演完了一整部分手連續劇。

結果對方只是去上廁所。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個一已讀不回你就開始腦補小劇場的另一半，或傳給那個害你腦補的人
variations: 進階版：你已經想好誰要搬出去、貓判給誰、過年要怎麼跟雙方家長交代，這時手機跳出來一句「剛剛在洗澡，幹嘛？」

en.content:
What a relationship is made of:
50% love,
49% 'good morning,' 'good night,' 'did you eat,'
and the last 1% is those three minutes after they read your text but don't reply,
while your brain produces an entire breakup miniseries.

Turns out they just went to the bathroom.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who triggers your overthinking spiral every time they leave you on read — or to the one who causes it
variations: Advanced version: you've already decided who moves out, who keeps the cat, and how to explain it to both families at the holidays — then the phone buzzes: 'sorry was in the shower, what's up?'

items.sourceNote: 冷氣溫度之爭，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
情侶睡覺最大的政治問題，不是誰先睡，
而是冷氣要開幾度。

一個人覺得26度是夏天，一個人覺得26度是北極。
一個人裹著棉被像個粽子，一個人踢開棉被像條烤魚。

最後你們發明了一套外交協議：
冷氣開24度，但其中一個人多蓋一條被，另一個人多開一台電風扇。
兩個人都不舒服，但至少公平。

這就是愛——一起忍受同一個溫度。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個跟你搶冷氣遙控器、半夜偷調溫度的另一半
variations: 升級版：你們最後買了一條可以分區控溫的電熱毯，從此各睡各的溫度，世界和平，感情升溫（字面上的）。

en.content:
The biggest political issue when a couple sleeps isn't who dozes off first.
It's what to set the thermostat to.

One thinks 78°F is summer. The other thinks 78°F is the Arctic.
One wraps up in the blanket like a burrito. The other kicks it off like a grilled fish.

Eventually you negotiate a treaty:
thermostat at 73, but one of you gets an extra blanket and the other gets an extra fan.
Neither of you is comfortable, but at least it's fair.

That's love — suffering the same temperature together.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who fights you for the remote and secretly adjusts the temperature at 3 a.m.
variations: Upgrade: you finally buy a dual-zone heated blanket, each side sleeps at its own temperature, world peace achieved, and the relationship warms up (literally).

items.sourceNote: 搶棉被梗，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
我女朋友說，搶棉被是一種浪漫。

那她昨晚一定愛我愛到不行，
因為我半夜醒來，發現自己睡在一片荒原上，
而她在對面裹成一個完整的蠶繭。

我沒有把棉被搶回來。
我只是默默看著她睡得那麼香，
然後默默去衣櫃拿了第二條被。

真正的愛情不是共用一條棉被，
是各自擁有一條，然後假裝那是一開始就計畫好的。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個每晚把你的棉被捲走、自己睡得像嬰兒的另一半
variations: 升級版：你們各蓋各的被，從此再也不吵架，睡眠品質提升300%。原來破壞感情的不是個性不合，是同一條棉被。

en.content:
My girlfriend says stealing the blanket is romantic.

Then she must have loved me to pieces last night,
because I woke up at 3 a.m. stranded on a barren wasteland of mattress,
while she was cocooned across from me like a fully formed silkworm.

I didn't steal it back.
I just watched her sleep so peacefully,
and quietly went to the closet for a second blanket.

Real love isn't sharing one blanket.
It's each having your own, and pretending that was the plan all along.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who rolls up in your blanket every night and sleeps like a baby
variations: Upgrade: you switch to separate blankets and never fight again, sleep quality up 300%. Turns out the threat to the relationship was never incompatibility — it was one shared blanket.

items.sourceNote: 情侶共享定位梗，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
我們互相開了定位共享。

不是因為不信任，
是因為——

當她傳訊息問「你在哪」的時候，
她其實已經在地圖上看著那個小圓點了。
這題沒有標準答案，只有正確答案。

所以現在我學會了一件事：
人可以遲到，但定位不能說謊。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個跟你共享定位、問你在哪其實只是在測試你誠實度的另一半
variations: 升級版：有天她的小圓點停在一家蛋糕店三十分鐘，你假裝不知道。生日當天你表現得很驚訝。這就是定位共享時代的浪漫——知道，但裝作不知道。

en.content:
We share our locations with each other.

Not because we don't trust each other,
but because —

when she texts 'where are you,'
she's already staring at that little dot on the map.
There's no standard answer to this question. Only a correct one.

So I've learned one thing:
a person can be late, but the location pin cannot lie.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who shares location with you and asks 'where are you' purely to test your honesty
variations: Upgrade: one day her dot parks at a bakery for thirty minutes and you pretend not to notice. On your birthday you act surprised. That's romance in the location-sharing era — knowing, but pretending you don't.

items.sourceNote: 情侶傳貼圖回應梗，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
交往初期，他傳一顆愛心，
我回一顆愛心。

交往三年後，他傳一顆愛心，
我回一張披薩貼圖。

這不是不愛了，
這是進化。

我們已經到了不需要用愛心證明愛的階段——
現在我們用「等等要不要叫披薩」來表達。
而他秒回「好」的速度，就是這段感情最真實的浪漫。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個跟你早就不傳愛心、改用點外送來表達愛的另一半
variations: 升級版：最高境界是你連訊息都不用傳，走到客廳兩人對看一眼，他就默默打開外送App。這叫做不用言語的愛，也叫做都很餓。

en.content:
Early in the relationship, he sends a heart.
I send a heart back.

Three years in, he sends a heart.
I reply with a pizza sticker.

This isn't falling out of love.
This is evolution.

We've reached the stage where we don't need hearts to prove anything —
now we say it with 'wanna order pizza later.'
And the speed of his instant 'yes' is the truest romance in this relationship.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner you've stopped trading hearts with and now express love through takeout orders
variations: Upgrade: the final form is when you don't even need to text. You both walk into the living room, exchange one look, and he silently opens the delivery app. Call it love without words. Also call it both being hungry.

items.sourceNote: 情侶比喻 Wi-Fi 的關係梗，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
我們的感情就像家裡的Wi-Fi。

大部分時候很穩，
偶爾會莫名其妙斷一下，
有時候訊號弱到讓人想砸手機。

但只要它一消失，
我整個人就會在房間裡焦躁地走來走去，
喊著：「喂？你還在嗎？怎麼都沒反應？」

然後我們都心知肚明——
再怎麼不爽，
誰都不可能真的把它關掉。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個你偶爾想掐死、但斷線一秒就慌的另一半
variations: 升級版：而且我們連密碼都懶得改，因為換了之後最麻煩的是要重新適應彼此——這個過程光想就累。
反向版：吵架版的Wi-Fi是這樣：訊號明明還在，但其中一方故意切成飛航模式，然後等對方來問「你怎麼了」。

en.content:
Our relationship is basically the home Wi-Fi.

Mostly stable,
occasionally drops for no reason,
and sometimes the signal gets so weak I want to throw my phone.

But the second it disappears,
I'm pacing around the room in a panic going,
'Hello? You still there? Why aren't you responding?'

And deep down we both know —
no matter how annoyed we get,
neither of us is ever actually turning it off.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner you occasionally want to strangle but panic over the moment they go quiet
variations: Upgrade: we don't even bother changing the password, because the real hassle isn't security — it's re-learning how to deal with each other. Exhausting just thinking about it.
Argument version: fighting Wi-Fi is when the signal is clearly still there, but one side switches to airplane mode on purpose and waits for the other to ask 'what's wrong.'

items.sourceNote: 情侶一起下廚引發煙霧警報的梗，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
我們決定今晚要浪漫一點，
兩個人一起下廚。

結果三十分鐘後，
餐桌上沒有燭光晚餐，
只有一台拼命尖叫的煙霧警報器，
和兩個一邊揮毛巾一邊大笑的人。

外送員送來救命的鹹酥雞時還問：
「你們是不是在辦趴？」

我說對啊，
慶祝我們又活過了一頓自己煮的晚餐。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個跟你一起把廚房當實驗室、最後還是叫外送的另一半
variations: 升級版：後來我們達成共識——所謂一起下廚，就是一個負責點火，一個負責點外送App，分工明確，從不爭執。
短版：浪漫的兩人晚餐，最後是煙霧警報器全程伴唱完成的。

en.content:
We decided to be romantic tonight
and cook together.

Thirty minutes later,
there was no candlelit dinner on the table,
just a smoke alarm screaming its lungs out
and two people fanning it with towels and cracking up.

When the delivery guy showed up with our rescue fried chicken, he asked,
'You guys throwing a party?'

I said yeah,
celebrating that we survived another homemade dinner.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who treats the kitchen like a lab with you and still ends up ordering takeout
variations: Upgrade: we eventually agreed that 'cooking together' means one of us lights the stove and the other opens the delivery app. Clear division of labor, zero arguments.
Short version: our romantic dinner for two was completed with the smoke alarm singing backup the entire time.

items.sourceNote: 情侶為了冷氣溫度像外交談判的梗，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
我和他為了冷氣溫度，
談判得比兩國元首還認真。

他主張二十二度，理由是「人活著要有尊嚴」，
我堅持二十六度，理由是「我不想凍成冰棒」。

最後我們達成歷史性協議：
設二十四度，
然後他偷偷把遙控器藏在沙發底下，
我半夜起來翻箱倒櫃地找。

所謂和平，
從來都只是暫時的停戰。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個跟你為了一兩度可以冷戰整晚的另一半
variations: 升級版：最終解決方案是各睡一條被子，他用薄的我用厚的，從此世界和平，代價是兩個人在同一張床上過著不同的季節。
反向版：冬天版的戰爭剛好相反——他要開暖氣，我要開窗，理由依然是「人活著要有尊嚴」，只是立場對調。

en.content:
He and I negotiate the thermostat
more seriously than two heads of state.

He argues for 72, on the grounds that 'a person deserves dignity.'
I hold the line at 78, on the grounds that 'I refuse to become a popsicle.'

We finally reached a historic agreement:
set it to 75,
after which he secretly hid the remote under the couch
and I spent the middle of the night ransacking the living room for it.

Peace, it turns out,
is only ever a temporary ceasefire.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who'll cold-war you all night over a degree or two
variations: Upgrade: the final solution was separate blankets — his thin, mine thick — and world peace was achieved. The price: two people living in different seasons in the same bed.
Reverse version: the winter war is the exact opposite — he wants the heat on, I want the window open, both still citing 'a person deserves dignity,' just with the sides swapped.

items.sourceNote: 情侶討論晚餐吃什麼永遠沒結論的梗，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
「今天晚餐想吃什麼？」
「都可以。」
「那吃火鍋？」
「不要，太熱。」
「那吃義大利麵？」
「不要，上禮拜吃過。」
「那你想吃什麼？」
「都可以啊。」

我們就這樣站在玄關，
一個說都可以，一個列出全市餐廳，
從天亮討論到天黑。

最後的結論永遠是——
「那……隨便煮個泡麵好了。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個說都可以、卻每個提議都否決的另一半
variations: 升級版：後來我學乖了，不問「想吃什麼」，改問「這兩家你選一家」，成功率瞬間提升百分之兩百，這就是談判的藝術。
短版：情侶最難的不是相愛，是回答「晚餐吃什麼」。

en.content:
'What do you want for dinner?'
'Anything's fine.'
'Hot pot?'
'No, too hot.'
'Pasta?'
'No, we had that last week.'
'So what DO you want?'
'Anything, really.'

And so we stand by the front door,
one of us saying 'anything,' the other listing every restaurant in town,
debating from sunrise to sunset.

The conclusion is always the same —
'You know what... let's just make instant noodles.'
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who says 'anything's fine' and then vetoes every single suggestion
variations: Upgrade: I eventually wised up and stopped asking 'what do you want.' Now I ask 'pick one of these two,' and my success rate shot up 200%. That's the art of negotiation.
Short version: the hardest part of being a couple isn't falling in love — it's answering 'what's for dinner.'

items.sourceNote: 同床搶棉被與打呼的梗，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
凌晨三點，我醒來，
發現自己身上一塊棉被都沒有，
而旁邊那個人，
像春捲一樣把整條被子捲在身上，
還睡得一臉幸福。

我輕輕拉了一下，
他立刻像有偵測雷達一樣捲得更緊，
嘴裡還喃喃說了句：「冷……」

冷的是我啊。

所謂同床共枕，
就是每天晚上進行一場無聲的棉被爭奪戰，
而我永遠是戰敗的一方。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個每天晚上把你的棉被捲走、自己卻睡得很安穩的另一半
variations: 升級版：後來我買了兩條被子，各蓋各的，世界瞬間和平了。代價是——半夜想靠過去取暖，要先翻過一道棉被長城。
短版：愛一個人，就是甘願每晚跟他搶棉被，搶輸了還幫他蓋好。

en.content:
Three a.m. I wake up
to find not a single inch of blanket on me,
while the person beside me
is rolled up in the entire thing like a spring roll,
sleeping with a look of pure bliss.

I give it a gentle tug,
and he instantly rolls tighter, like he's got radar,
mumbling, 'So cold...'

I'm the one who's cold.

Sharing a bed
means waging a silent blanket war every night,
and I am forever on the losing side.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who steals your blanket every night and somehow sleeps like a baby
variations: Upgrade: I eventually bought two separate blankets, and world peace was achieved instantly. The price — to cuddle up for warmth, I now have to scale a great wall of duvet.
Short version: loving someone means losing the blanket war every night, and tucking them back in anyway.

items.sourceNote: 情侶間搶食物、零食分享的梗，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
別人問我們之間有沒有信任。

有啊，
我完全信任他——
只要我一離開座位，
他一定會偷吃我的薯條。

這種信任，
比天還高，比海還深，
從來沒讓我失望過。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個嘴上說「我不餓」、卻把你盤子吃光的另一半
variations: 升級版：現在我學乖了，點餐一律多點一份「他說他不要、但其實會吃掉一半」的量，這叫超前部署。
反向版：他說他點的我一口都別想吃，結果吃到一半開始往我嘴邊送，情侶之間的食物，從來沒有真正的所有權。

en.content:
People ask if there's trust between us.

Of course there is.
I trust him completely —
the second I leave my seat,
he will absolutely steal my fries.

That kind of trust
is higher than the sky, deeper than the sea,
and it has never once let me down.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who says 'I'm not hungry' and then clears your plate
variations: Upgrade: I've wised up — now I always order an extra portion to cover 'the half he swears he won't touch.' Call it strategic planning.
Reverse version: he insists I can't have a single bite of his — then halfway through starts feeding it to me. Between a couple, food never really has an owner.

items.sourceNote: 情侶等對方回訊息、已讀的梗，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
我傳了一段很長的訊息給他，
認真規劃了我們週末的行程、晚餐、還有未來。

他回了一個字：「好。」

我盯著那個「好」，
研究了四十七遍，
試圖從一個字裡讀出他到底是開心、敷衍、還是在忙。

後來我才懂，
男生的一個「好」，
背後有時候真的就只有一個「好」，
沒有伏筆，沒有暗示，
是我自己加了太多戲。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個總是惜字如金、害你想太多的另一半
variations: 升級版：我也學會了，現在他傳長訊息給我，我也回「好」，他立刻打電話來問「你是不是在生氣」，原來這招這麼有用。
短版：談戀愛最累的不是吵架，是研究對方那個「好」到底是什麼意思。

en.content:
I sent him a long text,
carefully planning our weekend, dinner, and our entire future.

He replied with one word: 'Ok.'

I stared at that 'Ok,'
studied it forty-seven times,
trying to decode whether he was happy, brushing me off, or just busy.

Eventually I understood:
sometimes a guy's 'Ok'
really is just an 'Ok' —
no subtext, no hidden meaning.
I was the one writing the whole drama.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner whose one-word replies send you spiraling
variations: Upgrade: I learned the trick too — now when he sends a long text, I reply 'Ok,' and he calls within seconds asking 'Are you mad at me?' Turns out it works both ways.
Short version: the exhausting part of dating isn't the fights — it's decoding what that 'Ok' actually means.

items.sourceNote: 情侶共用充電線、互相依賴的梗，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
我們家只有一條充電線還是好的，
所以每天晚上都要上演一場談判：

「今天輪到我充。」
「可是我明天要早起。」
「我手機剩百分之三。」
「我剩百分之一。」

最後我們的解決方案是——
買了第二條線。

結果第二天，
那條線就被其中一個人帶去公司，
我們又回到了只有一條線的原始狀態。

感情的本質，
大概就是兩個人搶一條充電線，搶到天荒地老。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個總是跟你搶充電線、家裡永遠只有一條線是好的另一半
variations: 升級版：後來我們各自的線都用顏色做了記號，他的藍色我的粉色，從此天下太平——直到有一天藍色那條壞了。
短版：所謂同居，就是兩個人共用一條充電線，誰先沒電誰就輸。

en.content:
There's only one working charger in our place,
so every night we hold a negotiation:

'It's my turn to charge.'
'But I have to get up early tomorrow.'
'My phone's at three percent.'
'Mine's at one percent.'

Our final solution was —
to buy a second cable.

The next day,
one of us took that cable to the office,
and we were right back to the original one-charger economy.

The essence of a relationship
is probably two people fighting over a single charger, forever.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who's always racing you for the one cable that still works
variations: Upgrade: we eventually color-coded our cables — his blue, mine pink — and there was peace in the land. Until the day the blue one broke.
Short version: living together means sharing one charger, and whoever dies first loses.

items.sourceNote: 情侶共用冰箱信任問題，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
同居之後我才知道，
共用一台冰箱是世界上最大的信任考驗。

我冰了一塊蛋糕，貼了便利貼：「這是我的，請勿動。」
隔天蛋糕還在，便利貼也還在，
但蛋糕上多了一道明顯被叉子刮過的痕跡。

我問他：「你是不是吃了？」
他說：「我只是確認一下它有沒有壞。」
「那痕跡呢？」
「我用嘴巴確認的。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個總是偷吃你冰在冰箱裡、還理直氣壯說「幫你試毒」的另一半
variations: 升級版：後來我學乖了，把蛋糕藏在最裡面用一袋青菜擋住——果然安全，因為他從來不會主動碰到青菜。
短版：所謂信任，就是把最後一塊蛋糕冰進冰箱，然後祈禱。

en.content:
After moving in together, I learned that
sharing one fridge is the greatest trust exercise on earth.

I put a slice of cake in there with a sticky note: 'This is mine, do not touch.'
The next day the cake was still there, the note was still there,
but there was a clear fork-scrape mark across the top.

I asked him, 'Did you eat some?'
He said, 'I was just checking if it had gone bad.'
'And the mark?'
'I checked with my mouth.'
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who raids your fridge stash and insists they were 'just taste-testing it for safety'
variations: Upgrade: I eventually learned to hide the cake at the very back behind a bag of vegetables — perfectly safe, because he never voluntarily touches vegetables.
Short version: trust is putting the last slice of cake in the fridge and then praying.

items.sourceNote: 情侶冷氣溫度之戰，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
我們對所有事情都有共識，
除了冷氣的溫度。

我覺得二十四度剛好，他覺得二十四度是冰原。
他偷偷把溫度調到二十七，我半夜又調回二十四，
他再調到二十六，我再調回二十五，
就這樣一個晚上遙控器被按了十幾次。

後來我們終於談成了一個結論：
蓋兩條不同厚度的棉被，
各過各的氣候。

外交的最高境界，
就是在同一張床上，活在兩個不同的季節。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個跟你共用一台冷氣、卻活在完全不同體感溫度的另一半
variations: 升級版：後來我們買了兩台電風扇各自吹，看起來像兩個不相干的人睡在一起，但這就是和平的代價。
短版：愛情可以克服一切，除了冷氣設幾度。

en.content:
We agree on everything,
except the air conditioning.

I think 24 degrees is perfect; he thinks 24 degrees is a glacier.
He sneaks it up to 27, I bump it back to 24 in the middle of the night,
he nudges it to 26, I drop it to 25,
and that's how the remote gets pressed a dozen times before dawn.

Eventually we negotiated a treaty:
two blankets of different thickness,
each living in our own climate.

The pinnacle of diplomacy
is sharing one bed while living in two different seasons.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner you share one thermostat with despite living at completely different body temperatures
variations: Upgrade: we eventually bought two separate fans pointed at each of us — it looks like two strangers sleeping together, but that's the price of peace.
Short version: love conquers all, except what temperature to set the AC.

items.sourceNote: 情侶打呼日常，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
他睡覺會打呼，
剛交往的時候我覺得吵到不行，
常常半夜推他一把：「翻身、翻身。」

交往三年後，
有一次他出差，房間安靜得不得了，
我居然失眠了，
因為少了那個熟悉的、像遠方雷聲一樣的背景音。

原來人類很奇怪，
當初讓你睡不著的聲音，
到後來變成你睡得著的唯一原因。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個睡覺會打呼、但你已經離不開那個聲音的另一半——順便附上「快回來，太安靜了」
variations: 升級版：後來我錄了他打呼的聲音存在手機裡，出差時拿來助眠——他知道後說我有病，但那天晚上他在電話那頭故意打呼給我聽。
短版：所謂習慣一個人，就是連他的呼聲都變成你的搖籃曲。

en.content:
He snores when he sleeps.
Early on, I found it unbearable,
and I'd shove him at night: 'Roll over, roll over.'

Three years in,
he went away on a work trip, the room was dead quiet,
and I actually couldn't sleep,
because that familiar, distant-thunder background noise was gone.

Turns out humans are strange:
the sound that once kept you awake
eventually becomes the only reason you can fall asleep.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner whose snoring you can no longer sleep without — bonus points for adding 'come home, it's too quiet here'
variations: Upgrade: I eventually recorded his snoring on my phone to fall asleep to on his trips — he said I was unwell when he found out, but that night he deliberately snored down the phone for me.
Short version: getting used to someone means even their snoring becomes your lullaby.

items.sourceNote: 情侶猜心遊戲，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
我們情侶之間有一個從來沒人贏過的遊戲，
叫做「猜我在想什麼」。

「你猜我現在想什麼？」
「想我？」
「不是。」
「想吃東西？」
「……你怎麼知道。」
「我沒猜中啊，你說不是。」
「我是說不是只想吃東西，我是想跟你一起吃東西。」

你看，
這遊戲的規則就是：
不管你猜什麼，標準答案永遠由出題的人事後決定。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個總是要你「猜猜看」、然後不管你猜什麼都說不對的另一半
variations: 升級版：後來我學會了，無論她問我猜什麼，我一律回答「你在想我有多愛你」——意外地，這個答案的命中率高達百分之百。
短版：情侶版猜謎遊戲，唯一的規則就是出題者永遠是對的。

en.content:
We have a game no one has ever won,
called 'guess what I'm thinking.'

'Guess what I'm thinking right now?'
'About me?'
'No.'
'About food?'
'...How did you know.'
'I didn't get it right, you said no.'
'I meant not ONLY food — I'm thinking about eating it with you.'

See,
the rule of this game is:
no matter what you guess, the correct answer is decided afterward by whoever asked.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who's always making you 'take a guess' and then says you're wrong no matter what you say
variations: Upgrade: I eventually learned that whatever she asks me to guess, I just answer 'you're thinking about how much I love you' — and somehow that answer has a 100% hit rate.
Short version: the couple's guessing game has exactly one rule — the one who asks is always right.

items.sourceNote: 情侶搶棉被笑話，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
結婚誓詞應該要加一條：
「我願意，公平地分享這條棉被。」

但現實是——
半夜醒來，我發現自己像一隻被丟在岸上的魚，
全身赤裸地暴露在冷空氣中，
而身旁那個人，
把整條棉被捲成一根春捲，
還睡得一臉幸福。

愛情會讓人盲目，
但棉被會讓人清醒。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個每天把你的棉被捲走的另一半，附上一句「今晚我要裝GPS追蹤器在棉被上」
variations: 升級版：我後來買了兩條獨立的棉被，從此世界和平。原來有些感情問題，不需要溝通，只需要採購。
短版：所謂同床共枕，就是兩個人，一條棉被，零點五個人有蓋到。

en.content:
Wedding vows should include one extra line:
"I promise to share this blanket fairly."

But the reality is —
I wake up at 3 a.m. like a fish washed up on shore,
fully exposed to the cold air,
while the person next to me
has rolled the entire blanket into a spring roll
and is sleeping with a look of pure bliss.

Love makes you blind,
but a stolen blanket wakes you right up.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who steals your blanket every single night, with 'tonight I'm installing a GPS tracker on the duvet'
variations: Upgrade: I eventually bought two separate blankets, and there was peace on earth. Turns out some relationship problems don't need a conversation — they need a purchase.
Short version: sharing a bed means two people, one blanket, and exactly half a person covered.

items.sourceNote: 情侶像手機充電線笑話，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
有人問我，交往久了還有沒有心動的感覺。

我說有啊，
每次我手機剩百分之三，
而我男朋友剛好從口袋掏出一條充電線遞給我，
那一瞬間，我的心跳得比剛認識的時候還快。

後來我想通了：
他不是我的命中注定，
他是我的隨身行動電源。
但在這個時代，這兩個其實是同一件事。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個總是隨身帶充電線、在你手機快沒電時拯救你的另一半——現代版的英雄救美
variations: 升級版：分手後最捨不得的，竟然是他的快充頭。後來我們複合了，朋友說是真愛，只有我知道，是因為我還沒買到一樣好用的。
短版：他不在的時候我會慌，跟我手機剩百分之一的時候一模一樣。

en.content:
Someone asked if there's still a spark after dating this long.

There is.
Every time my phone hits 3%
and my boyfriend pulls a charging cable out of his pocket and hands it to me,
my heart races faster than it did when we first met.

Eventually I figured it out:
he's not my soulmate,
he's my portable power bank.
But in this day and age, those are pretty much the same thing.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who always carries a charger and rescues your dying phone — the modern knight in shining armor
variations: Upgrade: after we broke up, the thing I missed most was his fast charger. We got back together; friends called it true love. Only I know it's because I hadn't found one as good yet.
Short version: I panic when he's not around — exactly the way I panic at 1% battery.

items.sourceNote: 情侶搶遙控器笑話，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
在我們家，
遙控器的擁有權是一種神聖不可侵犯的世襲制度。

誰先坐到沙發上、手裡握著遙控器，
誰就是這個夜晚的最高統治者。

有一次我去上廁所，回來發現頻道被換了。
我問：「誰准你換的？」
他說：「你離開王座了，王位自動空缺。」

那一刻我才明白，
所謂愛情裡的權力鬥爭，
從來不是為了遙控器，
是為了證明「我說了算」。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個總是趁你離開就搶遙控器、然後理直氣壯的另一半
variations: 升級版：後來我們買了第二支遙控器，結果發現問題從來不是遙控器不夠，是兩個人都想看不一樣的東西卻又想坐在一起。
短版：情侶間真正的權力中心，不是錢包，是遙控器。

en.content:
In our house,
ownership of the remote is a sacred, hereditary institution.

Whoever sits on the couch first, remote in hand,
becomes the supreme ruler of the evening.

One time I went to the bathroom and came back to find the channel changed.
I said, "Who gave you permission?"
He said, "You left the throne. The seat was automatically vacant."

That's when I understood:
the power struggle in a relationship
was never about the remote.
It's about proving 'I'm the one who decides.'
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who grabs the remote the second you leave and then acts completely justified about it
variations: Upgrade: we eventually bought a second remote, only to discover the problem was never a shortage of remotes — it's two people wanting to watch different things while sitting together.
Short version: the real seat of power in a relationship isn't the wallet, it's the remote.

items.sourceNote: 情侶為一個詞吵架的真實故事，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
我們曾經為了「可能」這兩個字，冷戰了一整天。

起因是我說：「我『可能』會去買晚餐。」
他理解成「我一定會買」，
我的意思是「我也許會買，看心情」。

等他餓著肚子等到八點，發現晚餐沒有出現，
世界大戰就此爆發。

現在好了，
我們家裡只要有人說出「可能」「也許」「應該」，
另一個人就會立刻舉手：
「這句話現在生效嗎？請出示書面合約。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個跟你為了一句話的解讀方式可以吵超久的另一半，吵架的人會心一笑
variations: 升級版：後來「可能」變成我們的內部暗號，只要有人想逃避責任就會說「我可能會」，然後兩個人一起大笑。當年的戰火，變成今天的笑點。
短版：情侶吵架的最高境界，是為了一個副詞的精確定義，互不相讓。

en.content:
We once had a full-day cold war over the word "might."

It started when I said, "I 'might' grab dinner."
He interpreted that as "I definitely will,"
while I meant "maybe, depending on my mood."

By 8 p.m., starving and dinnerless,
world war broke out.

Now, in our house,
the moment anyone says "might," "maybe," or "probably,"
the other immediately raises a hand:
"Is that statement legally binding? Please produce a written contract."
en.editorNote: Send to the partner you can argue with for hours over how one sentence should be interpreted — fellow over-thinkers will smile
variations: Upgrade: 'might' became our inside code — whenever one of us wants to dodge responsibility, we say 'I might,' then crack up together. The old battlefield is now the punchline.
Short version: peak couple arguing is two people refusing to back down over the precise definition of an adverb.

items.sourceNote: 情侶搶叉子的真實趣事，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
我們最經典的一場戰爭，是為了一支叉子。

每次吃飯，他都不拿自己的叉子，
而是趁我夾完一口，順手把我的叉子拿去用，
用完再默默放回來。

我問他：「桌上明明有十支叉子，你為什麼一定要用我這支？」
他說：「因為你這支離我比較近，而且……比較有感情。」

我們現在認真討論，
要不要去刺青店，
各自在手上刺四個字：「不准動我叉子」。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個吃飯總是搶用你餐具的另一半，附上「下次我帶兩副，一副給你，請勿越界」
variations: 升級版：後來我發現，他搶的不是叉子，是一種「我們是一夥的」的感覺。從那天起，我把叉子推到我們中間，誰都可以用。愛情有時候就是，連叉子都不分你我。
短版：情侶之間最深的羈絆，是你的叉子永遠也是他的叉子。

en.content:
Our most legendary war was fought over a single fork.

Every meal, instead of grabbing his own fork,
he'd wait until I took a bite, swipe mine to use it,
then quietly put it back.

I asked, "There are ten forks on this table — why do you insist on using mine?"
He said, "Because yours is closer to me, and... it has more sentimental value."

We are now seriously discussing
whether to go to a tattoo parlor
and each get four words inked on our hands: 'Do not touch my fork.'
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who keeps stealing your utensils mid-meal, with 'next time I'm bringing two sets — one for you, stay in your lane'
variations: Upgrade: I eventually realized he wasn't stealing the fork, he was stealing a feeling of 'we're a team.' So I pushed the fork to the middle — anyone can use it now. Sometimes love means not even the fork is yours or mine.
Short version: the deepest bond between a couple is that your fork is forever also their fork.

items.sourceNote: 冷氣溫度之爭的情侶笑話，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
我們家最大的政治分歧，是冷氣溫度。

我覺得二十四度剛好。
她覺得二十四度是想把她冰成標本。

後來我們達成了歷史性協議：
設二十六度，然後她穿短袖，我蓋棉被。

同一個房間，兩個季節。
這就是愛情教會我的——
沒有對錯，只有各退一步，然後各自難受。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個跟你搶冷氣遙控器的另一半，附上「今晚誰先睡著誰就輸了」
variations: 升級版：後來我買了一條薄毯放在她那側，從此天下太平。原來感情裡很多問題，不是靠講道理解決的，是靠多買一條毯子。
短版：情侶最難談的不是未來，是冷氣要設幾度。

en.content:
The biggest political divide in our home is the thermostat.

I think 75°F is perfect.
She thinks 75°F is an attempt to freeze her into a museum specimen.

Eventually we reached a historic peace treaty:
set it to 78°F, then she wears a tank top and I sleep under a blanket.

Same room, two seasons.
That's what love has taught me —
there's no right or wrong, just both of us compromising, then both of us suffering.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner you fight the thermostat with, plus 'tonight whoever falls asleep first loses'
variations: Upgrade: I eventually bought a thin throw for her side of the bed, and peace reigned ever after. Turns out a lot of relationship problems aren't solved by logic — they're solved by buying one more blanket.
Short version: the hardest thing for a couple to negotiate isn't the future, it's the thermostat setting.

items.sourceNote: 情侶搶遙控器的笑話，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
我問他可不可以把遙控器拿給我。

他說：「遙控器在你手上啊。」
我說：「不是這個，是『決定看什麼』的那個權力。」

他沉默了三秒，說：
「那個遙控器，從我們交往第一天起，就一直在你手上，而且沒電過。」

我假裝生氣，其實心裡有點得意。
原來在一段感情裡，真正的遙控器，從來不需要換電池。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個總是讓你決定看什麼的另一半，附上「謝謝你永遠把遙控器讓給我」
variations: 升級版：後來我把遙控器推給他，說「今天你選」。他愣了很久，最後選了一部我一直想看的。原來把選擇權交出去，對方反而更懂你想要什麼。
短版：情侶間真正的權力，不是握著遙控器，是知道對方想看什麼。

en.content:
I asked him to pass me the remote.

He said, "The remote's already in your hand."
I said, "Not this one — I mean the power to decide what we watch."

He went quiet for three seconds, then said,
"That remote has been in your hand since day one of this relationship, and it's never once run out of batteries."

I pretended to be annoyed, but secretly I was pretty pleased.
Turns out in a relationship, the real remote never needs new batteries.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who always lets you pick what to watch, with 'thanks for forever handing me the remote'
variations: Upgrade: I pushed the remote back to him and said 'you choose tonight.' He froze for a long time, then picked something I'd been wanting to see for weeks. Turns out when you hand over the choice, your partner understands you even better.
Short version: the real power in a couple isn't holding the remote — it's knowing what the other person wants to watch.

items.sourceNote: 另一半打呼的笑話，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
我跟她說：「你昨晚打呼超大聲。」

她一臉震驚：「不可能，我整晚都醒著聽你打呼。」

我說：「那你怎麼會聽到我打呼，你不是醒著嗎？」
她說：「對啊，我醒著，因為你打呼太大聲。」

這個邏輯閉環，我們吵了二十分鐘，
最後的結論是——
我們兩個都打呼，只是都不承認，
而且都把對方當鬧鐘。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個堅持自己不打呼的另一半，附上「下次我錄音給你聽」
variations: 升級版：後來我真的錄音，放給她聽。她聽完說「這聲音好可愛喔」，然後我就再也沒辦法生氣了。原來連缺點，在愛的人耳裡都能變成優點。
短版：情侶之間最公平的事，就是你嫌我打呼，我也嫌你打呼，扯平。

en.content:
I told her, "You were snoring so loud last night."

She looked genuinely shocked: "Impossible, I was awake all night listening to YOU snore."

I said, "Then how did you hear me snore if you were awake?"
She said, "Exactly, I was awake — because you were snoring so loud."

We argued about this logical loop for twenty minutes,
and the final conclusion was —
we both snore, neither will admit it,
and we each use the other as an alarm clock.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who insists they don't snore, with 'next time I'm recording it for you'
variations: Upgrade: I actually recorded it and played it back for her. She listened and said 'aw, that sound is kind of cute,' and just like that I couldn't be mad anymore. Turns out even flaws sound like charms in the ears of someone who loves you.
Short version: the fairest thing between a couple is that you complain about my snoring and I complain about yours — we're even.

items.sourceNote: 情侶採買訊息的笑話，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
有人問我，交往久了還剩什麼浪漫？

我打開我們的對話紀錄給他看，
