最近一百則訊息，九十則是：

「家裡還有衛生紙嗎？」
「牛奶快沒了。」
「你要不要喝奶茶，我幫你帶。」
「醬油我買了。」

他說這也太不浪漫。
我說你不懂，
當一個人願意一輩子跟你討論家裡還缺什麼，
那就是他打算跟你一直缺下去、一直補下去。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個每天跟你討論家裡缺什麼的另一半，附上「我們的浪漫就是這些採買訊息」
variations: 升級版：後來我發現，那些「要不要幫你帶」的訊息，其實才是最深的情話。因為他連你今天想喝什麼都記得。
短版：交往久了的浪漫，是兩個人一直互相問『家裡還需要什麼』，直到老去。

en.content:
Someone asked me what romance is left after dating for years.

I opened our chat history and showed them:
of the last hundred messages, ninety were:

"Do we still have toilet paper?"
"We're almost out of milk."
"Want me to grab you a milk tea on the way?"
"Got the soy sauce."

They said that's not romantic at all.
I said you don't get it —
when someone is willing to discuss what the house is running low on for the rest of their life,
that means they plan to keep running low, and keep restocking, with you.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner you text grocery lists with daily, plus 'these supply runs ARE our romance'
variations: Upgrade: I eventually realized those 'want me to grab you one?' texts are the deepest love letters there are. Because they remember even what you feel like drinking today.
Short version: the romance of a long relationship is two people forever asking each other 'what does the house need?' until they grow old.

items.sourceNote: 「絕不帶著怒氣睡覺」的笑話，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
我們交往時立下一個約定：
絕對不帶著生氣的情緒睡覺。

聽起來很成熟、很健康對吧？

結果到目前為止——
我們已經連續三天沒睡了。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個常常半夜還在跟你「把話講清楚」的另一半，附上「今晚我們早點和好早點睡好嗎」
variations: 升級版：後來我們改了約定——可以帶著怒氣睡覺，但要記得隔天起床繼續氣，這樣比較有精神吵。結果隔天起來都忘了在氣什麼了。
短版：成熟的情侶不帶怒氣睡覺，我們是熬到太睏，氣自己就消了。

en.content:
When we started dating, we made a promise:
never go to bed angry at each other.

Sounds mature and healthy, right?

Well, as of right now —
we haven't slept in three days.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who keeps you up at 2am 'just talking it through,' with 'can we make up early and actually sleep tonight?'
variations: Upgrade: we eventually amended the rule — you CAN go to bed angry, just remember to keep being angry when you wake up so the fight stays efficient. Next morning we both forgot what it was about.
Short version: mature couples never go to bed angry. We just stay up until we're too sleepy to stay mad.

items.sourceNote: 「你需要空間」結果佔據整張床的笑話，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
她說她需要一點「個人空間」。

我以為她要我別黏太緊，
結果那天晚上我醒來，
發現她睡成一個大字型，
橫跨整張雙人床，
我整個人掛在床緣，半個屁股懸空。

原來她要的空間，
就是這張床的全部。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個睡相豪邁、每晚把你擠到床邊的另一半，附上「我的個人空間只剩五公分了」
variations: 升級版：後來我學乖了，買了張更大的床。結果她睡相也跟著升級，照樣把我擠到邊邊。我終於懂了，不是床的問題，是她的氣場問題。
短版：她說想要空間，我以為是心理上的，沒想到是物理上的——整張床。

en.content:
She said she needed some "personal space."

I thought she meant I was being too clingy.
Then that night I woke up
to find her sprawled in a perfect starfish,
diagonally across the entire bed,
while I clung to the edge with half a butt-cheek hanging off.

Turns out the space she needed
was the whole mattress.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner with the heroic sleep sprawl who exiles you to the edge nightly, plus 'my personal space is down to two inches'
variations: Upgrade: I wised up and bought a bigger bed. Her sprawl simply upgraded to match, and I was right back on the edge. I finally understood — it was never the bed, it was her aura.
Short version: she said she wanted space. I assumed emotional. She meant physical — the entire bed.

items.sourceNote: 情侶搶遙控器、追劇主導權的笑話，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
情侶之間最殘酷的權力鬥爭，
不是錢，不是家事，
是——遙控器。

誰拿到遙控器，誰就掌握了今晚的命運。

所以我們發明了一套制度：
誰先洗完碗，誰就能選台。

結果現在我們家最乾淨的，
就是那個水槽。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個跟你搶遙控器搶到要靠洗碗決勝負的另一半，附上「今晚換我選，我已經洗好了」
variations: 升級版：後來我們乾脆裝了兩台電視，背對背放。結果還是會吵——吵誰的音量比較大聲。愛情果然不是消除衝突，是不斷發明新的衝突。
短版：情侶感情好不好，看遙控器在誰手上就知道了。

en.content:
The most brutal power struggle between a couple
isn't about money, isn't about chores.
It's — the remote.

Whoever holds the remote controls the fate of the entire evening.

So we invented a system:
whoever finishes the dishes first gets to pick what we watch.

Which is why the cleanest thing in our home
is now that kitchen sink.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner you duel for the remote via dishwashing races, plus 'my turn tonight — sink's already done'
variations: Upgrade: we eventually got two TVs and put them back to back. We still fight — now it's over whose volume is louder. Love isn't about eliminating conflict, it's about endlessly inventing new ones.
Short version: you can tell how a couple's doing by checking whose hand the remote is in.

items.sourceNote: 「你都沒在聽我說話」的笑話，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
她常常說：「你都沒在聽我講話。」

至少……我想她大概是這樣說的。

她又說：「你看，你又分心了。」
我立刻反駁：「我有在聽！你剛剛說的是……呃……」
「說啊。」
「……你剛剛在說話。」
「對，這部分你聽到了。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個老是說你左耳進右耳出的另一半，附上「我這次真的有在聽，你說的每個字我都……大概記得」
variations: 升級版：後來我學會一招——她講話時我就重複她最後三個字。她說「我今天好累」，我說「好累喔」，她就覺得被理解了。婚姻的智慧，有時候就是三個字。
短版：她說我都沒在聽，我想反駁，但我忘了她剛剛說什麼了。

en.content:
She often says, "You never listen to me."

At least... I think that's what she said.

Then she goes, "See, you're zoning out again."
I shot back, "I AM listening! You just said... uh..."
"Go on."
"...You were just talking."
"Right, you caught that part."
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who swears it goes in one ear and out the other, plus 'I really listened this time — every word you said I... mostly remember'
variations: Upgrade: I learned a trick — when she talks, I just repeat her last three words. She says 'I'm so tired today,' I say 'so tired, huh,' and she feels understood. The wisdom of marriage is sometimes just three words.
Short version: she said I never listen. I wanted to argue, but I'd already forgotten what she just said.

items.sourceNote: 情侶共用充電線之爭，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
我們家有兩條充電線，照理說一人一條剛剛好。

但實際運作是這樣的：
我的線永遠插在牆上的那個好位置，躺在床上滑手機剛剛好。
她的線，永遠不知道在哪。

所以每天晚上，我的線都會神祕地出現在她那邊。
而我，會在凌晨兩點，手機剩百分之三的時候，
光著腳在客廳的沙發縫、餐桌底下、洗衣籃裡，
進行一場屬於我自己的尋寶遊戲。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個老是把你充電線「借走就回不來」的另一半，附上「我的線呢？我知道在你那邊，不要裝」
variations: 升級版：後來我買了第三條線，藏起來，當作我的私房線。三天後，那條線也出現在她那邊了。我開始懷疑這個家有一個充電線的黑洞，而黑洞的名字叫做愛情。
短版：判斷一對情侶感情好不好，看他們會不會為了同一條充電線吵架就知道了。

en.content:
We own two charging cables. In theory, one for each of us. Perfectly fair.

In practice:
My cable lives in the good wall outlet, the one you can reach while lying in bed.
Her cable is... somewhere. Nobody knows.

So every single night, my cable mysteriously migrates over to her side.
And I'm the one at 2 a.m., phone at three percent,
barefoot in the living room, digging through the couch cushions, under the dining table, inside the laundry basket —
on a treasure hunt of my very own.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner whose 'just borrowing it' charging cable never comes back, plus 'where's my cable? I know it's on your side, don't play dumb'
variations: Upgrade: so I bought a third cable and hid it as my secret stash. Three days later, it had also migrated to her side. I'm starting to think this house has a charging-cable black hole, and the black hole's name is love.
Short version: you can tell how a couple's doing by whether they fight over the same charging cable.

items.sourceNote: 情侶用食物交換家事的潛規則，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
我們家有一條沒寫下來、但雙方都心知肚明的規則：

任何家事，都可以用食物交換。

我最討厭收衣服，所以每次都跟她說：「一杯手搖，我幫你收一個禮拜。」
她最討厭倒垃圾，所以每次都回我：「一份鹹酥雞，這個月垃圾都你的。」

我們以為自己在偷懶，
後來才發現，這套系統運作三年，
家裡乾乾淨淨，我們兩個各胖了五公斤。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個跟你用美食談判家事的另一半，附上「今晚這攤我請，但你的洗碗額度先預支三天」
variations: 升級版：後來我們連吵架和好都用食物計價了。她生氣，一份甜點降一級；大生氣，要加一杯飲料。婚姻顧問建議溝通，我們建議：先看菜單。
短版：在我們家，沒有什麼問題是一份鹹酥雞解決不了的；如果有，那就兩份。

en.content:
Our home has one unwritten rule that we both understand completely:

Any chore can be traded for food.

I hate folding laundry, so I always say: "One bubble tea, and I'll fold yours for a week."
She hates taking out the trash, so she fires back: "One order of fried chicken, and the trash is yours all month."

We thought we were just being lazy.
Then we realized this system has been running for three years,
the house is spotless, and we've each gained ten pounds.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner you negotiate chores with via snacks, plus 'dinner's on me tonight, but I'm pre-charging three days of dishwashing to your account'
variations: Upgrade: eventually we started pricing out our fights and make-ups in food too. Mildly annoyed? One dessert downgrade. Very annoyed? Add a drink. Counselors recommend communication. We recommend: check the menu first.
Short version: in our house there's no problem one order of fried chicken can't fix. And if there is, then two.

items.sourceNote: 情侶共用一支手機看影片的笑話，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
我們各自有一支手機，明明可以各看各的。

但每天晚上，我們還是會擠在同一支手機前，
用一副只有一邊有聲音的耳機，
一人一隻，看同一部劇。

手機放在中間，誰都看不清楚字幕，
脖子歪到隔天會痠，
但就是沒有人提議「不然我們各看各的」。

因為各看各的很方便，
但擠在一起的這種不方便，
好像才是我們在一起的意義。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個總是跟你擠一支手機看劇的另一半，附上「今晚換你那隻耳機壞掉，所以聲音全給你，畫面全給我」
variations: 升級版：後來我們終於買了平板，螢幕大、聲音好、字幕清楚。結果用了一個禮拜就收起來了，因為距離變遠了，少了那種非要靠在一起的理由。科技進步，感情倒退。
短版：判斷感情濃度，看他們願不願意共用一副壞掉一邊的耳機。

en.content:
We each have our own phone. We could easily watch our own things.

But every night, we still squeeze in front of the same phone,
sharing one pair of earbuds where only one side works,
one earbud each, watching the same show.

The phone sits between us, neither of us can read the subtitles,
our necks crane at an angle that'll ache tomorrow,
and yet nobody ever suggests, "Why don't we just watch separately?"

Because watching separately is convenient.
But this inconvenience, squished together like this,
feels like the whole point of being together.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner you always crowd around one phone with, plus 'tonight your earbud is the broken one, so you get all the sound and I get the whole screen'
variations: Upgrade: eventually we bought a tablet — big screen, great sound, crisp subtitles. We packed it away after a week, because the distance felt wrong, and we'd lost the excuse to lean into each other. Tech advanced; the romance regressed.
Short version: you can measure a couple's closeness by whether they'll share one pair of earbuds with a dead side.

items.sourceNote: 情侶逛超市分頭行動卻失散的笑話，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
我們逛超市有一套很有效率的分工：
她去拿青菜水果，我去拿零食飲料，最後在收銀台會合。

聽起來很完美。

實際上每次都是這樣：
我五分鐘就拿完，站在收銀台等。
她半小時後傳訊息：「你在哪？」
我說：「收銀台。」
她說：「哪個收銀台？」
我說：「就……收銀台。」

最後我們是靠著在店內大喊對方名字重逢的，
像一對在大賣場走失的小孩，
只是這對小孩，已經一起生活五年了。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個逛超市永遠會走丟的另一半，附上「下次我們綁一條繩子，我是認真的」
variations: 升級版：後來我們學乖了，約定走散就去「賣御飯糰那一區」集合。結果有一次那區在改裝，我們兩個在空蕩蕩的貨架前各站一邊，互相等了二十分鐘。約定地點很重要，但約定地點還在不在，更重要。
短版：情侶逛超市分頭走，是一場永遠不知道會在哪裡重逢的浪漫冒險。

en.content:
We have a very efficient system for grocery shopping:
she grabs the produce, I grab the snacks and drinks, and we meet at checkout.

Sounds perfect.

In reality, every single time:
I'm done in five minutes and stand waiting at checkout.
Half an hour later she texts: "Where are you?"
I say: "Checkout."
She says: "Which checkout?"
I say: "The... checkout."

We always end up reuniting by yelling each other's names across the store,
like two kids who got lost in a supermarket —
except these kids have been living together for five years.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who always gets lost at the grocery store, plus 'next time we're tying a rope between us, I'm serious'
variations: Upgrade: so we got smart and agreed to regroup at 'the rice-ball aisle' if we got separated. Then one time that aisle was under renovation, and we each stood at opposite ends of an empty shelf, waiting twenty minutes. A meeting spot matters — but whether the meeting spot still exists matters more.
Short version: splitting up at the grocery store is a romantic adventure where you never know where you'll reunite.

items.sourceNote: 情侶睡覺搶棉被的笑話，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
結婚前，我以為兩個人蓋一條棉被，是一件很浪漫的事。

結婚後我才知道，那是一場持續整晚、雙方都在熟睡中進行的拔河比賽。

半夜我冷醒，發現棉被全捲在她身上，她睡得像一隻蠶寶寶。
我輕輕拉回一角，她在睡夢中立刻拉回去，動作快得像有裝雷達。

後來我們的解決方案是：各蓋各的棉被。
結果第一晚，我半夜還是冷醒了，
因為我自己一個人，把自己的棉被踢到地上去了。
問題從來不是她，是我這個人不配蓋棉被。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個半夜會把棉被全捲走的另一半，附上「今天我先把棉被縫在床上，看你還怎麼捲」
variations: 升級版：後來我們買了一張更大的床、兩條更大的棉被，理論上棉被多到永遠夠用。結果現在的問題變成：兩個人睡在同一張床上，卻像隔了一條河，要傳訊息才找得到對方。棉被夠了，距離遠了。
短版：兩個人蓋一條棉被的婚姻，本質上是一場誰也不會贏的拔河。

en.content:
Before marriage, I thought sharing one blanket was the most romantic thing two people could do.

After marriage, I learned it's an all-night tug-of-war that both parties conduct while fast asleep.

I wake up freezing at 3 a.m. to find the entire blanket cocooned around her, while she sleeps like a happy little silkworm.
I gently tug back a corner. In her sleep, she instantly yanks it back — fast, like she's got radar.

Our eventual solution: separate blankets.
First night with the new system, I still woke up freezing —
because all by myself, I'd kicked my own blanket onto the floor.
The problem was never her. It's that I, as a person, simply don't deserve a blanket.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who steals the whole blanket at night, plus 'tonight I'm sewing the blanket to the bed, let's see you cocoon now'
variations: Upgrade: so we got a bigger bed and two bigger blankets — in theory, endless blanket for everyone. Now the problem is: two people in the same bed, but it's like there's a river between us, and we have to text to find each other. Plenty of blanket; plenty of distance.
Short version: a marriage under one shared blanket is, at its core, a tug-of-war nobody ever wins.

items.sourceNote: 情侶搶充電線題材，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
家裡明明有四條充電線。
但永遠只有一條是「好用的那條」。

而那條，永遠插在她那邊。

我手機剩百分之三，全屋翻箱倒櫃，最後找到她正用著那條，幫她那台百分之八十的手機充電。
我說：「給我用一下嘛。」
她說：「你那條也可以充啊。」
我說：「那條充很慢。」
她說：「對啊，所以我才不要用。」

原來我們在一起這麼多年，唯一沒共識的，就是那條線該插在誰的插座上。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個老是把好充電線藏在自己那側的另一半，附一句「今晚那條線歸我，不然你睡客廳充電」
variations: 升級版：後來我們各買各的快充頭，以為從此天下太平。結果現在搶的是插座——牆上就兩個孔，兩個人四台裝置，每天晚上都要協商誰的手機可以活到明天。
短版：愛情裡最深的承諾，不是「我養你」，是「這條好線借你充」。

en.content:
We have four charging cables in this house.
But only one of them is ever "the good one."

And it is always plugged in on her side.

My phone hits three percent, I tear the whole apartment apart, and I find the good cable already in use — topping up her phone, which is sitting at eighty.
I say, "Can I borrow it for a sec?"
She says, "Just use your own cable."
I say, "Mine charges slow."
She says, "Exactly. That's why I'm not using it."

After all these years together, the one thing we've never reached consensus on is whose outlet the good cable belongs in.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who hoards the good charging cable on their side, with 'tonight that cable is mine or you can sleep on the couch and charge there'
variations: Upgrade: so we each bought our own fast charger, figured peace at last. Now we fight over the outlet — two holes in the wall, two people, four devices, and every night we negotiate whose phone gets to survive till morning.
Short version: the deepest promise in love isn't 'I'll take care of you,' it's 'here, charge with the good cable.'

items.sourceNote: 情侶冷氣溫度大戰，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
他覺得二十四度是天堂。
她覺得二十四度是冰庫。

於是我們達成了偉大的妥協：設二十六度。
結果他熱到睡不著，她冷到要蓋棉被。

二十六度的偉大之處在於——它讓兩個人，同時，用不同的理由，覺得自己吃虧。

婚姻顧問說這叫「雙輸」。
我們稱之為「公平」。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個總是偷偷把冷氣調高或調低的另一半，附一句「遙控器在我手上，今晚這間房我說了算」
variations: 升級版：後來我們買了兩台電風扇各吹各的，以為解決了。結果現在吵的是風扇要轉頭還是定點，以及為什麼她的風會掃到我這邊。問題從來不是溫度，是兩個人想活在不同的氣候帶。
短版：情侶之間最危險的三個字，不是「我們分手」，是「誰動了冷氣」。

en.content:
He thinks seventy-five is paradise.
She thinks seventy-five is a meat locker.

So we reached a glorious compromise: we set it to seventy.
Now he's too hot to sleep, and she needs a blanket.

The genius of seventy degrees is that it makes two people, simultaneously, feel cheated for completely different reasons.

A marriage counselor would call this "lose-lose."
We call it "fair."
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who keeps secretly nudging the thermostat, with 'the remote is in my hands and tonight this room runs on my settings'
variations: Upgrade: so we each got our own fan, figured that solved it. Now we argue about whether the fan should oscillate or stay put, and why her airflow keeps sweeping onto my side. It was never about temperature — it's two people who want to live in different climate zones.
Short version: the most dangerous three words between a couple aren't 'we should break up,' they're 'who touched the thermostat.'

items.sourceNote: 「需要買什麼嗎」訊息日常，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
交往久了之後，我們的訊息對話框長這樣：

「在外面，要買什麼嗎」
「衛生紙」
「好」

「到家了」
「衛生紙忘了買」

這三段對話，可以無限循環，跨越好幾個月。
衛生紙永遠在被想起和被遺忘之間徘徊，像一個我們都不願面對的共同課題。

談戀愛談到最後，浪漫不是燭光晚餐，是有一個人會在便利商店傳訊息問你：「要買什麼嗎。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個每次出門問「要買什麼」結果都會漏買一樣的另一半，附一句「這次你敢忘衛生紙我就把訊息截圖貼冰箱」
variations: 升級版：後來我們學乖了，改用共享購物清單 app。結果現在的問題是——兩個人都以為對方會買，最後誰都沒買，清單上那行字孤零零地存在了兩個禮拜。科技進步了，默契還在原地。
短版：成熟的愛情，就是把「我愛你」翻譯成「在外面，要買什麼嗎」。

en.content:
After you've been together long enough, our text thread looks like this:

"I'm out, need anything?"
"Toilet paper."
"Got it."

"Home now."
"You forgot the toilet paper."

Those three exchanges can loop forever, stretching across whole months.
The toilet paper lives permanently between being remembered and being forgotten, like a shared assignment neither of us wants to face.

In the end, romance isn't a candlelit dinner. It's having someone text you from a convenience store: "You need anything?"
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who asks 'need anything?' every trip and still forgets one item, with 'forget the toilet paper one more time and I'm taping this screenshot to the fridge'
variations: Upgrade: so we got smart and switched to a shared shopping-list app. Now the problem is both of us assume the other will buy it, nobody does, and that one line sits alone on the list for two weeks. Tech advanced; our telepathy stayed put.
Short version: mature love is just translating 'I love you' into 'I'm out, you need anything?'

items.sourceNote: 情侶關係像軟體更新的比喻，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
有人說，一段關係就像系統更新——漫長、莫名其妙，但總是在變好。

我覺得這比喻太樂觀了。

我們的關係比較像那種更新：
下載了四十分鐘，跑到百分之九十九卡住，跳出「發生未知錯誤」，然後叫你重開機。
重開機之後，問題沒解決，但也沒變更糟，於是你就學會跟它共存。

偶爾它會自己變順，你也不知道為什麼，你只敢小聲說一句「現在這樣挺好的」，然後絕對不去碰任何設定。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個跟你「磨合中」的另一半，附一句「我們現在大概在百分之九十九，拜託不要在這時候重開機」
variations: 升級版：交往第一年是大版本更新，天翻地覆什麼都要重學。後來變成小修補，每次吵架就像在修一個 bug。最近我們進入了「穩定版」——很久沒更新了，但也很久沒當機，這大概就是長久關係的最高境界。
短版：好的關係不是沒有 bug，是兩個人都懶得換系統了。

en.content:
They say a relationship is like a software update — long, confusing, but always improving.

I think that metaphor is way too optimistic.

Ours is more like the kind of update that:
downloads for forty minutes, freezes at ninety-nine percent, throws an "unknown error," and tells you to restart.
After the restart, the problem isn't fixed, but it isn't worse either, so you learn to live with it.

Every now and then it just runs smoother and you have no idea why. You whisper "this is actually pretty nice right now" — and then you absolutely do not touch a single setting.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner you're still 'working things out' with, plus 'we're at like ninety-nine percent right now, please do not restart at this exact moment'
variations: Upgrade: year one was a major version update — everything overturned, relearn it all. Then it became patches; every fight was just fixing a bug. Lately we hit the 'stable release' — no updates in ages, but no crashes either, which is probably the highest form a long relationship can reach.
Short version: a good relationship isn't bug-free — it's two people too lazy to switch systems.

items.sourceNote: 情侶一起做 DIY 變吵架現場，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
我們決定一起組那個有名的瑞典書櫃。

說明書上有十二個步驟。
第三步，我們對「A 板」是哪一塊產生了根本性的分歧。
第五步，多出一根螺絲，兩個人對視，誰都不敢承認那根應該裝哪。
第八步，已經沒有人在看說明書了，純粹靠意志力跟憤怒在硬撐。

書櫃最後是組好了。
它有點歪，門關不太緊，但它站著。
就像我們的感情——結構上不太合理，但它，站著。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個跟你一起組過家具還活下來的另一半，附一句「下次再買 DIY 家具，我們先簽離婚協議書放旁邊備用」
variations: 升級版：那根多出來的螺絲我們留了下來，放在抽屜裡。每次打開抽屜看到它，都會想起那天差點為了一個書櫃分手。它現在是我們的紀念品，提醒我們：有些東西，少一顆螺絲也能撐住。
短版：想測試一段感情夠不夠穩，不用算命，買一套需要兩個人組的家具就知道了。

en.content:
We decided to assemble that famous Swedish bookshelf together.

The manual had twelve steps.
By step three, we had a fundamental disagreement about which piece was "panel A."
By step five, there was one extra screw, and we just stared at each other, neither willing to admit where it was supposed to go.
By step eight, nobody was reading the manual anymore — we were running purely on willpower and resentment.

The bookshelf did get built.
It leans a little, the door doesn't shut all the way, but it stands.
Just like our relationship — structurally questionable, but standing.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who survived building furniture with you, plus 'next time we buy flat-pack, we sign the divorce papers first and keep them handy'
variations: Upgrade: we kept that extra screw in a drawer. Every time I open it and see it, I remember the day we almost broke up over a bookshelf. It's our souvenir now — a reminder that some things hold up even when they're a screw short.
Short version: want to test how solid a relationship is? Skip the fortune teller. Buy furniture that takes two people to assemble.

items.sourceNote: 情侶搶棉被的冷戰，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
交往前我以為兩個人蓋一條棉被是浪漫的象徵。

交往後我才知道，那是一場每晚開打、沒有贏家、沒有停戰協議的領土戰爭。

半夜三點我醒來，發現自己只剩肩膀露在外面，棉被全在對方那側捲成一個繭。
而那個人睡得像個天使，臉上還帶著微笑。

我看著他，心裡想：我這麼愛你，到底是哪裡出了問題。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給每晚跟你搶棉被的那個人，附一句「今晚開始一人一條，這是不可協商的和平方案」——通常會被已讀，然後當晚照搶不誤
variations: 升級版：後來我們真的買了兩條棉被。結果半夜醒來，發現兩條都在他那邊。問題從來不是棉被的數量。
短版：想知道一個人睡相多差，不用問，跟他蓋同一條棉被一個禮拜就懂了。

en.content:
Before we got together, I thought sharing one blanket was a romantic gesture.

After we got together, I learned it's a nightly territorial war with no winner, no loser, and absolutely no ceasefire.

At three in the morning I wake up with only my shoulder exposed, the entire blanket cocooned around the other side of the bed.
And that person is sleeping like an angel, a little smile on their face.

I look at them and think: I love you this much, so where exactly did it all go wrong?
en.editorNote: Send to whoever steals the blanket every night, with 'starting tonight we each get our own — this peace plan is non-negotiable.' Expect read receipt, zero behavior change.
variations: Upgrade: we actually bought two blankets. I woke up at 3 a.m. and found both of them on their side. The problem was never the number of blankets.
Short version: want to know how badly someone sleeps? Don't ask. Share one blanket with them for a week.

items.sourceNote: 情侶「你最愛的通知」哏，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
我手機裡所有 App 的通知我都關了。

群組訊息：靜音。
限時動態：靜音。
那個一直叫我領獎勵的遊戲：直接刪了。

全世界都安靜了。

只有一個人傳訊息進來時，我的手機還是會亮、會震、會發出那個聲音。

不是我不會關，是我捨不得關。
你是我唯一沒有靜音的人。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合在對方抱怨「你都不回我訊息」的時候傳這段過去——堵嘴神器，附一句「全世界我只留你一個提示音」
variations: 反向版：他說「那你怎麼還是常常不回我？」——因為亮歸亮，我看到的時候在忙，等忙完想回，已經過了三小時，這時候回顯得我很冷淡，乾脆假裝沒看到，明天再裝作剛看到。情侶之間的訊息往來，有時候比國際談判還複雜。
短版：通知全部關掉，只留一個人。這不是科技，這是愛。

en.content:
I've turned off notifications for every app on my phone.

Group chats: muted.
Stories: muted.
That game that keeps nagging me to claim my reward: deleted.

The whole world went quiet.

There's only one person whose message still makes my phone light up, buzz, and make that little sound.

It's not that I can't mute it. It's that I won't.
You're the only notification I never silenced.
en.editorNote: Perfect comeback when they complain 'you never reply to me' — send this with 'out of the entire world I kept exactly one alert sound, and it's you.'
variations: Reverse version: he says 'then why do you still leave me on read?' — because it lights up, sure, but I'm busy when I see it, and by the time I'm free three hours have passed, and replying now makes me look cold, so I pretend I didn't see it and 'just now' notice it tomorrow. Couple texting is more complicated than international diplomacy.
Short version: muted every app, kept one person. That's not technology. That's love.

items.sourceNote: 情侶共用冰箱的信任考驗，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
共用一個冰箱，是一段感情裡最高等級的信任考驗。

我昨天特地留了一塊蛋糕在冰箱，貼了便利貼寫「這是我的，不要吃」。

今天打開冰箱，蛋糕沒了。
便利貼還在，背面多了一行字：「對不起，我以為那是在考驗我的自制力。我失敗了。」

我沒有生氣。
因為我發現他連道歉都寫在同一張便利貼上，捨不得多撕一張。
這個人，連道歉都這麼省。我還能怎麼辦。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個會偷吃你冰箱食物的另一半，附一句「下次再偷吃，便利貼換成監視器」——通常隔天蛋糕還是會不見
variations: 升級版：後來我學乖了，把蛋糕藏在燙青菜下面。三天後打開，蛋糕還在，但燙青菜也不見了。我低估了這個人。
短版：想知道一個人愛不愛你？把最後一塊蛋糕放冰箱，看他吃了之後敢不敢承認。

en.content:
Sharing a fridge is the highest level of trust test a relationship can offer.

Yesterday I deliberately saved a slice of cake in the fridge with a sticky note: "This is MINE. Do not eat."

Today I opened the fridge. The cake was gone.
The note was still there, with a line added on the back: "Sorry, I thought it was a test of my self-control. I failed."

I wasn't angry.
Because I noticed he wrote the apology on the same sticky note, too thrifty to tear off a fresh one.
This person economizes even on apologies. What am I supposed to do with that.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who raids your fridge, with 'next time you steal my food the sticky note becomes a security camera.' The cake will still vanish tomorrow.
variations: Upgrade: I got smart and hid the cake under the steamed vegetables. Three days later the cake was still there — but the vegetables were gone. I underestimated this person.
Short version: want to know if someone loves you? Leave the last slice of cake in the fridge and see whether they'll admit to eating it.

items.sourceNote: 情侶調冷氣溫度像外交談判，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
我們對冷氣溫度的協商，比任何一場國際高峰會都還要艱難。

我覺得 24 度剛好。
他覺得 24 度是要逼死人。

於是我們發明了一套外交制度：
他偷偷調到 27，我半夜偷偷調回 25，他早上起來再調到 26，我們在 26 度達成了脆弱的停火協議。

沒有人真正滿意。
但這就是和平——兩邊都覺得自己吃虧，才能撐得久。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個跟你搶冷氣遙控器的另一半，附一句「26 度是我們的耶路撒冷，誰都不准再動」——適合夏天每天上演的溫度大戰
variations: 升級版：後來我們各買了一條薄被跟一台小電扇，冷氣設一個彼此都不爽但能接受的溫度。成熟的感情不是找到完美的溫度，是學會在不完美的溫度裡，各自帶好自己的裝備。
短版：情侶之間最難的談判，不是要不要結婚，是冷氣要開幾度。

en.content:
Our negotiations over the AC temperature are harder than any international summit.

I think 24 is perfect.
He thinks 24 is attempted murder.

So we invented a whole diplomatic system:
He secretly bumps it to 27, I sneak it back to 25 at midnight, he resets it to 26 in the morning, and at 26 degrees we reach a fragile ceasefire.

Nobody is actually satisfied.
But that's peace — it only lasts when both sides feel they got the short end.
en.editorNote: Send to whoever fights you for the AC remote, with '26 is our Jerusalem, nobody touches it again.' For the temperature war that plays out every summer night.
variations: Upgrade: eventually we each bought a thin blanket and a little fan and set the AC to a temperature we both dislike but can tolerate. A mature relationship isn't finding the perfect temperature — it's learning to bring your own gear to an imperfect one.
Short version: the hardest negotiation between a couple isn't whether to get married. It's what to set the thermostat to.

items.sourceNote: 同居後發現對方的奇怪睡覺習慣，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
同居之後我才發現，他睡覺會說夢話。

但他的夢話毫無邏輯。
上禮拜他半夜突然坐起來，很嚴肅地說：「那隻鴨子不應該坐在那裡。」然後就倒回去繼續睡。

我問他在夢什麼，他完全不記得。

從那天起，每次他講夢話我都會錄下來。
我現在有一個資料夾，裡面全是他在睡夢中對這個世界發表的重大聲明。
這是我最珍貴的收藏。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個會講夢話的另一半，附一句「你昨晚又對宇宙發表演說了，要不要聽錄音」——適合同居情侶互相爆料
variations: 反向版：結果有一天他放給我聽，說我也會講夢話，而且我講的是「不要按那個按鈕」，連續三天。我們到現在都不知道那是什麼按鈕。
短版：真正了解一個人，不是看他清醒時說什麼，是看他睡著後對著空氣說什麼。

en.content:
After we moved in together, I discovered he talks in his sleep.

But his sleep-talk makes zero sense.
Last week he suddenly sat up in the middle of the night and said, very seriously, "That duck should not be sitting there." Then he flopped back down and kept sleeping.

I asked what he was dreaming about. He had no memory of it.

Since that night, I record him every time he talks in his sleep.
I now have a folder full of the major statements he's made to the world while unconscious.
It's my most treasured collection.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who talks in their sleep, with 'you addressed the universe again last night, want to hear the recording?' For couples living together who love exposing each other.
variations: Reverse version: one day he played it back and told me I talk in my sleep too — and what I say is 'don't press that button,' three nights in a row. To this day neither of us knows what button.
Short version: you don't truly know someone by what they say awake. You know them by what they say to thin air after they fall asleep.

items.sourceNote: 另一半走進玻璃門留下鼻印的梗，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
我男友剛剛直接撞上我們家的玻璃拉門。

發出一聲悶響，整片門都在晃。

我衝過去，第一句話不是「你還好嗎」。
是「你有沒有在上面留鼻印，我今天早上才擦過。」

他摀著鼻子看著我，眼神受傷。
我蹲下來檢查玻璃。
上面真的有一個。

我們現在還在冷戰，但不是因為他覺得我不關心他。
是因為我堅持要他自己擦掉。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個在家裡橫衝直撞的另一半，附一句「下次撞之前先看路，玻璃我擦的」——適合走過熱戀期、開始斤斤計較家事的情侶
variations: 反向版：後來換我撞到，他默默走過來，遞給我一張衛生紙——擦玻璃用的。我才知道報應來得這麼快。
短版：愛一個人，是擔心他撞痛了；在一起久了，是擔心他把你剛擦好的玻璃弄髒了。兩種都是愛，只是第二種比較真實。

en.content:
My boyfriend just walked straight into our glass sliding door.

A loud thud. The whole panel shook.

I rushed over, and my first words were not "are you okay."
They were "did you leave a nose print, I literally just cleaned that this morning."

He stood there holding his nose, looking wounded.
I crouched down to inspect the glass.
There was, in fact, one.

We're still not speaking, but not because he thinks I don't care about him.
It's because I'm insisting he wipe it off himself.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who keeps bumping into things at home, with 'look where you're going next time, I'm the one who cleans that glass.' For couples past the honeymoon phase who now keep score on chores.
variations: Reverse version: later I was the one who walked into it. He quietly came over and handed me a tissue — for the glass. That's when I learned karma is fast.
Short version: loving someone is worrying they got hurt. Being together a while is worrying they smudged the glass you just cleaned. Both are love. The second one is just more honest.

items.sourceNote: 交往太久連同一個笑話都能笑無數次的梗，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
長久關係的祕訣，其實很簡單。

就是同一個爛笑話，你要有辦法陪他笑三百八十二萬次。

他每次經過廚房看到香蕉，都會說同一句冷到結冰的諧音梗。
第一年我覺得好可愛。
第三年我覺得還好。
第五年我已經能在他開口前接出下一句。

上禮拜他又走進廚房，看到香蕉，張開嘴——
我搶先把整句講完了。
他愣了一下，然後笑得比平常都還大聲。

原來他要的從來不是新笑話。
是知道有一個人，把他講過的每句廢話都記得這麼牢。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個老是講同一個爛笑話的另一半，附一句「下一句我幫你講」——適合在一起很久、已經能背出彼此口頭禪的情侶
variations: 反向版：後來我發現我也有一句重複講了五年的話，是每次他洗碗我都會說「你今天好帥」。他說他從第二年就聽膩了，但還是每次都默默多洗一個鍋。
短版：浪漫不是每天都有新鮮事，是同一件無聊的事，你願意陪他重複一輩子還笑得出來。

en.content:
The secret to a lasting relationship is actually pretty simple.

You just need to be able to laugh at the same terrible joke three million, eight hundred and twenty thousand times.

Every time he walks past the kitchen and sees a banana, he makes the same ice-cold pun.
Year one, I thought it was adorable.
Year three, I thought it was fine.
Year five, I can finish the punchline before he opens his mouth.

Last week he walked into the kitchen, saw the banana, opened his mouth —
and I beat him to the whole line.
He froze for a second, then laughed louder than he ever has.

Turns out he never needed a new joke.
He needed to know there's one person who remembers every dumb thing he's ever said.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who keeps telling the same terrible joke, with 'I'll say the next line for you.' For couples who've been together so long they have each other's catchphrases memorized.
variations: Reverse version: later I realized I have a line I've repeated for five years too — every time he does the dishes I say 'you look so handsome today.' He says he got sick of it in year two, but he still quietly washes one extra pot every time.
Short version: romance isn't something new every day. It's being willing to repeat the same boring thing with someone for a lifetime and still laughing.

items.sourceNote: 另一半偷偷在Netflix幫對方按讚無聊紀錄片報復的梗，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
我女友生我氣的時候，不會跟我吵。

她會打開我的串流帳號，幫我按讚一堆超無聊的紀錄片。

所以現在我的首頁推薦，全部都是「十六世紀荷蘭排水系統的演進」、「鹽的全球貿易史下集」。

我問她為什麼這樣，她說：「我沒有啊。」然後繼續吃她的洋芋片。

我們和好的方式也很特別。
是她願意點進那些紀錄片，把它們從我的觀看紀錄裡看掉。

那一刻我就知道，她氣消了。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個生氣會搞小破壞的另一半，附一句「我首頁那些紀錄片是不是你」——適合用幼稚方式互相報復、但其實很可愛的情侶
variations: 反向版：後來我學會了，吵架後直接打開她的帳號，幫她追蹤一堆健身頻道。她現在每天首頁都是「三十天腹肌挑戰」，被罪惡感折磨。我們扯平了。
短版：成熟的情侶吵架不冷戰、不翻舊帳。他們默默破壞對方的演算法。

en.content:
When my girlfriend is mad at me, she doesn't argue.

She opens my streaming account and thumbs-up a pile of the most boring documentaries she can find.

So now my homepage recommendations are entirely "The Evolution of 16th-Century Dutch Drainage Systems" and "A Global History of Salt, Part Two."

I asked her why she does this. She said, "I don't." And kept eating her chips.

The way we make up is special too.
It's when she's willing to actually click into those documentaries and watch them out of my history.

That's the moment I know the anger's gone.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who commits petty sabotage when angry, with 'are those documentaries on my homepage your doing?' For couples who get revenge in childish ways but are secretly adorable about it.
variations: Reverse version: eventually I learned — after a fight, I just open her account and follow a bunch of fitness channels. Now her homepage is all '30-day ab challenge,' and the guilt eats at her. We're even.
Short version: mature couples don't give the silent treatment or bring up old grudges. They quietly poison each other's algorithm.

items.sourceNote: 交往後吃雞翅留肉的反差梗，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
剛交往的時候，他吃雞翅都吃得很秀氣。

啃一啃就放下，骨頭上還留著一半的肉。
那時候我覺得：好斯文喔，好有教養喔。

交往三年後的某一天，我看著他丟下的骨頭，整個人停住了。

那上面，連一條筋都沒有。
啃得乾乾淨淨，像被某種小型猛獸處理過。

我問他什麼時候變這樣的。
他說：「在一起久了，我才發現那上面還有差不多十塊錢的肉，丟掉很可惜。」

這就是愛情。
它讓你卸下偽裝，露出最真實的、最節省的那一面。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個交往後原形畢露的另一半，附一句「你以前吃雞翅明明很秀氣」——適合一起吃過無數頓飯、早就不裝了的情侶
variations: 反向版：後來換我被他發現，我以前說「我吃不多」，現在我們點餐都直接幫我多加一份。他什麼都沒說，只是默默把菜單往我這邊推。
短版：交往初期吃相是給對方看的，交往久了吃相是給食物看的——那骨頭上的每一塊肉都不該被辜負。

en.content:
When we first started dating, he ate chicken wings so delicately.

A few nibbles and he'd set it down, half the meat still on the bone.
Back then I thought: how refined, how well-mannered.

Then one day, three years in, I looked at the bones he'd dropped and froze.

There wasn't a single shred left.
Picked clean, like they'd been processed by some small, efficient predator.

I asked when he changed.
He said, "After being together a while, I realized there's like a dollar-seventy-five of meat still on those things. It's a waste to throw it out."

This is love.
It lets you drop the act and reveal your truest, most frugal self.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who showed their true colors after you got together, with 'you used to eat wings so daintily.' For couples who've shared countless meals and stopped pretending long ago.
variations: Reverse version: later he caught me too — I used to say 'I don't eat much,' and now we just automatically add an extra order for me. He never said a word. He just quietly slides the menu my way.
Short version: early on, your table manners are for your partner. Later, they're for the food — and not one shred of meat on that bone deserves to be wasted.

items.sourceNote: 找到一個一輩子想煩的人這個梗，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
小時候大人都說，要找一個能聊一輩子的人。

後來我才發現，這句話是騙人的。

真正的關係不是「能聊一輩子」，是「能煩一輩子」。

我每天都有一堆無聊到不行的事想跟她講：剛剛那隻貓走路的樣子、我突然想到的一個冷笑話、便利商店出了新口味的飯糰。

這些事跟任何人講都會被已讀，只有她，會回我一句「然後呢」，然後繼續聽我把這件完全沒重點的事講完。

能找到一個願意被你煩一輩子的人，比能聊一輩子難多了。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個被你用各種無聊小事轟炸、卻從來沒嫌過你煩的另一半——願意聽你講廢話的人，才是真的把你放在心上
variations: 反向版：後來我發現她也一樣。半夜傳訊息問我「你睡了嗎」，我說睡了，她說「喔好那沒事」——其實根本沒事，她只是想煩我一下確認我還在。
短版：愛情的最高境界不是無話不談，是有一堆廢話只想對一個人說。

en.content:
Growing up, adults always said: find someone you can talk to for a lifetime.

Turns out that's a lie.

Real love isn't finding someone you can talk to forever. It's finding someone you can annoy forever.

Every day I've got a pile of utterly pointless things I want to tell her: the weird way that cat just walked, a terrible pun that popped into my head, the new rice ball flavor at the convenience store.

Tell any of that to anyone else and you get left on read. But her — she'll reply 'and then?' and let me finish the whole nothing-story.

Finding someone willing to be annoyed by you for life is much harder than finding someone to talk to.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner you bombard with pointless little updates who's never once called you annoying — the person who listens to your nonsense is the one who actually keeps you in mind.
variations: Reverse version: turns out she's the same. Texts me 'you asleep?' at midnight, I say yeah, she says 'oh ok nothing then' — there was never anything. She just wanted to bug me and confirm I'm still there.
Short version: the peak of love isn't having everything to say. It's having a pile of nonsense you only want to say to one person.

items.sourceNote: 情侶吵架誰才是對的、誰先睡這個梗，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
情侶吵架，從來不是在吵「誰對」。

是在吵「誰要先承認對方對」。

我們可以為了一件三天後誰都記不得的小事，背對背躺在同一張床上，假裝睡著，呼吸都裝得很均勻。

中間隔著一條三八線，誰的腳都不准越界。

然後過了二十分鐘，黑暗中有一隻手悄悄伸過來，勾住對方的小指。

沒有人說話。
但戰爭就這樣結束了。

我們從來不是真的想贏，我們只是都不想先輸而已。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個跟你冷戰到一半、會默默把手伸過來的另一半——吵架時嘴硬，睡覺時手軟，這就是大部分情侶的真相
variations: 反向版：後來我們發明了一個規則：誰先笑誰就輸。結果每次背對背躺三分鐘，就有一個人開始忍不住抖肩膀，然後兩個人一起笑出來。我們再也沒有真正吵贏過。
短版：成熟情侶吵架的結局只有兩種：一種是有人先伸手，一種是兩個人都餓了，然後決定先去吃個宵夜。

en.content:
Couples never actually fight about who's right.

They fight about who has to admit the other one is right first.

We can lie back to back in the same bed over something neither of us will remember in three days, both pretending to be asleep, breathing in suspiciously even rhythms.

There's an invisible line down the middle of the mattress. No feet allowed across.

Then twenty minutes later, in the dark, a hand quietly reaches over and hooks one pinky around the other.

Nobody says a word.
But that's how the war ends.

We never actually wanted to win. We just both didn't want to lose first.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who quietly reaches a hand over mid-standoff — stubborn lips, soft hands at bedtime, that's the truth about most couples.
variations: Reverse version: eventually we made a rule — first one to laugh loses. Now we lie back to back for three minutes before someone's shoulders start shaking, and we both crack up. We've never actually won an argument since.
Short version: a mature couple's fight ends one of two ways — someone reaches over first, or you both get hungry and decide to go grab a midnight snack.

items.sourceNote: 「你想吃什麼」之外另一個世紀難題——搶被子，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
結婚誓詞應該誠實一點。

不要再說什麼「無論貧窮或富有、健康或疾病」。

應該改成：「我願意跟你共度餘生，並且在接下來的每一個晚上，跟你爭奪同一條被子。」

我每天半夜都會經歷一場無聲的拔河。

我醒來的時候，被子永遠在她那邊，捲成一個密不透風的春捲，而我露在外面，像一隻被遺棄的蝦。

我問她怎麼每次都這樣。
她說：「我睡著了我哪知道。」

睡著的人永遠是無辜的。
清醒的人永遠在挨凍。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個半夜會把被子捲走、白天還一臉無辜的另一半——適合每天早上醒來發現自己被晾在被子外面的人
variations: 反向版：後來我買了兩條被子，一人一條，以為從此天下太平。結果第二天早上，我發現我抱著我那條，她也抱著我那條。她有兩條，我有零條。
短版：判斷你們是不是真愛很簡單——就看半夜搶被子搶輸的那個，會不會默默再去櫃子裡拿一條，而不是把對方挖起來。

en.content:
Wedding vows should be more honest.

Drop the whole 'for richer or poorer, in sickness and in health.'

Replace it with: 'I take you to spend the rest of my life with, and to fight you for the same blanket every single night from here on out.'

Every night I'm in a silent tug-of-war.

I wake up and the blanket is always on her side, wound into an airtight burrito, while I'm left out in the open like an abandoned shrimp.

I ask how this happens every time.
She says, 'I was asleep, how would I know.'

The sleeping party is always innocent.
The awake party is always freezing.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who steals the blanket at 3am and plays innocent by morning — for anyone who wakes up stranded outside the covers every single day.
variations: Reverse version: so I bought two blankets, one each, figuring peace at last. Next morning I find I'm clutching mine, and she's clutching mine too. She has two. I have zero.
Short version: easy way to tell if it's true love — when the one who loses the 3am blanket war just quietly grabs another from the closet instead of waking the other person up.

items.sourceNote: 情侶要不要出門的拖延梗，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
「好，我們出門。」

這句話對一般人來說，意思是「我們現在要出門了」。

對情侶來說，這句話是一場儀式的開始。

說完之後，會有一個人想起來要上廁所，另一個人趁機又躺回沙發滑了一下手機。

上完廁所那個人問：「咦你怎麼還沒穿鞋？」
躺回去那個人說：「我等你啊。」

然後其中一個人突然想到忘記帶充電線，回房間找。
另一個人說「那我順便去喝個水」。

從說「好我們出門」到真正踏出大門，平均耗時十七分鐘。
科學家至今無法解釋這段時間到底發生了什麼。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個每次說要出門、卻還能再拖二十分鐘的另一半——適合永遠遲到、但兩個人都有責任的情侶
variations: 反向版：後來我們學乖了，約定「說出門就是出門」。結果第一次實施，兩個人都準時站在門口，互看一眼，然後同時說：「等等，我好像沒關電鍋。」
短版：情侶之間最長的距離，不是異地，是從「好我們出門」到真的走出那扇門。

en.content:
"Okay, let's go."

To a normal person, this sentence means 'we are leaving now.'

To a couple, it's the opening ceremony.

The moment it's said, one person remembers they need the bathroom, and the other takes the chance to flop back onto the couch and scroll for 'just a sec.'

The bathroom person comes back: 'Wait, why don't you have your shoes on?'
Couch person: 'I was waiting for you.'

Then one of them suddenly remembers they forgot the charging cable and goes back to the bedroom.
The other says, 'I'll just grab some water while you're at it.'

From 'okay, let's go' to actually stepping out the door: an average of seventeen minutes.
Scientists still cannot explain what happens during that time.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who says 'let's go' and then somehow stretches it another twenty minutes — for couples who are always late, but it's equally both their fault.
variations: Reverse version: eventually we wised up and agreed 'let's go means we go.' First time we tried it, we both stood at the door right on time, looked at each other, and said in unison: 'Wait, did I unplug the rice cooker?'
Short version: the longest distance between two people in love isn't long-distance. It's the gap between 'okay, let's go' and actually walking out the door.

items.sourceNote: 情侶洗碗機擺法之爭，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
一段感情裡，通常會有一個人有一套洗碗機擺放系統，另一個人只想看世界燃燒。

有系統的那位：碗盤要分類、刀叉尖端朝下、杯子斜放固定角度，連洗潔劑都要按瓶身指示量。

另一位：把鍋子卡進去，能關起來就是贏。

第一位忍不住重排了一次。
第二位看著被重排的成果說：「你看，我放的也能洗乾淨啊。」
第一位深吸一口氣，沒有回話。

這場戰爭沒有勝負，只有一個天天默默重排碗盤的人，跟一個永遠不知道自己放錯的人。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個會偷偷重排碗盤的另一半，或那個被重排還渾然不覺的——通常一傳出去，對方就知道自己是哪一種了
variations: 進階版：有系統的那位終於放手不管，告訴自己「能洗乾淨就好」。結果三天後，他半夜爬起來重排，邊排邊說：「我只是睡不著。」
短版：洗碗機是測試一段感情能不能長久的終極考驗——不是看你們合不合，是看誰先學會閉嘴。

en.content:
In every relationship, one person has a dishwasher-loading system. The other just wants to watch the world burn.

The system person: plates sorted by size, forks tines-down, cups at a fixed angle, detergent measured per the bottle's instructions.

The other person: jams the pot in, and if the door closes, that's a win.

The first one quietly re-loads the whole thing.
The second one looks at the redone work and says, 'See? Mine would've washed fine too.'
The first one takes a deep breath and says nothing.

There's no winner in this war. Just one person silently re-loading dishes every day, and one person who will never know they did it wrong.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who secretly re-loads the dishwasher — or the one who gets re-loaded and never notices. Either way, they'll know which one they are.
variations: Advanced version: the system person finally lets go and tells themselves 'as long as it gets clean.' Three days later they're up at 2am re-loading it, muttering, 'I just couldn't sleep.'
Short version: the dishwasher is the ultimate test of whether a relationship lasts — not whether you're compatible, but who learns to shut up first.

items.sourceNote: 情侶冷氣溫度外交戰，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
情侶為了冷氣溫度的攻防，比外交談判還精密。

你調到二十四度。
半小時後，遙控器顯示二十七度。
你不動聲色調回二十五度，當作各退一步。
再半小時，二十七度。

你終於問：「你是不是一直在偷調？」
對方蓋著兩條毯子，理直氣壯：「我冷啊。」
「那你穿件外套不就好了？」
「為什麼要我穿外套，你不能蓋毯子嗎？」

這場談判沒有共識，只有一個流汗的人跟一個裹成蠶蛹的人，在同一個房間裡，假裝對方不存在。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個總是嫌冷／嫌熱的另一半——夏天最容易引戰，附上一句「這就是我們」保證引發討論
variations: 和解版：後來我們買了兩條不同厚度的毯子跟一支小電風扇，各取所需。現在唯一的問題只剩——電費單來的時候，誰也不敢先講話。
短版：愛情能跨越很多差異，但跨不過二十四度跟二十七度之間那神聖的三度。

en.content:
Couples negotiate the thermostat with more precision than diplomats.

You set it to 75.
Thirty minutes later, the display reads 81.
You quietly nudge it back to 77 — a fair compromise, you tell yourself.
Thirty minutes later: 81.

You finally ask, 'Have you been changing it this whole time?'
The other person, buried under two blankets, replies without shame: 'I'm cold.'
'Then put on a sweater?'
'Why should I put on a sweater? Can't you just grab a blanket?'

No treaty is reached. Just one person sweating and one person cocooned in fleece, sharing a room and pretending the other doesn't exist.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who's always too hot or too cold — summer is peak conflict season. Add 'this is us' and watch the debate begin.
variations: Peace-treaty version: we eventually bought two blankets of different thickness and a small fan. Everyone gets what they need. The only remaining issue is who speaks first when the electric bill arrives.
Short version: love can bridge a lot of differences, but not the sacred three degrees between 'comfortable' and 'I'm freezing.'

items.sourceNote: 情侶『要不要買什麼』訊息日常，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
情侶到後來，最常傳的訊息只剩一句：「回家路上要不要買什麼？」

「不用，家裡都有。」
（回家後）
「欸怎麼沒有牛奶？」
「就是要你買啊。」
「你剛剛說都有。」
「我以為你會自己想到。」

於是隔天的訊息變成：「要不要買什麼？認真問，我真的會買。」
「……衛生紙吧。」
「家裡不是還有？」
「快沒了。」
「快沒是還有多少？」
「……你買就對了。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個總是說「不用買」結果回家又缺東西的另一半——同居情侶的日常神預言
variations: 進階版：後來我們學乖，建了一個共用購物清單。現在的問題是，兩個人都在等對方把東西加進清單，結果清單永遠是空的，家裡永遠缺東西。
短版：所謂同居，就是兩個人輪流問「要不要買什麼」，再輪流忘記到底要買什麼，直到其中一個人退休為止。

en.content:
Eventually the most common text between a couple is just: 'Need anything on my way home?'

'Nope, we've got everything.'
(arrives home)
'Hey, why's there no milk?'
'That's what I was asking you to buy.'
'You literally said we had everything.'
'I figured you'd think of it yourself.'

So the next day's text becomes: 'Need anything? Seriously, I'll actually buy it.'
'...Toilet paper, I guess.'
'Don't we still have some?'
'Running low.'
'How low is low?'
'...Just buy it.'
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who always says 'we don't need anything' and then comes home to find the fridge empty — peak cohabitation prophecy.
variations: Advanced version: we got smart and made a shared shopping list. Now the problem is we're both waiting for the other to add things, so the list stays empty and the house stays out of everything.
Short version: living together is two people taking turns asking 'need anything from the store?' and taking turns forgetting what it was — until one of you retires.

items.sourceNote: 情侶共用密碼信任比喻，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
我們這段感情，是建立在愛……跟共用密碼之上的。

手機密碼、串流平台帳號、外送 App、連超商集點都是同一組登入。

剛開始我覺得這就是信任的最高境界。
直到有一天，我打開追劇平台，發現「繼續觀看」那排全是我沒看過的劇，進度還都到第八集。

「你偷看？」
「不是偷看，是我先看了，怕劇透才沒跟你說。」
「那我們不是說好要一起看？」
「……我會陪你重看啊。」

所謂共用密碼，就是把信任跟背叛，放在同一個帳號裡。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個偷跑進度的另一半——共用串流帳號的情侶看了會心一笑，順便查一下對方的觀看紀錄
variations: 進階版：後來我們約定「偷跑進度者，負責晚餐一週」。第一週是他，第二週還是他，第三週他乾脆說：「我請一個月，但我要看完整季。」
短版：情侶之間最大的背叛，不是看別人，是把說好要一起追的劇，偷偷看完。

en.content:
Our relationship is built on love… and shared passwords.

Phone passcode, streaming accounts, the food-delivery app — even the convenience-store rewards login is the same one.

At first I thought this was the highest form of trust.
Then one day I opened our streaming app and the entire 'Continue Watching' row was full of shows I'd never seen — all the way to episode eight.

'You watched ahead?'
'I didn't watch ahead, I just watched first, and I didn't tell you because spoilers.'
'We agreed to watch it together.'
'...I'll rewatch it with you.'

A shared password is just trust and betrayal living in the same account.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who watched ahead — couples sharing a streaming login will smile knowingly, then go check the watch history.
variations: Advanced version: we made a rule — 'whoever watches ahead cooks dinner for a week.' Week one was him. Week two was still him. Week three he just said, 'I'll cook for a month, but I'm finishing the whole season.'
Short version: the biggest betrayal between a couple isn't looking at someone else — it's secretly finishing the show you swore you'd watch together.

items.sourceNote: 情侶感情比喻成 Wi-Fi，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
有人說我們的感情像 Wi-Fi。

我本來覺得是稱讚，後來想想——還真的滿準的。

訊號好的時候，整個人黏在一起，下載速度飛快，連對方在想什麼都自動同步。
訊號弱的時候，明明坐在隔壁，回個訊息要轉圈圈轉三分鐘。

但最可怕的不是這個。
最可怕的是——斷線的那一秒，我才發現我有多依賴它。

所以拜託，吵架可以，但不要直接登出。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個一冷戰就已讀不回的另一半——用 Wi-Fi 比喻把「我其實很依賴你」講得不肉麻，剛吵完架傳特別有效
variations: 進階版：後來我把他設成「自動連線、永不忘記這個網路」。他說這樣很沒安全感，我說這叫忠誠。
短版：我們的感情像 Wi-Fi，平常嫌它慢，斷掉才知道活不下去。

en.content:
Someone said our relationship is like Wi-Fi.

I thought it was a compliment. Then I thought about it — and honestly, it's painfully accurate.

When the signal's strong, we're glued together, downloading at full speed, syncing thoughts before either of us speaks.
When the signal's weak, we're sitting right next to each other and a single text takes three minutes of that little spinning circle.

But that's not even the scary part.
The scary part is — the second it drops, I realize how much I depend on it.

So please. We can fight. Just don't log out on me.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who goes 'read, no reply' the moment things get cold — the Wi-Fi metaphor lets you admit 'I depend on you' without sounding sappy. Hits hardest right after a fight.
variations: Advanced version: I set him to 'auto-connect, never forget this network.' He said that's clingy. I said it's loyalty.
Short version: our love is like Wi-Fi — we complain it's slow until it cuts out and we can't function.

items.sourceNote: 情侶用表情吵架的沉默冷戰，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
我知道我們的感情是來真的，是從哪一刻開始的？

是從我們學會「用表情吵架」那一刻。

一句話都不用講。
他挑一下眉，我翻一個白眼，他嘆一口氣，我「哼」一聲把頭轉過去。
全程沒有任何聲音，但客廳的空氣已經吵到第三回合了。

隔壁鄰居以為我們在看默劇。
其實我們在進行一場——不需要字幕，全世界只有我們兩個看得懂的冷戰。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個跟你培養出「沉默吵架默契」的另一半——把冷戰寫得好笑又有愛，適合冷戰結束後當破冰用
variations: 進階版：最後通常是肚子餓的那個先投降。上禮拜是他，他邊夾菜邊說：「我不是認輸，我是不想餓死在道德高地上。」
短版：我們吵架不用開口，一個眼神就能讓對方知道——今晚棉被是你的了。

en.content:
You want to know the moment I knew our relationship was the real deal?

It's the moment we learned to argue using only our faces.

Not a single word.
He raises an eyebrow, I roll my eyes, he sighs, I do a little 'hmph' and turn my head away.
Dead silent — but the living room air is already three rounds deep into a fight.

The neighbors think we're rehearsing a mime show.
Really, we're having a cold war that needs no subtitles — one only the two of us in the entire world can follow.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner you've developed a 'silent argument' rhythm with — frames the cold war as funny and affectionate. Great as an ice-breaker once the standoff is over.
variations: Advanced version: whoever gets hungry first usually surrenders. Last week it was him — mid-bite he goes, 'I'm not conceding, I just don't want to starve to death on the moral high ground.'
Short version: we don't need to speak to fight. One look tells you — you're getting the blanket tonight, buddy.

items.sourceNote: 情侶散步前先吵要去哪散步，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
我提議：「我們去散個步吧，浪漫一下。」
他眼睛一亮：「好啊。」

然後我們就站在玄關，開始討論要往哪邊散。

「往河堤？」「太遠。」
「那繞公園？」「上次去過了。」
「巷口便利商店？」「那叫買宵夜不叫散步。」

二十分鐘後，鞋都還沒穿，浪漫的氣氛已經散完了。

後來我們達成共識：所謂情侶，就是兩個人花一小時討論去哪，最後決定——還是在家躺著比較浪漫。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個跟你「光是決定去哪就能耗掉一個晚上」的另一半——把選擇困難寫成情侶日常的默契，又好笑又中肯
variations: 進階版：現在我們發明了猜拳決定法。輸的人不用負責去哪，輸的人負責——閉嘴跟著走。
短版：情侶就是兩個人，花一小時討論去哪散步，最後決定不去了。

en.content:
I suggested: 'Let's go for a walk. Be a little romantic.'
His eyes lit up: 'Sure.'

Then we just stood in the entryway, debating which way to walk.

'The riverside?' 'Too far.'
'Loop the park?' 'Did that last time.'
'The convenience store down the street?' 'That's a snack run, not a walk.'

Twenty minutes later, shoes still off, and the romance had fully evaporated.

Eventually we reached a verdict: being a couple is two people spending an hour deciding where to go, only to conclude — lying on the couch is actually the more romantic option.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner with whom 'just deciding where to go' can burn an entire evening — turns indecision into a relatable inside joke. Funny because it's true.
variations: Advanced version: now we settle it with rock-paper-scissors. The loser doesn't pick where to go — the loser just shuts up and follows.
Short version: a couple is two people who spend an hour debating where to walk, then decide not to go.

items.sourceNote: 情侶感情比喻成穿舊的拖鞋，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
交往滿五年那天，他很認真地對我說：「我覺得我們的感情，就像一雙穿很久的拖鞋。」

我整個臉垮下來。

他急忙補充：「不是你想的那樣！我是說——超合腳，閉著眼睛都找得到，穿上去就放鬆，全世界最舒服。」

我說：「那後半句呢？」
他停頓了一下：「……偶爾是有點味道，但我習慣了，而且我絕對不換。」

你知道嗎，這大概是我聽過最不浪漫、但最真實的告白。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給交往多年、早就過了曖昧期的老夫老妻——把「習慣彼此」寫成最高級的情話，紀念日或交往週年傳剛剛好
variations: 進階版：我後來回他：「那我是你左腳還是右腳？」他說：「你是兩隻，因為少一隻我就走不動。」
短版：在一起久了不是失去浪漫，是把浪漫穿成一雙合腳的拖鞋，舒服到捨不得脫。

en.content:
On our fifth anniversary, he looked at me very seriously and said: 'I think our relationship is like a well-worn pair of slippers.'

My whole face dropped.

He rushed to add: 'Not like that! I mean — perfectly broken in, I can find you with my eyes closed, you put me at ease, the comfiest thing in the world.'

I said: 'And the rest of that metaphor?'
He paused. '...Okay, occasionally a little smelly. But I'm used to it. And I'm never, ever replacing them.'

You know what? That's the least romantic and most honest confession I've ever heard.
en.editorNote: Send to the long-term partner you're well past the butterflies with — reframes 'being used to each other' as the highest form of love. Perfect for an anniversary.
variations: Advanced version: I shot back, 'So am I the left slipper or the right one?' He said, 'You're both — lose one and I can't walk.'
Short version: being together a long time isn't losing the romance — it's wearing the romance in like a pair of slippers too comfy to take off.

items.sourceNote: 情侶搶棉被、半夜冰腳，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
結婚前我以為，兩個人蓋一條被子，是世界上最浪漫的畫面。

結婚後我才知道，那其實是一場每晚準時開打、沒有裁判、沒有休戰的拔河比賽。

凌晨三點，我醒來，發現自己只剩下一個被角，他像捲春捲一樣把整條被子捲在身上，睡得無比安詳。

更可怕的是——就在我冷得發抖的時候，一雙冰到像剛從冷凍庫拿出來的腳，準確無誤地貼上了我的後背。

結婚，就是學會在零度的腳掌和搶不到的被子之間，依然覺得這個人很值得。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個每晚跟你搶棉被、還用冰腳偷襲你的另一半——把睡覺戰爭寫成又氣又愛的日常，附上「今晚被子歸我」當宣戰
variations: 進階版：後來我們買了兩條被子各蓋各的，從此天下太平——但他半夜還是會把腳偷偷伸過來，說沒有那雙腳他睡不著。
短版：感情的考驗不是吵架，是半夜那雙冰腳貼上來，你還是沒把他踢下床。

en.content:
Before we moved in together, I thought two people sharing one blanket was the most romantic image in the world.

Afterward I learned it's actually a nightly, refereeless, ceasefire-free tug of war.

3 a.m. I wake up with exactly one corner of blanket left. He's wrapped the entire thing around himself like a spring roll, sleeping in perfect peace.

And then — just as I'm shivering — a pair of feet, cold like they came straight out of the freezer, lands with pinpoint accuracy on the small of my back.

Commitment is learning that, somewhere between the zero-degree feet and the blanket you'll never win back, this person is still completely worth it.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who steals the covers and ambushes you with ice-cold feet — turns the bedtime war into an affectionate gripe. Add 'the blanket is mine tonight' as a declaration of war.
variations: Advanced version: we eventually bought two separate blankets and finally found peace — but he still sneaks a foot over, says he can't sleep without it.
Short version: the real test of love isn't the fights — it's those freezing feet on your back at 3 a.m. and still not kicking him out of bed.

items.sourceNote: 情侶已讀不回的腦補小劇場，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
我傳訊息給他：「在嗎？」
顯示「已讀」。

然後，沒了。

接下來的十分鐘，我在腦中導演了一整部電影：

第一分鐘——他可能在忙。
第三分鐘——他是不是生氣了？我哪句話講錯了？
第五分鐘——他是不是不愛我了。
第八分鐘——我們是不是要分手了，這段感情走到頭了。

第十分鐘，他回了：「剛剛在上廁所，手機沒帶，怎麼了寶貝？」

我：「沒事，想你而已。」
（電影下檔，票房慘澹，但我演得很投入。）
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個一已讀不回就讓你腦補三千字的另一半——把「等回訊息」的小劇場寫成笑話，自嘲多疑又戳中所有人
variations: 進階版：後來我學乖了，已讀不回我就去睡覺，醒來發現他回了二十則訊息問我是不是生氣了——原來腦補小劇場是會傳染的。
短版：愛一個人最明顯的症狀，就是把對方的「已讀」當成一部懸疑片在看。

en.content:
I texted him: 'You there?'
It says 'Read.'

Then — nothing.

Over the next ten minutes I directed an entire feature film in my head:

Minute 1 — he's probably busy.
Minute 3 — is he mad? What did I say wrong?
Minute 5 — he doesn't love me anymore.
Minute 8 — we're breaking up, this is the end of us.

Minute 10, he replies: 'Sorry, was in the bathroom, didn't have my phone. What's up babe?'

Me: 'Nothing, just missed you.'
(Film canceled. Box office: terrible. My performance, however, was deeply committed.)
en.editorNote: Send to the partner whose unanswered 'Read' receipt sends you spiraling into a three-thousand-word inner monologue — self-deprecating about the overthinking, and painfully universal.
variations: Advanced version: I eventually wised up and just went to sleep on the unanswered text — woke up to twenty messages asking if I was mad. Turns out the inner-movie spiral is contagious.
Short version: the clearest symptom of loving someone is treating their 'Read' receipt like a suspense thriller.

items.sourceNote: 情侶「我沒生氣」的口是心非，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
情侶之間最危險的三個字，不是「我愛你」，是——「我沒事」。

翻譯對照表：

「我沒事。」＝我很有事，而且你應該自己知道是什麼事。
「隨便你。」＝你最好不要隨便。
「我不餓。」＝（在你吃到一半時）借我吃一口。
「你去忙你的。」＝你敢去我就記到明年。
「我先睡了。」＝我會一直醒著等你來哄。

交往的第一課不是談戀愛，是學第二外語。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個嘴上說沒事、其實寫滿台詞的另一半——把情侶間的口是心非編成翻譯對照表，看完保證會心一笑加猛點頭
variations: 進階版：最高境界是兩個人都說「我沒事」，然後一起沉默地坐在沙發兩端，等對方先投降——通常是肚子先投降。
短版：「我沒事」是全世界最短的長篇小說，字數三個，內容三萬。

en.content:
The three most dangerous words between a couple aren't 'I love you' — they're 'I'm fine.'

Translation guide:

'I'm fine.' = I am very not fine, and you should already know why.
'Whatever you want.' = You'd better not actually do whatever you want.
'I'm not hungry.' = (halfway through your meal) Let me have a bite.
'Go do your thing.' = Do it and I'll remember it until next year.
'I'm going to sleep.' = I'll stay awake waiting for you to come make up.

The first lesson of dating isn't romance. It's learning a second language.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who says 'I'm fine' while running a full script underneath — turns couple-speak into a translation chart that earns a knowing laugh and vigorous nodding.
variations: Advanced version: peak relationship is both of you saying 'I'm fine,' then sitting in silence at opposite ends of the couch waiting for the other to cave — usually it's a stomach that caves first.
Short version: 'I'm fine' is the world's shortest epic novel — three words, thirty thousand of subtext.

items.sourceNote: 情侶一起逛超市的失控採購，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
我們約好只是去超市買「醬油跟衛生紙」兩樣東西。

進去前，我還很有把握地說：「五分鐘搞定。」

結帳的時候，購物車裡有：兩種口味的洋芋片、一盒草莓、一隻烤雞、限時特價的冰淇淋、一個我們其實用不到但很可愛的馬克杯、四罐氣泡水。

沒有醬油。也沒有衛生紙。

我們在停車場對看了一眼，然後一起笑出來。

所謂情侶，就是兩個本來只想買兩樣東西、最後扛回一整個週末的人。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個每次逛超市都會跟你一起失控、最後忘記買真正要買的東西的另一半——把日常採購寫成情侶默契的笑點
variations: 進階版：回到家才發現，明天早餐要用的醬油還是沒有——於是我們又出門了一趟，這次買回來：兩支冰棒，還是沒有醬油。
短版：情侶逛超市的鐵律——清單上的東西一定忘記買，清單外的東西一定買滿。

en.content:
We agreed to run to the store for exactly two things: soy sauce and toilet paper.

Before going in, I confidently said: 'Five minutes, tops.'

At checkout, the cart contained: two flavors of chips, a box of strawberries, a rotisserie chicken, a flash-sale tub of ice cream, a mug we absolutely do not need but is undeniably cute, and four cans of sparkling water.

No soy sauce. No toilet paper.

We locked eyes in the parking lot and burst out laughing.

A couple is two people who set out to buy two things and came home carrying an entire weekend.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who spirals out of control with you at the store and forgets the one thing you actually came for — turns a grocery run into an inside joke about your shared chaos.
variations: Advanced version: got home and realized we still had no soy sauce for tomorrow's breakfast — so we went back out, and returned with two popsicles. Still no soy sauce.
Short version: the iron law of couples at the grocery store — you'll forget everything on the list and buy everything that isn't.

items.sourceNote: 情侶共用一支手機充電線的爭奪戰，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
外人以為，情侶吵架都是為了大事——劈腿、錢、未來、要不要結婚。

錯。

我們昨天最激烈的一場爭執，是為了——「家裡唯一那條沒壞的充電線現在歸誰用」。

他：「我手機剩百分之三。」
我：「我剩百分之五，而且我等下要出門。」
他：「百分之三比百分之五低。」
我：「但你又不出門。」

我們認真地辯論了整整十分鐘，論點之嚴謹堪比法庭。

最後的解決方案是：我們又買了三條充電線，放在家裡每一個房間。

感情走到後來，浪漫的盡頭不是燭光晚餐，是兩個人永遠都有電。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個會跟你搶充電線、搶遙控器、搶最後一口飯的另一半——把生活裡最雞毛蒜皮的爭奪戰寫成笑話，越平凡越有共鳴
variations: 進階版：自從每個房間都有充電線之後，我們找到了新的戰場——「冷氣要開幾度」。戰爭從不結束，只會換主題。
短版：成熟的感情，就是再也不為充電線吵架——因為我們直接買了五條。

en.content:
Outsiders assume couples fight about the big stuff — cheating, money, the future, whether to get married.

Wrong.

Our most heated argument yesterday was over: who gets the one charging cable in the house that still works.

Him: 'My phone's at three percent.'
Me: 'Mine's at five, and I'm about to leave.'
Him: 'Three is lower than five.'
Me: 'But you're not even going anywhere.'

We debated this, in earnest, for ten full minutes — with the rigor of a courtroom.

The final resolution: we bought three more cables and put one in every room.

Deep into a relationship, the end of romance isn't candlelit dinners — it's both of you always having battery.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who fights you over the charging cable, the remote, the last bite — turns the pettiest household skirmishes into a joke. The more mundane, the more relatable.
variations: Advanced version: ever since every room got its own cable, we found a new battlefield — 'what temperature the AC should be.' The war never ends, it just changes topics.
Short version: a mature relationship is never fighting over the charging cable again — because we just bought five of them.

items.sourceNote: 另一半否認自己會打呼、堅稱是「大力呼吸」的梗，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
「你昨天晚上打呼打超大聲。」
「我沒有打呼。」
「我錄音了。」
「……那不是打呼。」
「那是什麼？」
「那是……我在用力呼吸。是一種深層放鬆的呼吸法。」
「深層放鬆到隔壁鄰居敲牆壁？」
「他們是在打拍子。」

世界上沒有一個人承認自己會打呼。
打呼的人永遠是另一個人，或是那台不存在的除濕機。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個睡覺會打呼、卻死不承認的另一半——半夜被吵醒的怨氣，用笑話包起來送出去，比直接抱怨更有殺傷力也更好笑
variations: 進階版：後來我學聰明了，我也開始打呼，而且打得比他還大聲。從那天起，他再也沒說過我打呼——因為他根本睡得比我沉。
短版：情侶睡覺的真相——會打呼的那個永遠睡得最好，被吵的那個負責記仇。

en.content:
"You were snoring so loud last night."
"I don't snore."
"I recorded it."
"...That's not snoring."
"Then what is it?"
"That's... me breathing forcefully. It's a deep-relaxation breathing technique."
"So relaxed the neighbors banged on the wall?"
"They were keeping the beat."

No one on Earth admits to snoring.
The snorer is always the other person — or a dehumidifier that doesn't exist.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who snores and will die before admitting it — wrapping your 3 a.m. resentment in a joke lands harder, and funnier, than complaining ever could.
variations: Advanced version: I eventually got smart and started snoring too, louder than him. He never mentioned my snoring again — because he was always out cold before I was.
Short version: the truth about couples and sleep — the one who snores sleeps best, and the one who gets woken up keeps score.

items.sourceNote: 情侶出門前一方等另一方化妝／打理超久的梗，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
「還有五分鐘就要出門囉。」
「好，我快好了。」

——這句「快好了」，是人類語言中最不精確的時間單位。

它可以是三分鐘，也可以是四十分鐘。
它取決於：頭髮今天聽不聽話、找不找得到那件想穿的衣服、以及站在鏡子前那一刻對整個宇宙的自我懷疑。

我坐在玄關，已經穿好鞋、拿好鑰匙、滑完整個社群軟體、看完一部短影片合輯。

她還在裡面喊：「我真的快好了！」

情侶出門，從來不是兩個人的事——是一個人在等另一個人跟宇宙和解。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個每次出門都要等超久、或每次都在門口等到天荒地老的另一半——把「快好了」這句世紀謊言寫成溫柔的吐槽，等待也變得可愛
variations: 進階版：後來我學會了，只要我想準時出門，就跟她報「提早半小時」的假時間。結果她發現了，現在她會問我：「你說的是真的時間，還是騙我的時間？」信任就是這樣一點一滴瓦解的。
短版：情侶出門的物理定律——先說好的人負責在門口罰站，後準備的人負責跟宇宙和解。

en.content:
"We're leaving in five minutes."
"Okay, I'm almost ready."

— 'Almost ready' is the least precise unit of time in all of human language.

It could mean three minutes. It could mean forty.
It depends on: whether the hair cooperates today, whether that one specific shirt can be found, and the existential crisis that strikes the moment you stand in front of the mirror.

I'm sitting by the door. Shoes on, keys in hand, scrolled through all of social media, finished a whole short-video compilation.

She's still in there yelling: "I'm SO close!"

A couple leaving the house is never about two people — it's one person waiting for the other to make peace with the universe.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who takes forever to get ready, or the one who's been waiting by the door since the dawn of time — turning 'almost ready' into a gentle roast makes the wait almost cute.
variations: Advanced version: I eventually learned to tell her a fake time half an hour early whenever I actually needed to be punctual. She figured it out. Now she asks: 'Is that the real time or the lie time?' That's how trust erodes, one departure at a time.
Short version: the physics of couples leaving the house — whoever's ready first serves the sentence by the door; whoever's still getting ready is negotiating with the cosmos.

items.sourceNote: 情侶暱稱隨交往時間越叫越隨便的演變梗，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
情侶暱稱的演化史：

交往第一週：「寶貝」「親愛的」「我的小天使」
交往三個月：「寶」
交往一年：「欸」
交往三年：直接發出一個「嗯？」的鼻音，對方就知道在叫他。
交往五年：不叫名字，直接開始講事情——「垃圾車來了。」對方立刻起身。

感情越深，稱呼越短。
到最後，最親密的呼喚，是連名字都不用叫，一個眼神、一個聲音，對方就懂了。

這不是不浪漫。這是浪漫到不需要包裝了。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個早就不叫你「寶貝」、改用鼻音跟你溝通的另一半——把暱稱的退化寫成默契的升級，看似吐槽其實是放閃
variations: 進階版：交往夠久之後，連「嗯？」都省了，直接用一個轉頭的動作叫對方。旁人以為我們冷淡，其實我們是把對話壓縮成了摩斯密碼。
短版：情侶的稱呼跟感情成反比——叫得越隨便，愛得越死心塌地。

en.content:
The evolution of a couple's pet names:

Week one: "Babe." "Sweetheart." "My little angel."
Three months: "Bae."
One year: "Hey."
Three years: just a nasal "Mm?" and they know it's them being addressed.
Five years: no name at all — you just start talking. "Trash truck's here." They immediately get up.

The deeper the love, the shorter the name.
Eventually the most intimate call requires no name at all — one glance, one sound, and they understand.

This isn't unromantic. This is romance so complete it no longer needs wrapping.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who long ago stopped calling you 'babe' and now communicates in nasal grunts — reframing the decay of pet names as an upgrade in intimacy. Looks like a roast, secretly a flex.
variations: Advanced version: after long enough, even the 'Mm?' gets dropped — you summon each other with a single turn of the head. Others think we're cold; really we've just compressed conversation into Morse code.
Short version: a couple's pet names are inversely proportional to their love — the lazier the name, the more hopelessly devoted the heart.

items.sourceNote: 同居後衣櫃／浴室檯面空間被另一半逐漸佔領的梗，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
剛同居的時候，我們很公平地把衣櫃分成兩半：左邊你的，右邊我的。

一年後，我的衣服被擠到只剩一根桿子的邊角，像難民一樣緊緊靠在一起。

她的衣服呢？佔領了另外的三根桿子、兩個抽屜、還有一個原本說好放棉被的層架。

浴室檯面也是。我的東西：一支牙刷、一條牙膏。
她的東西：我至今叫不出名字的十七種瓶瓶罐罐，排列得像一支軍隊。

我沒有抗議。因為我知道——所謂同居，就是慢慢接受「我的空間」其實從第一天起，就是「我們的空間」，而「我們的空間」的意思，通常是「她的空間，加上我可以放牙刷的地方」。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個把家裡空間慢慢佔領、卻佔領得理直氣壯的另一半——把「地盤被侵略」的無奈寫成甘願的告白，抱怨的底下是滿滿的縱容
variations: 進階版：後來我發現，我唯一完全屬於我的領土，是冰箱裡最上層那一格——因為她構不到。那是我最後的堡壘，我守得很好。
短版：同居的真相——衣櫃永遠會朝著同一個方向擴張，而那個方向不是我。

en.content:
When we first moved in together, we split the closet fairly: left side yours, right side mine.

A year later, my clothes have been squeezed into the corner of a single rod, huddled together like refugees.

Hers? They've annexed the other three rods, two drawers, and a shelf that was supposedly reserved for spare blankets.

Same with the bathroom counter. My stuff: one toothbrush, one tube of toothpaste.
Her stuff: seventeen bottles and jars I still can't name, arranged like a standing army.

I never protested. Because I've learned — moving in together means slowly accepting that 'my space' was, from day one, 'our space,' and that 'our space' usually means 'her space, plus somewhere I'm allowed to keep a toothbrush.'
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who's been quietly annexing every inch of the home with total confidence — reframing the loss of territory as a willing surrender. Under the complaint is nothing but indulgence.
variations: Advanced version: I eventually discovered the one territory that's entirely mine — the top shelf of the fridge, because she can't reach it. It's my last stronghold, and I defend it well.
Short version: the truth about moving in together — the closet always expands in one direction, and that direction isn't me.

items.sourceNote: 情侶共用 Netflix/串流帳號，一方偷追進度的梗，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
我們約好，那部影集要一起追，一集都不能偷跑。

這是神聖的約定，等同於婚前財產協議那種等級的嚴肅。

結果昨天晚上，她假裝要早睡，我假裝要去洗澡。

十一點半，我躲在客廳沙發，音量調到最小，戴著耳機，偷偷看了兩集。

看得正緊張的時候，房間門開了。她探出頭，我們四目相對——她手上也拿著手機，耳機也還掛在耳朵上。

我們都沉默了三秒。

然後同時問對方：「你看到第幾集？」

原來偷跑的不只我一個。原來所謂「一起追」，只是兩個人各自偷跑、最後在進度上假裝驚訝的一場精密演出。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個發誓要一起追劇、結果比你還早偷看完的另一半——最甜的背叛，就是兩個人偷偷做了同一件壞事，還都被抓包
variations: 進階版：後來我們約好「這次真的一起追」，然後兩個人一邊看一邊偷瞄對方的表情，確認對方是不是在裝驚訝。信任，就這樣一集一集地崩解又重建。
短版：情侶版的互相背叛——你偷看了，我也偷看了，我們假裝一起看得很開心。

en.content:
We had a deal: we'd watch that series together, not a single episode ahead.

A sacred pact. Roughly the seriousness of a prenup.

So last night, she pretended she was turning in early, and I pretended I was going to shower.

Eleven thirty, I'm hiding on the living room couch, volume at its lowest, headphones on, sneaking in two episodes.

Just as it's getting tense, the bedroom door opens. Her head pokes out, our eyes meet — she's holding her phone too, headphones still in her ears.

Three seconds of silence.

Then we ask each other at the same time: "What episode are you on?"

Turns out I wasn't the only one who cheated. Turns out 'watching together' was just two people each sneaking ahead, then performing surprise at the plot on cue.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who swore you'd watch it together, then binged the whole thing before you — the sweetest betrayal is two people committing the same crime and both getting caught
variations: Advanced version: afterward we agreed 'this time we really watch together,' then spent the whole time side-eyeing each other's reactions to check if the surprise was genuine. Trust, dismantled and rebuilt one episode at a time.
Short version: the couple's mutual betrayal — you peeked, I peeked, and we pretend we're watching it fresh together.

items.sourceNote: 情侶「我沒生氣」但明顯在生氣的梗，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
「你是不是在生氣？」
「沒有。」

這個「沒有」有很多種版本，交往久了你就能分辨：

輕快的「沒有～」：真的沒有。
平淡的「沒有。」：有一點，但可以哄。
簡短的「沒。」：警報。
加上「我為什麼要生氣」的：紅色警報，立刻放下手機。
最恐怖的是那句——「我沒生氣，你去忙你的。」

聽到這句話，任何一個活著回來的男人都知道：接下來一個小時，你唯一該忙的事，就是搞清楚你到底做錯了什麼。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個嘴上說沒生氣、眼神卻在寫遺書的另一半——「我沒生氣」是全世界最需要翻譯的三個字，而翻譯錯誤的代價，通常是睡沙發
variations: 進階版：最高段的是「隨便你」。這三個字的意思從來不是隨便你，而是「你最好給我選對，選錯的話後果你自己承擔」。
短版：情侶對話翻譯指南第一課——「我沒生氣」的正確翻譯是「你有三秒鐘可以道歉」。

en.content:
"Are you mad?"
"No."

That 'no' comes in many editions, and after enough years you learn to tell them apart:

The breezy 'no~': genuinely fine.
The flat 'No.': slightly mad, but salvageable.
The clipped 'Nope.': red alert.
The 'why would I be mad': DEFCON 1, put the phone down now.
And the most terrifying of all — 'I'm not mad, go do your thing.'

Any man who's lived to tell the tale knows: for the next hour, the only thing you should be doing is figuring out exactly what you did wrong.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who says they're not mad while their eyes draft a will — 'I'm not mad' is the three most mistranslated words in any language, and a bad translation usually means the couch
variations: Advanced version: the highest tier is 'whatever you want.' Those words have never once meant whatever you want. They mean 'you'd better choose correctly, and if you don't, that's on you.'
Short version: couple-speak lesson one — 'I'm not mad' correctly translates to 'you have three seconds to apologize.'

items.sourceNote: 情侶睡覺搶棉被／睡姿佔床的梗，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
我們買了一張加大雙人床，號稱可以睡得像兩個國王一樣寬敞。

事實是：

她睡成一個大字型，橫跨整張床的對角線，一隻腳還踩到我的枕頭上。

棉被，百分之九十在她那邊，捲成一個溫暖的繭。

而我，蜷縮在床的最邊緣，用剩下那百分之十的棉被勉強蓋住一隻手臂，隨時可能滾下床，姿勢像一隻掛在懸崖上的貓。

整個晚上我都在進行一場無聲的談判：小心翼翼地把棉被拉回來一公分，她就會在睡夢中把它捲回去兩公分。

我從沒贏過。而且我發現，早上醒來看她睡得那麼香、那麼安穩，我居然一點都不想吵醒她。

這大概就是愛——甘願在懸崖邊睡一整晚，只為了不打擾對方那個霸佔全床的美夢。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個睡相像海星、卻能把你擠到床邊還睡得香甜的另一半——關於愛最真實的證明，就是你寧願自己冷，也不忍心叫醒睡得正甜的她
variations: 進階版：後來我學聰明了，睡前先把棉被的一角壓在自己身體下面。結果早上醒來，棉被還是在她那邊，我壓的那一角不知何時被抽走了。她連睡著都比我強。
短版：加大雙人床的真相——床變大了，我的地盤沒有。

en.content:
We bought a king-size bed, the kind that supposedly lets two people sleep as spaciously as royalty.

Here's the reality:

She sprawls into a starfish across the diagonal, one foot planted on my pillow.

The blanket? Ninety percent of it wrapped around her in a warm cocoon.

And me — curled up on the very edge, the remaining ten percent barely covering one arm, one roll away from falling off, posed like a cat clinging to a cliff.

All night long I conduct a silent negotiation: I gently reclaim one inch of blanket, and she, deep in sleep, spins it back two.

I've never won. And I've realized — when I wake up and see her sleeping so soundly, so peacefully, I don't want to wake her at all.

That's probably what love is: willingly sleeping on the edge of a cliff all night, just so you don't disturb the person hogging the entire bed in her sweet dreams.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who sleeps like a starfish and shoves you to the edge while resting blissfully — the truest proof of love is choosing to freeze rather than wake the one sleeping so sweetly
variations: Advanced version: I eventually got clever and tucked a corner of the blanket under my body before sleep. Come morning, the blanket was on her side again, and my tucked corner had somehow been extracted. She out-competes me even unconscious.
Short version: the truth about a king-size bed — the bed got bigger, my territory didn't.

items.sourceNote: 情侶一方拍照要求重拍很多次的梗，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
「幫我拍張照。」

這六個字，是我這輩子聽過最有陷阱的一句話。

我拍了第一張。「腿太短，重拍。」
第二張。「你手在抖是不是，糊掉了。」
第三張。「這個角度顯胖，蹲低一點。」
第四張，我蹲到膝蓋快斷了。「後面那個垃圾桶入鏡了，往左移。」
第五張。「光線不對，我們換個地方。」

拍到第二十七張，我已經全身汗，蹲在地上，用一種跪拜的姿勢仰角拍攝，像在朝聖。

她看了一眼：「就用第一張吧，剛剛那張其實還不錯。」

那一刻我明白了：所謂幫女友拍照，不是攝影，是一場長達半小時、只為了回到起點的修行。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個能讓你蹲到腿麻、拍二十七張最後選第一張的另一半——男友的攝影技術不是天生的，是被要求重拍逼出來的血淚結晶
variations: 進階版：後來我學會一個訣竅——第一張隨便拍，然後不管她怎麼要求，接下來二十張我都偷偷連拍一模一樣的，最後給她看「新拍的第一張」，她說「這張明顯好多了」。
短版：幫女友拍照的終極真相——拍一百張，選第一張，你的膝蓋為藝術犧牲。

en.content:
"Take a photo of me."

Those five words are the most booby-trapped sentence I've ever heard.

I took the first shot. "My legs look short, again."
Second. "Were your hands shaking? It's blurry."
Third. "This angle makes me look wide, crouch lower."
Fourth, I'm crouched until my knees nearly snap. "There's a trash can in the background, move left."
Fifth. "The light's wrong, let's find another spot."

By shot twenty-seven, I'm drenched in sweat, crouched on the ground, shooting upward from a full kneeling position like a pilgrim in worship.

She glances at the screen: "Let's just use the first one, that one was actually pretty good."

And in that moment I understood: taking your girlfriend's photo isn't photography — it's a half-hour spiritual trial whose only purpose is to return you to the starting point.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who makes you crouch till your legs go numb, shoot twenty-seven takes, then pick the first — a boyfriend's photography skills aren't born, they're beaten out of him one retake at a time
variations: Advanced version: I eventually learned a trick — take the first shot casually, then no matter what she asks, secretly burst-fire twenty identical copies, and finally show her the 'newly taken first one.' She says, 'this one's clearly so much better.'
Short version: the ultimate truth about photographing your girlfriend — a hundred shots, the first one wins, and your knees are sacrificed to art.

items.sourceNote: 情侶一方買了東西回家藏起來、被發現的梗，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
她最近迷上網購。

每次包裹送到，她都會用一種特務等級的手法處理：趁我不在家的時候簽收，把紙箱拆掉丟到樓下回收，再把新衣服悄悄混進衣櫃裡，跟舊衣服放在一起，讓它看起來「本來就在那裡」。

我沒有拆穿。因為我發現，這樣其實對我們兩個都好。

直到有天，她穿了一件很好看的洋裝，我忍不住稱讚：「這件真好看，新買的？」

她眼睛都沒眨：「舊的啊，我穿過很多次了你都沒發現。」

那一刻我差點鼓掌。因為那件洋裝，前一天我才在垃圾桶旁邊看到它的吊牌。

我決定什麼都不說。有些真相，讓它安靜地掛在衣櫃裡，就是給彼此最溫柔的體貼。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個網購成癮、還會把新衣服說成「這件很舊了」的另一半——最有默契的愛情，就是一個假裝那是舊衣服，另一個假裝真的相信
variations: 進階版：後來我也學會了。我買了新的遊戲機，放在櫃子最底層，跟舊的線材放在一起。她發現的時候我說「這台很久了啊」，她點點頭說「喔」。我們都懂，我們都選擇不戳破。
短版：情侶網購潛規則——所有新衣服都是「這件很舊了」，所有新玩具都是「這個放很久了」。

en.content:
She's been really into online shopping lately.

Every time a package arrives, she handles it with the finesse of a secret agent: signs for it while I'm out, breaks down the box and dumps it in the downstairs recycling, then quietly slips the new clothes into the closet next to the old ones so it all looks like it 'was always there.'

I never called her out. Because I figured it was better for both of us this way.

Until one day she wore a gorgeous dress and I couldn't help complimenting it: "That looks great, is it new?"

Without blinking: "It's old, I've worn it tons of times, you just never noticed."

I nearly applauded. Because the day before, I'd seen that exact dress's price tag sitting next to the trash can.

I decided to say nothing. Some truths are best left hanging quietly in the closet — that silence is the gentlest kind of tenderness.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner addicted to online shopping who insists the new dress is 'super old' — the most in-sync love is one person pretending it's an old outfit and the other pretending to believe it
variations: Advanced version: eventually I learned the trick too. Bought a new game console, stashed it at the back of the cabinet next to the old cables. When she found it I said 'oh, I've had that forever,' and she nodded, 'huh.' We both know. We both choose not to poke the bubble.
Short version: the unspoken rule of couple's online shopping — every new dress is 'this old thing,' and every new gadget has 'been sitting there forever.'

items.sourceNote: 情侶偷吃對方薯條的梗，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
我點了大薯，她說她不吃，她在減肥。

於是我開始吃。第三根的時候，一隻手默默伸過來，拿走了一根。
「你不是不吃？」
「我只吃你的。」

我懂了。原來她不是不吃薯條，她是不吃「自己的」薯條。自己的有熱量，我的沒有。這是一種只存在於情侶之間的物理定律。

等我回過神，紙盒已經空了，她一臉無辜地說：「你吃好快喔。」

那天我學到一件事：跟另一半出去，永遠要點兩份大薯，一份給她吃，一份也給她吃。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個嘴上說減肥、手卻誠實地伸向你薯條的另一半——愛情就是我的薯條沒有熱量，你的才有
variations: 進階版：後來我學乖了，我點兩份大薯。她看了看說：「你吃這麼多喔？」然後把其中一份整包拿走。我還是只吃到一份。
短版：情侶版能量守恆定律——薯條的熱量，只有在「自己那份」的時候才存在。

en.content:
I ordered a large fries. She said she wasn't having any — she's on a diet.

So I started eating. By the third fry, a hand quietly reached over and took one.
"I thought you weren't eating?"
"I only eat yours."

And I understood. She doesn't dislike fries — she dislikes *her own* fries. Hers have calories. Mine don't. It's a law of physics that exists only between couples.

By the time I snapped out of it, the box was empty and she said, all innocent: "Wow, you eat fast."

That day I learned one thing: whenever you go out with your partner, always order two large fries — one for her to eat, and one for her to also eat.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who swears they're dieting while their hand reaches for your fries — love means my fries have no calories, only yours do
variations: Advanced version: I wised up and ordered two large fries. She looked at them and said 'that's a lot for you,' then took an entire box. I still only got one.
Short version: the couple's law of conservation of energy — fries only have calories when they're 'your own portion.'

items.sourceNote: 情侶同一個房間卻用手機傳訊息的梗，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
我們坐在同一張沙發上，中間隔了大概三十公分。

她傳訊息給我：「你會不會餓？」
我打字回：「有一點。」
她再傳：「那要不要叫外送？」
我回：「好啊你想吃什麼？」
她傳：「都可以。」

我抬起頭看她，她也抬起頭看我，兩個人對看了兩秒，然後不約而同地低下頭，繼續用手機打字。

有時候我覺得，我們之間最遠的距離，不是天涯海角，是三十公分加一支手機。

但奇怪的是，我一點也不想改變這件事。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個明明就坐在你旁邊、卻還是用傳訊息跟你聊天的另一半——最舒服的親密，就是連話都懶得說出口也不覺得尷尬
variations: 進階版：後來外送到了，我們還是用傳訊息討論誰去開門。最後是我去開的，因為她傳了一個「拜託～」的貼圖，這種攻擊我防不了。
短版：情侶最遠的距離——坐在同一張沙發上，卻用手機問對方餓不餓。

en.content:
We were sitting on the same couch, maybe a foot apart.

She texted me: "Are you hungry?"
I typed back: "A little."
She texted: "Want to order delivery?"
I replied: "Sure, what do you feel like?"
She sent: "Anything."

I looked up at her. She looked up at me. We held eye contact for two seconds — then, in perfect sync, both lowered our heads and kept typing.

Sometimes I think the greatest distance between us isn't oceans or continents. It's one foot and a phone.

And the strange thing is, I don't want to change a single bit of it.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who's literally sitting next to you and still chats by text — the coziest intimacy is being too lazy to even speak out loud and not finding it weird
variations: Advanced version: when the food arrived, we texted about who'd get the door. I went, because she sent a 'pleaaase~' sticker, and that's an attack I have no defense against.
Short version: the greatest distance between a couple — sitting on the same couch, texting to ask if the other one's hungry.

items.sourceNote: 情侶一方睡覺搶棉被的梗，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
同居之後我才發現，她睡覺的時候是一個掠奪者。

晚上我們蓋著同一條棉被入睡，一切都很和諧。但到了半夜，我會在一種莫名的寒冷中醒來，發現自己像一顆被剝了殼的水煮蛋，赤裸裸地暴露在冷氣底下。

而她，在床的另一端，把整條棉被捲成一個溫暖的蛹，睡得像個嬰兒，臉上還帶著滿足的微笑。

我試著把棉被拉回來一點點。她立刻無意識地反手一捲，捲得更緊了。這是一種深植在基因裡的本能，比她本人還要清醒。

最後我認命了，去衣櫃拿了另一條棉被。從此我們一人一條，相安無事。

愛一個人，就是願意為她多買一條棉被。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個睡覺會把棉被捲走、還睡得特別香的另一半——真愛不是共蓋一條棉被，是心甘情願去買第二條
variations: 進階版：後來我買了第二條棉被，結果半夜醒來發現她兩條都捲走了，我蓋著一件外套。我開始懷疑這是一種需要專業協助的狀況。
短版：同居真相——一條棉被撐不過一對情侶，這是購物清單上第一個要更新的項目。

en.content:
After we moved in together, I discovered she's a predator in her sleep.

At night we drift off under the same blanket, all harmonious. But somewhere around 3 a.m. I wake up in a mysterious cold, only to find myself like a peeled hard-boiled egg, fully exposed to the air conditioning.

And she — over on the far side of the bed — has rolled the entire blanket into a warm cocoon, sleeping like a baby, a satisfied little smile on her face.

I try to tug some of it back. Instantly, without waking, she counter-rolls and wraps herself tighter. It's an instinct coded deep in her genes, more alert than she is.

Eventually I gave up and grabbed a second blanket from the closet. From then on, one blanket each, total peace.

Loving someone means being willing to buy her one more blanket.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who steals the whole blanket and sleeps extra soundly for it — true love isn't sharing one blanket, it's cheerfully going out to buy a second
variations: Advanced version: I bought a second blanket, then woke at 3 a.m. to find she'd rolled up both and I was sleeping under a jacket. I'm starting to think this needs professional help.
