Short version: the truth about moving in together — one blanket can't survive a couple, and it's the first thing on the shopping list to upgrade.

items.sourceNote: 情侶一方說「沒事」其實有事的梗，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
情侶之間最危險的兩個字，是「沒事」。

「你怎麼了？」
「沒事。」

這個「沒事」，翻譯成人類的語言，是：「有事，而且是大事，而且你應該要自己知道是什麼事，如果你需要我告訴你，那就是又多了一件事。」

我花了三年的時間，才勉強破譯出這套加密系統。現在的我聽到「沒事」，會啟動全身的雷達，開始回想過去二十四小時我說過的每一句話、做過的每一個表情。

通常答案藏在一個我完全想不到的地方。比如三天前我隨口說「這件衣服有點顯胖」，指的是我自己。

她記得。她一直記得。「沒事」的意思，是她還在等我自己想起來。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個嘴上說「沒事」、心裡其實有一本帳的另一半——「沒事」是全世界最需要被認真對待的兩個字
variations: 進階版：後來我學會了，聽到「沒事」我不再猜，我直接把過去一週的對話全部認錯一遍。總有一句會中。這叫地毯式道歉。
短版：情侶翻譯機——「沒事」＝有事；「隨便」＝不隨便；「你決定」＝你最好決定對。

en.content:
The two most dangerous words between a couple: "It's fine."

"What's wrong?"
"It's fine."

Translated into human language, "it's fine" means: "Something is wrong, and it's big, and you should already know what it is, and if I have to tell you, that's now one more thing wrong."

It took me three years to barely crack this encryption. These days, the moment I hear "it's fine," my full-body radar activates and I start replaying every sentence I've said and every face I've made in the last twenty-four hours.

The answer is usually hidden somewhere I'd never guess. Like three days ago when I said offhand, "this shirt makes me look kind of wide" — meaning myself.

She remembered. She always remembers. "It's fine" means she's still waiting for me to figure it out on my own.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who says 'it's fine' while quietly keeping a ledger — 'it's fine' is the most important two words in the world to take seriously
variations: Advanced version: I eventually learned — when I hear 'it's fine,' I stop guessing and just apologize for every conversation from the past week. One of them always lands. It's called carpet-bomb apologizing.
Short version: the couple's translator — 'it's fine' = not fine; 'whatever' = not whatever; 'you decide' = you'd better decide right.

items.sourceNote: original couple joke on communication

zh-tw.content: 女友說「隨便」的時候，我就知道接下來每一個選項都是陷阱，包括「不然妳決定」。
zh-tw.editorNote: 情侶約會前選餐廳鬼打牆時，傳給對方緩和氣氛用。
variations: 「我沒生氣」是全世界最需要翻譯的三個字。

en.content: The moment she says 'whatever's fine,' I know every option ahead is a trap, including 'okay, you decide.'
en.editorNote: Send it mid-argument about where to eat, to break the standoff.
variations: 'I'm not mad' is the three-word phrase most in need of subtitles.

items.sourceNote: original couple joke on shared food

zh-tw.content: 她說「我不餓，你點就好」，然後吃掉了我一半的薯條。原來愛情裡沒有「我的」，只有「我們的，但主要是她的」。
zh-tw.editorNote: 情侶共食被搶最後一塊時，配這句自嘲最對味。
variations: 交往後我學會的第一件事：永遠多點一份薯條。

en.content: She said 'I'm not hungry, just order for yourself,' then ate half my fries. In love there's no 'mine,' only 'ours, but mostly hers.'
en.editorNote: Perfect caption when your partner swipes the last bite off your plate.
variations: First thing I learned after we got together: always order extra fries.

items.sourceNote: original couple joke on sleeping habits

zh-tw.content: 同居後才發現，一張雙人床的真相是：她佔四分之三，我佔四分之一，棉被則完全獨立自主，屬於她。
zh-tw.editorNote: 同居情侶早上搶棉被醒來時，拍張床照配這句。
variations: 所謂共枕眠，就是我睡邊緣、她睡整張。

en.content: After moving in together I learned the truth about a queen bed: she gets three quarters, I get one, and the blanket has fully declared independence in her favor.
en.editorNote: Snap the bed in the morning after the nightly blanket tug-of-war.
variations: 'Sharing a bed' means I sleep on the edge and she sleeps on all of it.

items.sourceNote: 情侶簡訊已讀不回／回覆長度落差梗，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
我打了一大段小論文跟她道歉，字斟句酌，寫了快十分鐘。
她回我：「嗯嗯。」

兩個字。
我不確定我們是和好了，還是我又多了一件錯事。
zh-tw.editorNote: 跟另一半冷戰後、傳完長訊息在等回覆的空窗期，最適合傳這句給朋友取暖。
variations: 進階版：我傳一段落落長的真心話，她回一個「哦」。單一個字，殺傷力比整段吵架還大。

en.content:
I typed out a whole apology paragraph. Chose every word carefully. Ten minutes of my life.
She replied: "k."

One letter.
I'm not sure if we made up or if I just added a new item to the list.
en.editorNote: Perfect to send a friend while you're staring at the screen waiting for a reply after a fight.
variations: Advanced version: I send my whole heart in one message and get back a single 'lol.' Three letters, more damage than the actual argument.

items.sourceNote: 情侶做家事分工／洗碗推託梗，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
我們的洗碗規則很公平：誰煮飯，另一個人就洗碗。
所以現在我們兩個都不會煮飯，家裡的碗永遠是乾淨的——因為都是外送。
這叫用制度解決問題。
zh-tw.editorNote: 同居情侶點外送、看著空水槽時，傳給對方會心一笑。
variations: 同居後才懂，家事分工的最高境界不是輪流做，而是兩個人一起裝作沒看到，直到有客人要來。

en.content:
Our dish rule is totally fair: whoever cooks, the other one washes up.
So now neither of us cooks, and the dishes are always spotless — because it's all takeout.
We call this solving the problem with policy.
en.editorNote: Send to your partner while you're both staring at an empty sink over another delivery order.
variations: Turns out the peak of splitting chores isn't taking turns. It's both of you pretending not to see the mess until someone announces they're coming over.

items.sourceNote: 情侶吵架永遠是「她對我錯」梗，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
交往三年，我終於掌握了吵架必勝的方法：
第一步，承認我錯了。
第二步，沒有第二步。

這方法從來沒失敗過，因為它根本不是要贏，是要活著。
zh-tw.editorNote: 跟朋友聊感情、被問「你們都怎麼和好」時，最適合丟這句。
variations: 所謂成熟的關係，就是我學會在還沒搞清楚自己錯在哪之前，先說對不起。細節之後再慢慢查。

en.content:
Three years in, I've finally mastered the foolproof way to win an argument:
Step one, admit I'm wrong.
Step two, there is no step two.

It has never once failed, because it was never about winning. It's about surviving.
en.editorNote: Great to drop when a friend asks how you two always make up so fast.
variations: A mature relationship is when I've learned to say sorry before I even figure out what I did. I'll look into the details later.

items.sourceNote: 長期關係／笑同一個笑話幾百萬次梗，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
在一起久了才發現，感情長久的秘訣是：
同一個超冷的笑話，你有辦法笑出第三千次。

他每次經過廚房都要說那句一模一樣的爛梗，我每次都翻白眼，然後每次都笑。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合配一張和另一半的日常合照，寫給那個「爛梗永遠講不膩」的人。
variations: 熱戀是被對方逗笑，長久是明知道他要講什麼、還是被逗笑。

en.content:
Been together long enough to learn the real secret to lasting love:
being able to laugh at the exact same terrible joke for the three-thousandth time.

He says the same awful line every time he walks through the kitchen. I roll my eyes every time. And every time, I laugh.
en.editorNote: Pair with a candid photo of the two of you — for the person whose worst joke never gets old.
variations: Falling in love is being surprised into laughing. Staying in love is knowing exactly what he's about to say and laughing anyway.

items.sourceNote: 情侶購物清單／「家裡需要什麼」永無止盡梗，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
同居之後，我們的感情大概就是——
互相傳「家裡還缺什麼嗎」傳到其中一個人先走為止。

浪漫嗎？不浪漫。
但我人在超市，收到那句「順便買衛生紙」的時候，突然覺得這就是家。
zh-tw.editorNote: 在超市收到另一半補貨訊息時，拍張購物車配這句，土但真的甜。
variations: 所謂長相廝守，就是這輩子有一個人會固定問你「牛奶是不是快沒了」。

en.content:
After moving in together, our whole relationship is basically this:
texting each other "do we need anything from the store?" until one of us dies.

Romantic? Not really.
But standing in the aisle when "grab toilet paper too" comes through — that's when it hits me that this is home.
en.editorNote: Snap your cart when your partner's restock text arrives — cheesy, but genuinely sweet.
variations: Growing old together just means there's one person who'll ask you, for the rest of your life, whether you're out of milk.

items.sourceNote: 打呼／棉被爭奪戰梗，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
我：「你打呼吵到我了。」
他：「我沒有打呼，我是在模仿鯨魚的叫聲。」

我：「那你棉被也捲太多了。」
他：「我不是搶棉被，我是在幫我們保存溫暖。」
他保存的溫暖，剛好都在他那一邊。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合傳給那個晚上會把整條棉被捲走、還理直氣壯的另一半，附一張被搶到剩一角的自拍。
variations: 所謂同床共枕，就是每天晚上為了棉被的主權，安靜地打一場沒有贏家的仗。

en.content:
Me: "You're snoring again."
Him: "I'm not snoring, I'm practicing whale songs."

Me: "And you rolled up in the whole blanket."
Him: "I'm not stealing it, I'm preserving our warmth."
Funny how the warmth he preserves always ends up on his side.
en.editorNote: Send it to the partner who steals the entire blanket every night and somehow feels justified — bonus points for a selfie clutching your one remaining corner.
variations: Sharing a bed just means quietly fighting a nightly war over blanket sovereignty that nobody ever wins.

items.sourceNote: 同一沙發上互傳訊息梗，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
我們說好要好好溝通。
所以現在我們坐在同一張沙發上，中間隔了十公分——用傳訊息的。

「你要不要喝水？」
「要。」
沒有人起身。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合配一張兩個人並肩滑手機的照片，寫給那種「明明就在旁邊還要傳訊息」的懶散默契。
variations: 感情夠好的證明：你可以隔著半個沙發跟他傳「我肚子餓」，然後兩個人都繼續躺著，等對方先餓死。

en.content:
We promised we'd communicate more.
So now we sit on the same couch, four inches apart — and text each other.

"Want some water?"
"Sure."
Neither of us moves.
en.editorNote: Pair it with a photo of the two of you scrolling side by side — for the lazy telepathy of a couple who'd rather text than turn their heads.
variations: Real proof you're comfortable: you can text "I'm hungry" from the other end of the couch, and both of you just lie there waiting to see who starves first.

items.sourceNote: 冷氣／溫控主導權梗，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
愛情是什麼？
愛情就是願意把冷氣遙控器交給對方——有時候啦。

他說二十六度剛剛好，我說我快變冰棒。
最後我們的解決方案是：他二十六度，我一條薄被，各過各的冬天，共處同一個夏天。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合怕冷跟怕熱的組合，吵完溫度和好後拍張裹棉被配冷氣的照片，土但很真實。
variations: 所謂互相包容，就是我把冷氣調高兩度，他默默去拿了電風扇，誰也沒贏，但誰也沒走。

en.content:
What is love?
Love is being willing to hand over the AC remote… sometimes.

He swears 79°F is perfect; I'm turning into a popsicle.
Our final solution: he gets his 79, I get a thin blanket — two people living out separate winters inside the same summer.
en.editorNote: Perfect for the always-cold-plus-always-hot couple — snap a photo of you bundled up under the AC after making peace over the thermostat. Cheesy but true.
variations: Compromise is me nudging the AC up two degrees, him quietly going to grab a fan — nobody wins, but nobody walks out either.

items.sourceNote: 同一個爛笑話笑三百萬次／長期關係梗，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
有人問長久關係的秘訣是什麼。
我想了想：大概是有能力把同一個超難笑的笑話，聽第三百六十八萬次還笑得出來。

他的笑話沒有變好笑，是我變了。
變成那種、只要是他講的爛梗，就會笑的人。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合寫給交往很久的另一半，配一張你被他逗笑的照片，甜在「明知道很爛還是愛」。
variations: 熱戀是他很有趣，長久是他其實沒那麼有趣，但你已經自願被他逗一輩子了。

en.content:
Someone asked me the secret to a long-term relationship.
I thought about it: probably being able to laugh at the same terrible joke for the 3,680,000th time.

His jokes never got funnier. I changed.
I became the kind of person who laughs at anything, as long as he's the one saying it.
en.editorNote: Write it for a longtime partner, paired with a photo of you mid-laugh — the sweetness is loving a joke you know is bad.
variations: Early on, he was hilarious. Years later, he's honestly not that funny — but you've already signed up to be amused by him for life.

items.sourceNote: 另一半打呼／睡眠梗，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
我說：「你昨晚打呼超大聲。」
他一臉無辜：「不可能，我根本沒睡著。」

沒睡著的人，怎麼會發出那種可以震到牆壁的聲音？
這個世界上最堅定的信仰，就是「打呼的人堅信自己沒有打呼」。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合傳給總是否認自己打呼的另一半，可以配一段偷錄的呼聲音檔，甜在「吵到我，但我還是每天睡你旁邊」。
variations: 婚姻／長期關係就是：用一整晚的安靜，換一個會打呼的人陪你——有時候覺得不划算，但你從來沒真的想換。

en.content:
I said, "You were snoring so loud last night."
He looked genuinely offended: "Impossible. I never even fell asleep."

How does someone who never slept produce a sound that rattles the wall?
The strongest faith on Earth is a snorer's absolute belief that they don't snore.
en.editorNote: Send it to a partner who always denies snoring — bonus points for attaching a secretly recorded clip. The sweetness is 'you keep me up, and I still sleep next to you every night.'
variations: A long relationship is trading a whole night of silence for someone who snores next to you — some nights it feels like a bad deal, but you've never once actually wanted to trade back.

items.sourceNote: 情侶開車導航／後座指揮梗，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
開車出門，車上有兩個聲音在指路。
一個是 Google 導航，一個是坐在副駕的她。

導航說：「前方一百公尺右轉。」
她說：「你為什麼不現在就切過去？」
導航說：「重新規劃路線中。」
她說：「你看，連它都被你搞糊塗了。」

最後我學會了：導航可以關，副駕不能。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合傳給每次上車就變導航的另一半，配一張你們塞在路上的照片，甜在「吵歸吵，方向盤還是握在同一台車裡」。
variations: 情侶開車的終極默契：他假裝有在聽副駕指路，她假裝相信他真的有在聽，兩個人就這樣平安到了目的地。

en.content:
We're driving, and there are two voices giving directions.
One is Google Maps. The other is her, in the passenger seat.

Maps says: "In 100 meters, turn right."
She says: "Why aren't you merging over NOW?"
Maps says: "Recalculating."
She says: "See? Even it's confused by how you drive."

Eventually I learned: you can mute the GPS. You cannot mute the passenger seat.
en.editorNote: For the partner who turns into a second navigation system the moment they buckle up — pair it with a photo of you both stuck in traffic. The sweetness: you argue the whole way, but you're still in the same car.
variations: The real teamwork of a driving couple: he pretends to listen to the backseat directions, she pretends to believe he's listening — and somehow they arrive safe every time.

items.sourceNote: 情侶共用充電線／搶電梗，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
檢驗一段感情的方式，就是看剩最後一條充電線的時候，插頭會插在誰的手機上。

他百分之十八，我百分之九。
照理說應該我先充。
但我把線遞給了他，因為等等要靠他導航、訂位、查營業時間——
我犧牲的不是電量，是整個晚上的行程規劃權。

這不叫大方，這叫務實的愛情。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合傳給那個總是搶到充電線的另一半，配一張兩支手機共用一個插座的照片，甜在「電讓給你，但你哪都別想自己去」。
variations: 所謂互相，就是我把充電線給他，他默默把行動電源塞進我包包——沒有人說謝謝，但兩個人的手機都活到了回家。

en.content:
The real test of a relationship is who gets the last charger when there's only one left.

He's at 18%, I'm at 9%.
Technically, I should charge first.
But I handed him the cable — because I'm about to need him for directions, reservations, and store hours.
What I sacrificed wasn't battery. It was control over the entire evening's itinerary.

That's not generosity. That's pragmatic love.
en.editorNote: For the partner who always snags the charger first — pair it with a photo of two phones sharing one outlet. The sweetness: 'you can have the charge, but you're not going anywhere without me.'
variations: 'We're a team' means I hand him the charger, and he quietly slips a power bank into my bag — nobody says thank you, but both phones make it home alive.

items.sourceNote: 情侶傳訊息「對方正在輸入」焦慮梗，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
傳完一句「我們需要談談」，看著對方那三個跳動的點。

點點點出現了。
點點點消失了。
點點點又出現了。

在那十五秒裡，我腦內已經演完了我們的相遇、交往、爭吵、分手，還有我一個人搬家的畫面。

然後他回：「好啊，晚餐想吃什麼？」

——原來要談的是這個。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合傳給那個回訊息很慢、但你其實根本沒事的另一半，甜在「我小劇場演一齣，你只是在想晚餐」。
variations: 熱戀的三個點是心跳加速，長期關係的三個點是——反正他等等就會回，我先去洗個碗。

en.content:
I sent "we need to talk," then stared at those three dancing dots.

The dots appeared.
The dots vanished.
The dots came back.

In those fifteen seconds, my brain played out our whole story — how we met, dated, fought, broke up, and the scene of me moving out alone.

Then he replied: "Sure, what do you want for dinner?"

— Oh. That's what we needed to talk about.
en.editorNote: For the slow-to-reply partner when it turns out nothing was wrong at all — the sweetness is 'I filmed an entire drama in my head while you were just thinking about dinner.'
variations: In the honeymoon phase, the typing dots make your heart race. In a long relationship, the dots just mean — eh, he'll reply eventually, I'll go do the dishes first.

items.sourceNote: 情侶搶最後一口／偷吃對方食物梗，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
他說：「最後一塊給你吃。」
我心裡一暖，正要感謝他的體貼——
結果他補了一句：「因為我剛剛已經先吃了兩塊。」

所謂情侶之間的分享，就是：
你以為的禮讓，其實是他計算過的結果。
而你還是會笑著吃掉，因為被他算計，也是一種被放在心上。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合傳給那個總是把最後一口留給你、但自己早就先偷吃過的另一半，配一張分食甜點的照片，甜在「精打細算，但心裡有你」。
variations: 真正的信任，是我從來不數盤子裡剩幾塊，因為我知道就算他先偷吃，最後一口也一定會留給我。

en.content:
He said, "You can have the last piece."
My heart melted — I was about to thank him for being so thoughtful —
until he added: "Because I already had two while you weren't looking."

Sharing, in a relationship, means this:
what you thought was chivalry was actually his carefully calculated math.
And you eat it anyway, smiling — because being outsmarted by him is its own kind of being cared for.
en.editorNote: For the partner who always saves you the last bite but secretly snuck a few first — pair it with a photo of you splitting a dessert. The sweetness: 'sneaky math, but you're still on his mind.'
variations: Real trust is never counting how many pieces are left on the plate — because you know that even if he sneaks a few first, the last bite is always coming to you.

items.sourceNote: 「要不要買什麼」訊息梗，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
交往久了才發現，我們一整天的對話大概是這樣：

早上：「我出門了。」
中午：「吃飽沒？」
下午：「路過超市，家裡要買什麼嗎？」
晚上：「衛生紙沒了、還有你上次說的醬油。」
睡前：「晚安。」

沒有情話，沒有小作文，就是一連串的生活庶務。
但奇怪的是——如果哪天這幾則訊息沒出現，我會慌。
原來所謂的愛，後來就長成了：一個會問你家裡缺不缺衛生紙的人。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合傳給那個每天跟你報備行蹤、順路幫你補日用品的另一半，甜點在於「不浪漫的訊息，才是最穩的浪漫」。
variations: 熱戀期的訊息是『在幹嘛想你了』，老夫老妻的訊息是『垃圾車來了下去倒』——但我不會拿前者換後者，因為後者代表你真的住進了我的日子裡。

en.content:
After enough years together, I realized our entire daily conversation goes like this:

Morning: "Heading out."
Noon: "Did you eat?"
Afternoon: "Passing the store — need anything?"
Evening: "We're out of toilet paper, and that soy sauce you mentioned."
Bedtime: "Night."

No love notes, no paragraphs. Just a running list of household errands.
But here's the strange part — the day those texts don't show up, I panic.
Turns out love eventually becomes this: a person who checks whether you're out of toilet paper.
en.editorNote: For the partner who reports their whereabouts and restocks the essentials on the way home. The sweetness: 'the unromantic texts are the steadiest romance.'
variations: Honeymoon-phase texts are 'thinking about you.' Old-married texts are 'trash truck's here, take it down.' But I wouldn't trade the second for the first — because it means you actually live inside my days now.

items.sourceNote: 冷戰比誰先開口梗，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
冷戰的本質，是一場比賽：
看誰先忍不住開口。

第一小時：我絕對不會先低頭。
第三小時：他是不是根本不在乎？
第五小時：好餓，但我不能去廚房，去了就輸了。
第六小時：他傳來一則訊息——「你的份我幫你留了，在電鍋裡。」

——比賽結束。
沒有輸家，因為想讓對方吃飽這件事，本來就比輸贏重要。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合傳給那個冷戰時嘴硬、卻默默幫你留飯的另一半，甜在「你可以不理我，但我不准你餓到」。
variations: 冷戰最怕的不是對方兇，而是對方比你先笑出來——然後你也繃不住，兩個人氣到一半突然一起笑場,架就這樣不了了之。

en.content:
The silent treatment is, at its core, a competition:
who caves and speaks first.

Hour one: I am absolutely not apologizing first.
Hour three: Does he even care?
Hour five: I'm starving, but I can't go to the kitchen — that counts as losing.
Hour six: a text comes through — "Saved you a plate. It's in the rice cooker, still warm."

— Game over.
No losers, because wanting the other person fed was always bigger than winning.
en.editorNote: For the partner who stays stubborn during a standoff but quietly saves you dinner. The sweetness: 'you can ignore me, but I won't let you go hungry.'
variations: The scariest thing in a cold war isn't the other person getting angrier — it's them cracking a smile first, and then you can't hold it either, and you both dissolve into laughing halfway through the fight.

items.sourceNote: 情侶「妥協」梗，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
朋友問我：「你們感情這麼好，怎麼做到的？」
我說：「靠妥協啊。」
他很感動：「哇，兩個人互相退讓。」

我說：「不是。是我承認我錯了，然後她同意我錯了。」

——這就是我們的相處之道：
一個很會道歉，一個很會原諒。
分工明確，配合愉快。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合傳給那個吵架後總是你先開口道歉的另一半，或當作情侶帳號的幽默貼文，笑點在於『假裝的公平，其實是甘願』。
variations: 所謂門當戶對，就是：一個死不認錯，一個嘴上很兇但三分鐘就心軟——剛好湊成一對，誰也治不了誰，但誰也離不開誰。

en.content:
A friend asked me, "You two are so solid — what's your secret?"
I said, "Compromise."
He looked touched. "Wow, both of you giving a little."

I said, "Not quite. I admit I'm wrong, and then she agrees that I'm wrong."

— That's our whole system:
one of us is great at apologizing, one of us is great at forgiving.
Clear division of labor. Runs beautifully.
en.editorNote: For the partner who's always the first to say sorry after a fight, or as a humorous post for a couple's account. The joke: 'the fake fairness is actually willingness.'
variations: A perfect match is this: one who never admits fault, and one who talks tough but softens in three minutes — they cancel each other out perfectly. Neither can fix the other, but neither can leave either.

items.sourceNote: 「笑同一個笑話幾百萬次」梗，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
長久關係的秘訣，其實很簡單：
就是有辦法把同一個爛笑話，笑上三百八十萬次。

他那個冷到不行的諧音梗，我第一次聽翻白眼，第五十次還是翻白眼，
但翻到第三百次的時候，我發現——
我居然開始期待他講。

原來所謂合拍，不是兩個人都很有趣，
而是有一個人願意當永遠的觀眾，笑同一個梗笑到天長地久。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合傳給那個總愛講冷笑話、而你嘴上嫌棄卻默默記住每一個的另一半，甜在『你的爛梗，我聽一輩子也不膩』。
variations: 感情的保鮮期，取決於你們有沒有一套只有彼此聽得懂的內部梗——外人一臉問號，你們卻笑到不行,那個瞬間就是別人插不進來的默契。

en.content:
The secret to a lasting relationship is actually simple:
it's being able to laugh at the same terrible joke 3,820,000 times.

His painfully bad pun — I rolled my eyes the first time, still rolled my eyes the fiftieth time,
but somewhere around the three-hundredth time I realized —
I'd started looking forward to him telling it.

Turns out compatibility isn't two people both being funny.
It's one person willing to be the forever audience, laughing at the same joke till the end of time.
en.editorNote: For the partner who loves bad jokes while you pretend to hate them but secretly remember every one. The sweetness: 'your terrible jokes — I could hear them for a lifetime and never get bored.'
variations: A relationship's shelf life depends on whether you've built a set of inside jokes only the two of you understand — outsiders are baffled, but you're cracking up. That moment is the kind of closeness no one else can break into.

items.sourceNote: 「共用浴室、忍受對方毫無形象」梗，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
感情走到最深的階段，長這樣：

他一邊在洗手台旁邊喊「等我先放個屁再進來洗澡」，
我一邊回「好喔」，然後看著他，
心裡竟然還是覺得——這個人怎麼可以這麼可愛。

所謂親密，不是永遠維持最好看的樣子給對方看，
而是把最沒形象的一面攤開來，對方看了還願意牽你的手。
交往初期我們互相隱藏，交往久了我們互相認命，
認命到最後，居然變成一種安心。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合傳給那個在你面前徹底放飛、毫無包袱的另一半，甜點在『我最醜最沒形象的樣子你都收下了，這輩子跑不掉了』。
variations: 熱戀期：憋著不敢在對方面前打嗝。老夫老妻期：打完嗝還會轉頭問對方『幾分？』——這不是不愛了，是愛到不用演了。

en.content:
This is what the deepest stage of a relationship looks like:

He yells from the sink, "Let me rip one before you get in the shower,"
and I go, "Sure," and I look at him,
and somehow I still think — how is this person so adorable.

Intimacy isn't keeping your best face on for each other forever.
It's laying out your least flattering self and having them still want to hold your hand.
Early on we hide everything; years in we just accept each other,
and that acceptance somehow turns into feeling safe.
en.editorNote: For the partner who's fully unfiltered around you with zero shame. The sweetness: 'you've seen me at my ugliest and least dignified — I'm never getting away now.'
variations: Honeymoon phase: holding in your burp so they don't hear. Old-married phase: burping, then turning to ask 'how many points?' That's not the love fading — that's love that no longer needs to perform.

items.sourceNote: 「看著另一半睡覺覺得超愛→打呼瞬間破功」梗，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
半夜看著他睡臉的內心小劇場：

「天啊我怎麼會這麼愛這個人，他就是我的全部，我願意為他——」

（他打了一個震動全床的呼）

「……我沒辦法跟這個人過一輩子。」

三秒後他翻身安靜了，我又：
「……好啦，還是可以啦。」

愛情就是這樣，每個晚上在『我願意為你死』跟『我今天就想掐死你』之間，來回橫跳個八百次。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合傳給那個打呼超大聲、你嘴上抱怨卻還是每天睡在他旁邊的另一半，笑點在『愛與殺意，一線之隔，而且是同一個人』。
variations: 進階版：我戴上耳塞、翻到床的另一邊、拿枕頭蓋住頭，做了這麼多努力就是不肯去睡沙發——因為再吵，我還是想睡在你旁邊。

en.content:
My inner monologue watching him sleep at 2am:

"God, how do I love this man so much, he's my whole world, I would do anything for—"

(he lets out a snore that rattles the entire bed)

"...I cannot spend my life with this person."

Three seconds later he rolls over and goes quiet, and I'm back to:
"...okay, fine, I guess I still can."

That's love: bouncing eight hundred times a night between 'I would die for you' and 'I want to end you right now.'
en.editorNote: For the partner who snores like a chainsaw while you complain and still sleep beside them every night. The joke: 'love and murder — one thin line, same person.'
variations: Advanced version: I put in earplugs, rolled to the far side of the bed, buried my head under a pillow — did all of that specifically so I wouldn't have to go sleep on the couch. Because even at full volume, I still want to sleep next to you.

items.sourceNote: 「互相標記迷因＝現代情侶的情話」梗，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
我們這代情侶表達愛的方式：

凌晨一點，他標記我一則迷因，配字「這根本你」。
我看完標記另一則回他，配字「不，這才是你」。
就這樣一來一回傳到兩點，一句「晚安」都沒有，
但那一整串標記的意思都是——
「我看到好笑的東西，第一個想到的人是你。」

以前的人寫情書，我們傳迷因。
形式不一樣，核心一模一樣：
這世界上有件事我只想跟你分享，別人看了都不懂，只有你會笑。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合傳給那個總在深夜標記你一堆迷因的另一半，甜點在『看到好笑的第一個想到你，就是我這代人的我愛你』。
variations: 認證熱戀的標準：你們有一個誰都插不進來的私訊對話串，裡面九成是迷因、五成是只有你們懂的梗、還有零星幾句『在幹嘛』——外人看一頭霧水，你們笑到不行。

en.content:
How my generation of couples says 'I love you':

1am, he tags me in a meme captioned "this is literally you."
I look at it, tag him in another one back, captioned "no, THIS is you."
Back and forth till 2am, not a single "goodnight" exchanged,
but that whole thread of tags all means the exact same thing —
"I saw something funny and the first person I thought of was you."

People used to write love letters. We send memes.
Different format, identical core:
there's something in this world I only want to share with you — nobody else would get it, only you would laugh.
en.editorNote: For the partner who tags you in a pile of memes at midnight. The sweetness: 'you being the first person I think of when something's funny is my generation's I love you.'
variations: Certified in-love checklist: you have a DM thread no one else can break into — 90% memes, 50% inside jokes only you two understand, and a scattered few 'wyd's. Outsiders are baffled; you two are dying laughing.

items.sourceNote: 「情侶共享定位」現代日常梗，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
交往之後才懂的現代浪漫：共享定位。

不是為了查勤，是為了——
他下班移動了，我就知道差不多可以開始煮飯。
他的點停在超市，我立刻傳：「順便買醬油。」
他的點停在鹹酥雞攤前太久，我傳：「幫我加一份地瓜。」

表面上是科技監控，實際上是一種奇妙的安心：
地圖上那個小點在動，就代表這個人好好的、正在回家的路上，
而家裡有個人在等他，這件事本身就很值得。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合傳給那個跟你共享定位、彼此都很安心的另一半，甜點在『我看的不是你在哪，是你正在回我身邊的路上』。
variations: 反差萌版：共享定位最大的功能，其實是他的點停在你最愛的那家蛋糕店超過十分鐘時，你要非常努力地假裝不知道、假裝等一下被驚喜到。

en.content:
Modern romance you only understand after you're together: sharing your location.

Not to keep tabs — it's so that
when his dot starts moving from work, I know it's about time to start cooking.
His dot stops at the supermarket, I instantly text: "grab soy sauce while you're there."
His dot lingers a little too long at the fried-chicken stand, I text: "add a sweet potato for me."

On the surface it's tech surveillance; really it's a strange kind of comfort:
that little dot moving across the map means this person is okay and on their way home,
and someone being home waiting for them — that alone is worth a lot.
en.editorNote: For the partner you share your location with, where it's pure reassurance for both of you. The sweetness: 'I'm not watching where you are — I'm watching you make your way back to me.'
variations: Cute-contrast version: the real killer feature of shared location is when his dot parks at your favorite cake shop for over ten minutes and you have to try VERY hard to act clueless and pretend you'll be surprised later.

items.sourceNote: 共用密碼/串流帳號梗，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
交往之後才發現，感情最堅固的證據不是那些告白，
而是我知道你的 Netflix 密碼、你知道我的外送 App 帳號，
我們共用同一組 Wi-Fi、同一個購物車、同一段觀看紀錄。

分手最麻煩的從來不是心痛，
是要把糾纏在一起的十幾組帳號一個一個拆開。
所以有時候懶得吵架，某種程度上，也是一種愛。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合傳給那個已經跟你帳號綁死的另一半，配一句「我們現在分手成本太高了」，通常會換來一句「你也是」
variations: 現實版：真正的承諾不是戒指，是你在他家的追劇進度不敢亂點，怕跳掉他看到一半的那集。
反差版：吵到最兇的那次，最後和好的原因是新一季上架了，兩個人默默坐回沙發同一側。

en.content:
After you've been together a while, you realize the strongest proof of love isn't the confessions —
it's that I know your Netflix password and you know my food-delivery login,
we share one Wi-Fi, one shopping cart, one tangled watch history.

The worst part of a breakup was never the heartbreak.
It's untangling fourteen intertwined accounts one by one.
So sometimes not bothering to fight is, in its own way, a kind of love.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner whose accounts are already fused with yours, with 'our breakup cost is way too high now' — you'll usually get 'same' back
variations: Real-life version: true commitment isn't a ring, it's not daring to touch the show on his profile in case you knock him out of the episode he paused halfway.
Contrast version: the worst fight ended the moment a new season dropped, and both of you quietly slid back onto the same side of the couch.

items.sourceNote: 「還需要買什麼」訊息循環梗，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
有人問長期關係到底長什麼樣子。
其實就是兩個人一輩子在傳同一則訊息：
「你在超市嗎？家裡還需要買什麼？」

「衛生紙好像快沒了。」
「牛奶。」
「還有你昨天說要吃的那個，我忘記叫什麼了。」
「我也忘了。」
「那算了下次再說。」

然後下次，兩個人又會在同一包快沒了的衛生紙前面，重新想起這件事。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合傳給正在超市的另一半，直接複製第一句就好，對方會秒懂這是日常的開場白
variations: 升級版：他傳「還要買什麼」，你回一長串，他到家只帶了其中一樣，理由是「其他的我覺得家裡應該還有」。
溫馨版：吵完架冷戰中，破冰的第一句往往不是道歉，是「你要不要喝奶茶，我順路。」

en.content:
Someone asked what a long-term relationship actually looks like.
Honestly it's two people texting each other the same message for the rest of their lives:
"Are you at the store? Do we need anything?"

"I think we're low on toilet paper."
"Milk."
"And that thing you said you wanted yesterday, I forget what it's called."
"I forget too."
"Never mind, next time."

And next time, standing in front of the same nearly-empty roll, you'll both remember all over again.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who's at the store right now — just copy the first line; they'll instantly know it's the opening move of everyday life
variations: Escalated version: he texts 'what do we need,' you send a whole list, he comes home with one item because 'I figured we probably already had the rest.'
Sweet version: mid-silent-treatment, the first icebreaker is rarely an apology — it's 'you want a milk tea? I'm passing by anyway.'

items.sourceNote: 「如果我變成蟲你還愛我嗎」荒謬假設題梗，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
「如果有一天我變成一隻蟲，你還會愛我嗎？」
這種問題的重點從來不是答案，
是你敢不敢在被窩裡認真思考一隻蟲的感受，然後回她：

「會啊，但我要先確認一下你是哪種蟲，如果會咬人我可能要重新想。」

她笑了，你就贏了。
感情裡最珍貴的能力，是把一個沒有意義的問題，接得比誰都認真。
zh-tw.editorNote: 半夜另一半突然丟你一個無厘頭假設題的時候用，重點是別急著給標準答案，先陪她把這個蟲的設定認真演完
variations: 追加版：「那如果我是一隻很醜的蟲呢？」「醜的更要愛啊，不然你被別的蟲嫌棄怎麼辦。」
反向版：換你問她同樣的問題，她想了三秒說「看那天心情」，然後你們為了一隻不存在的蟲，認真爭執了十分鐘。

en.content:
"If one day I turned into a worm, would you still love me?"
The point of this question was never the answer.
It's whether you'll lie there under the covers and seriously consider the feelings of a worm, then say:

"Yeah, but I need to know what kind of worm first — if you bite, I might have to reconsider."

She laughs, you win.
The rarest skill in a relationship is fielding a completely pointless question more seriously than anyone else would.
en.editorNote: For when your partner lobs a random hypothetical at midnight — the move is not to give the 'correct' answer, but to seriously play out the whole worm scenario with them
variations: Follow-up version: 'What if I were a really ugly worm?' 'Ugly ones need it more — otherwise what if the other worms judge you?'
Reverse version: you ask her the same thing, she thinks for three seconds and says 'depends on my mood that day,' and then you both seriously argue for ten minutes over a worm that doesn't exist.

items.sourceNote: 兩人各自滑手機的陪伴梗，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
外人以為熱戀是整晚聊不完的話。
交往久了才知道，最舒服的一種狀態，
是兩個人躺在同一張沙發上，各自滑各自的手機，一句話都沒有。

偶爾其中一個人「欸你看這個」把螢幕轉過去，
另一個瞄一眼「喔哈哈」，然後又各自滑回去。

不用一直找話題，也不會覺得尷尬，
這種安靜不是沒話講，是終於可以不用講話了。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合傳給那個可以跟你安靜待在一起的人，配一句「這就是我們」，通常對方正躺在你旁邊滑手機，會抬頭看你三秒再繼續滑
variations: 細節版：真正的默契是他滑到好笑的東西會忍不住笑出聲，你不用問就知道等一下手機一定會轉過來。
反差版：唯一會打破這種安靜的，是其中一個人肚子餓了，於是話題自然進入「那我們要吃什麼」的世紀難題。

en.content:
Outsiders think the honeymoon phase is talking all night and never running out of things to say.
After a while together you learn the comfiest state of all
is two people on the same couch, each scrolling their own phone, not a word between them.

Every so often one goes 'hey, look at this' and turns the screen over,
the other glances 'oh, haha,' and they both scroll back into their own worlds.

No need to keep hunting for topics, no awkwardness either.
This quiet isn't running out of things to say — it's finally being allowed not to say anything.
en.editorNote: Send to the person you can sit in silence with, labeled 'this is us' — odds are they're scrolling next to you right now and will look up for three seconds before going back to it
variations: Detail version: real chemistry is him snorting at something funny, and you already know the phone is about to swing your way without having to ask.
Contrast version: the only thing that breaks this silence is one of you getting hungry, which naturally escalates into the eternal 'so what do we want to eat' crisis.

items.sourceNote: 冷氣/棉被溫度戰爭梗，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
同床之後才發現，感情裡最難調解的衝突，
不是價值觀，也不是家事分工，
是冷氣要開幾度。

一個嫌熱，非二十四度不睡；
一個怕冷，蓋著棉被還要穿襪子。
最後的和平協議往往是：冷氣照他要的開，棉被全部歸我，
中間那條看不見的分界線，誰也不准越界。

能為了一度僵持半小時，又願意各退半度，這大概就是同居的真諦。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合傳給那個跟你搶冷氣遙控器的另一半，配一句「今晚二十五度，這是我最後的底線」，通常會被回「二十四，不然你自己去客廳睡」
variations: 談判版：最高境界是一人一條被，冷氣開折衷的溫度，怕冷的那個默默把腳伸到怕熱那個的腿上取暖。
反差版：吵完溫度、關燈、背對背，五分鐘後兩個人還是不知不覺靠在一起，因為人再怎麼講原則，還是敵不過想靠近的本能。

en.content:
Only after sharing a bed do you discover the hardest conflict in a relationship to mediate
isn't values, and it isn't the division of chores —
it's what temperature to set the AC.

One runs hot, can't sleep above seventy-five degrees;
the other runs cold, wears socks under the blanket.
The peace treaty usually goes: AC set his way, blanket entirely mine,
and that invisible border down the middle of the bed — no one is allowed to cross it.

Standing your ground over one degree, then each giving up half a degree — that's pretty much the essence of living together.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner you fight over the AC remote with, labeled 'seventy-six tonight, that's my final line' — expect 'seventy-five, or go sleep on the couch' in reply
variations: Negotiation version: peak evolution is one blanket each and a compromise temperature, while the cold one quietly tucks their feet against the hot one's leg for warmth.

Contrast version: you fight over the temperature, turn off the light, lie back to back — and five minutes later you've drifted together anyway, because no matter how firm your principles, they never beat the instinct to be close.

items.sourceNote: 情侶明明在旁邊還互傳迷因，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
我們坐在同一張沙發上，中間隔不到二十公分。

他突然「噗」地笑出來，然後手機一震——我收到他傳來的一支影片。
我看完也笑了，回他一個表情，沒有抬頭。
他也沒抬頭。

我媽問我：「你們在一起都聊什麼？」
我想了想，說：「互相轉貼。」

她一臉困惑。她不懂，那些我們捨不得自己笑完的東西，第一個想到的就是對方。
近到可以直接講出來，卻還是選擇傳給你——
因為有些好笑的事，就是要「跟你一起知道」才算數。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合配一張兩個人並肩滑手機的照片，寫給那種「明明就在旁邊還要傳訊息」的懶散默契，甜在「看到好笑的第一個想到你」
variations: 升級版：兩支手機共用一條充電線，各滑各的，但每隔幾分鐘就會有一個人把螢幕轉過來說「你看這個」。這叫做各自安靜，一起熱鬧。
反差版：吵架冷戰中，還是忍不住把一支超好笑的影片傳過去，對方回一個「哈」，冷戰就這樣不小心結束了。迷因是我們之間最有效的和平協議。

en.content:
We're on the same couch, less than a foot apart.

He suddenly snorts, then my phone buzzes — he's sent me a video.
I watch it, laugh, send back an emoji. Neither of us looks up.

My mom asks, "What do you two even talk about all day?"
I think about it and say, "We forward each other stuff."

She looks confused. She doesn't get it — whenever something's too good to laugh at alone, the first person we think of is each other.
Close enough to just say it out loud, and we still choose to send it —
because some funny things only count once you know them together.
en.editorNote: Pair with a photo of the two of you side by side on your phones — for the couple whose love language is 'you're right there but I'll still text you this'
variations: Upgraded version: two phones sharing one charger, each scrolling separately, but every few minutes one turns the screen over and says 'look at this.' Quiet apart, alive together.

Contrast version: mid-fight, mid-silent-treatment, one of us still can't resist forwarding a truly funny clip. The other replies 'lol,' and just like that the cold war is over. Memes are our most effective peace treaty.

items.sourceNote: 「你喜歡一個禮拜哪一天」土味情話反被將軍，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
我想撒嬌，鋪了一個梗。

我：「你猜，一個禮拜裡我最喜歡哪一天？」
他：「星期六吧，可以睡到中午。」
我：「不對，再猜。」
他：「發薪日？」
我：「不對啦，是有你的每一天。」

他愣了兩秒，然後說：
「所以你答案早就想好了，還要我猜三次？」
我：「……你就不能配合一下嗎。」
他：「我配合了啊，我猜錯兩次讓你有機會講。」

——原來土味情話最大的敵人，不是尷尬，是一個太懂你套路的人。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合傳給那個總是拆穿你小心機、卻還是願意陪你演完的另一半，甜在「知道你要撒嬌，先讓你贏」
variations: 反向版：換他問我最喜歡哪一天，我直接說「有你的每一天」，他說「抄我的」，我說「好的東西本來就要抄」。
高階版：交往久了土味情話會進化成——他一開口「你猜」，我就直接說「有你的每一天，可以了吧」，然後兩個人一起笑場。默契就是連肉麻都能同步。

en.content:
I wanted to be cute, so I set up the bit.

Me: "Guess which day of the week is my favorite?"
Him: "Saturday? You get to sleep till noon."
Me: "Nope, guess again."
Him: "Payday?"
Me: "No — it's every day I'm with you."

He pauses two seconds, then goes:
"So you had the answer ready the whole time and still made me guess twice?"
Me: "...Can you not just play along?"
Him: "I did play along. I missed twice on purpose so you'd get to say it."

— Turns out the biggest enemy of a cheesy line isn't embarrassment. It's someone who knows your moves too well.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who always sees your little schemes coming but plays out the scene anyway — sweet because they let you win before you even start
variations: Reverse version: he asks which day is my favorite, I say 'every day with you,' he says 'you stole my line,' I say 'good things are meant to be stolen.'

Advanced version: after enough years, the cheesy line evolves — the second he says 'guess,' I cut in with 'every day with you, we good?' and we both crack up. Real chemistry is when even the corny stuff is synchronized.

items.sourceNote: 老夫老妻嘴上嫌棄實則寵溺，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
交往久了，情話的形狀會變。

剛在一起時他說：「你是我看過最漂亮的女生。」
現在他說：「你最近好像有胖……不過沒關係，反正胖在我心上，跑不掉。」

剛在一起時他說：「你笑起來像陽光。」
現在他說：「你這個笑聲真的很吵，全公車都聽得到——但我就是喜歡聽。」

剛在一起時他小心翼翼。
現在他嘴巴很欠，但每句嫌棄的後半段，都藏著一個「還是愛你」。

——嘴巴說著缺點，其實全世界最捨不得的就是你。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合寫給那個天天虧你、卻把最軟的話藏在最後半句的另一半，甜在「嫌你嫌得那麼熟練，是因為早就把你放心裡了」
variations: 反向版：換我虧他「你頭髮是不是又少了」，他說「少的都長到我對你的耐心上了」，我瞬間輸掉這場鬥嘴。
情境版：他一邊嫌我煮的菜太鹹，一邊把整盤吃光。嘴巴誠實，行動更誠實。

en.content:
The longer you're together, the more love talk changes shape.

Early on he said, "You're the prettiest girl I've ever seen."
Now he says, "You've put on a little weight lately... but it's fine, it all landed in my heart, so you can't escape."

Early on he said, "Your smile is like sunshine."
Now he says, "That laugh is so loud the whole bus can hear it — and I love the sound of it anyway."

Early on he was careful.
Now his mouth is a menace, but the back half of every insult is hiding an 'I still love you.'

— The mouth lists your flaws, while the heart can't bear to let you go.
en.editorNote: For the partner who roasts you daily but tucks the softest line into the very end — sweet because the fluency of the teasing means you've been in their heart all along
variations: Reverse version: I tease him, 'losing more hair up there?' and he says, 'it all grew into my patience for you' — and just like that I lose the argument.
Scene version: he complains my cooking is too salty, then finishes the entire plate. The mouth is honest; the actions are more honest.

items.sourceNote: 同居水槽裡的碗是一種沉默的角力，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
同居之後才知道，水槽裡那疊沒洗的碗，是一場沒有裁判的比賽。

規則很簡單：誰先受不了，誰就輸。

第一天，碗放著。
第二天，碗還在，我瞄它一眼，走開。
第三天，他經過水槽，停頓了一下——我屏住呼吸——結果他只是拿了一個乾淨的杯子倒水。
第四天，我實在受不了了，捲起袖子開始洗。

他從房間探出頭：「你在洗碗喔？我正打算等等要洗耶。」
我：「你這句話已經講第四天了。」
他：「所以你看，我很有耐心。」

——長久關係的真相是：不是誰比較勤勞，是誰的忍耐度比較低。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合傳給那個跟你在水槽前互相耗著的同居對象，配一張堆滿碗的水槽照片，寫「這局又是我先投降」，真實到會心一笑
variations: 升級版：後來我們買了洗碗機。它最大的功勞不是洗碗，是終結了那場我們誰都不肯認輸的冷戰。科技真的能拯救感情。
反差版：有次我出差三天，回來水槽乾乾淨淨。原來他不是不會洗，是平常在等我先動手——而想我的時候，連洗碗都變得心甘情願。

en.content:
Only after moving in together do you learn that the stack of dishes in the sink is a game with no referee.

The rule is simple: whoever cracks first, loses.

Day one, the dishes sit there.
Day two, still there. I glance at them and walk away.
Day three, he pauses at the sink — I hold my breath — and then just grabs a clean cup for some water.
Day four, I finally can't take it, roll up my sleeves, and start washing.

He pokes his head out of the bedroom: "Oh, you're doing the dishes? I was totally about to get to those."
Me: "That's the fourth day you've said that."
Him: "See? That's how patient I am."

— The truth about long relationships: it's not about who's more diligent. It's about whose tolerance is lower.
en.editorNote: Send to the roommate-slash-partner you have a standoff with at the sink — pair it with a photo of a full sink and 'I surrendered first again,' true enough to earn a knowing laugh
variations: Upgraded version: eventually we bought a dishwasher. Its greatest achievement wasn't washing dishes — it was ending the cold war neither of us would concede. Technology really can save a relationship.

Contrast version: once I was away for three days, and came back to a spotless sink. Turns out he can wash them fine — he was just waiting for me to move first. And when he missed me, even the dishes got done willingly.

items.sourceNote: 選片半小時、一開播對方就睡著梗，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
選片流程：
打開串流平台，滑了四十分鐘。
「這部？」「看過了。」
「那這部？」「太長。」
「這部評價很好。」「不想動腦。」
終於選好一部，兩個人都同意，鬆一口氣按下播放。

片頭跑完，第八分鐘，我轉頭問：「你覺得那個男主角是不是有問題？」

沒有回答。
他睡著了。

——我們花四十分鐘選的片，他只撐了八分鐘。而我還要一個人把它看完，明天再假裝我們一起看過。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個每次都說「我沒睡我只是閉眼休息」的另一半，配一句「第八分鐘就休息了喔」，準確到讓對方無法反駁
variations: 進階版：後來我學乖了，選片直接選他一定會睡著的紀錄片，這樣我就能名正言順地霸佔遙控器切成我想看的。選片權，是用他的睡意換來的。
反轉版：有次換我先睡著，醒來發現他默默把片子暫停，等我。原來我們都在等對方睡醒，只是從來不承認。

en.content:
The movie-picking process:
Open the streaming app, scroll for forty minutes.
"This one?" "Seen it."
"This?" "Too long."
"This has great reviews." "Don't want to think that hard."
Finally we agree on one, both relieved, and hit play.

Eight minutes in, I turn and ask, "Do you think the lead guy is kind of sketchy?"

No answer.
He's asleep.

— Forty minutes to pick it, eight minutes to lose him. And now I have to finish it alone and pretend tomorrow that we watched it together.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who always insists 'I wasn't sleeping, I was just resting my eyes' — add 'you started resting at minute eight,' too accurate to argue with
variations: Advanced version: eventually I got smart and started picking documentaries he'd definitely doze off to, so I could rightfully hijack the remote and switch to what I actually wanted. Remote rights, purchased with his drowsiness.

Twist version: one night I fell asleep first, and woke to find he'd quietly paused the movie and waited for me. Turns out we're both just waiting for the other to wake up — we've simply never admitted it.

items.sourceNote: 組裝家具是感情的終極測驗、少一根螺絲梗，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
都說旅行是感情的試金石。
錯了，是一起組一個櫃子。

第十分鐘：「說明書上那個是A還是B？」「你看不懂圖嗎？」
第二十五分鐘：「這根螺絲哪裡來的？剛剛不是全用完了？」
第四十分鐘：整個櫃子裝反了，要全部拆掉重來。
第五十分鐘：兩個人背對背坐在地上，中間隔著一堆木板，誰都不說話。

最後櫃子站起來了。它有點歪，右邊那扇門關不太起來。
但我們也還站著，沒散。

——能一起熬過少一根螺絲的下午，大概真的能一起熬過很多事。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個跟你在客廳地板上大眼瞪小眼組過家具的人，配一句「那個歪掉的櫃子還在用喔」，苦中帶甜
variations: 進階版：後來我們立了規矩——組家具一人負責看說明書，一人負責動手，中途不准互相指導。感情，是靠劃清權責保住的。
反轉版：多出來的那根螺絲，我們留著沒丟。它現在放在抽屜裡，像個紀念品——紀念我們差點為了一個櫃子分手，最後卻沒有。

en.content:
They say travel is the true test of a relationship.
Wrong. It's assembling one cabinet together.

Minute ten: "Is that piece A or B in the diagram?" "Can you not read a picture?"
Minute twenty-five: "Where did this screw come from? Didn't we just use them all?"
Minute forty: the whole thing is backwards and has to come apart again.
Minute fifty: we're sitting back-to-back on the floor, a pile of boards between us, neither one talking.

In the end the cabinet stands. It leans a little, and the right door won't quite close.
But we're still standing too. Still together.

— If you can survive an afternoon of one missing screw together, you can probably survive a lot together.
en.editorNote: Send to the person you once stared down across the living-room floor mid-assembly, with 'that crooked cabinet is still in use, by the way' — bittersweet and true
variations: Advanced version: afterward we made a rule — one person reads the manual, one person builds, no coaching from the sidelines. Love, preserved by a clear division of labor.
Twist version: we kept the leftover screw instead of tossing it. It lives in a drawer now, like a souvenir — a reminder that we almost broke up over a cabinet, and somehow didn't.

items.sourceNote: 情侶對天氣預報／要不要帶傘的分歧梗，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
出門前的天氣攻防：

我：「氣象App說今天下午會下雨，帶傘。」
他：「你看窗外，大晴天，帶什麼傘。」
我：「App說降雨機率百分之七十。」
他：「App又不是神。」

結果下午暴雨，他淋成落湯雞回來。

我沒有說「我早就跟你說了」。
我只是默默把玄關那把沒被帶走的傘，遞到他面前。

他看了傘一眼，看了我一眼，說：「……App運氣好而已。」

——在一起最難的不是誰對，是對的那個人要忍住不說「我就說吧」。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個永遠不信天氣預報、寧願淋雨也不肯帶傘的另一半，附一張傘的照片，勝過千言萬語
variations: 進階版：後來我學會了，不再跟他爭，直接在他包包側袋塞一把折疊傘，不說話。他淋不到雨，我也贏了——這叫做無聲的勝利。
反向版：也有他對的時候。有次我堅持帶傘，結果一整天大太陽，那把傘我扛了一天。他一句話都沒說，只是笑得很欠揍。

en.content:
The pre-departure weather standoff:

Me: "The weather app says rain this afternoon. Bring an umbrella."
Him: "Look out the window — clear skies. No umbrella needed."
Me: "App says seventy percent chance of rain."
Him: "The app isn't a prophet."

Come afternoon: downpour. He comes home soaked to the bone.

I don't say 'I told you so.'
I just quietly pick up the umbrella he left by the door and hold it out to him.

He looks at the umbrella. Looks at me. Says, "…The app just got lucky."

— The hardest part of being together isn't being right. It's being right and biting your tongue on 'told you so.'
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who never trusts the forecast and would rather get drenched than carry an umbrella — attach a photo of an umbrella, worth a thousand words
variations: Advanced version: eventually I stopped arguing and just slid a folding umbrella into his bag's side pocket without a word. He stays dry, and I still win — call it the silent victory.

Reverse version: he gets it right sometimes too. Once I insisted on the umbrella, and it was blazing sun all day — I lugged that thing around for hours. He didn't say a word, just wore the most punchable little smile.

items.sourceNote: 情侶暱稱越叫越荒謬、忘記本名梗，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
情侶暱稱的演化史：

第一個月：「寶貝。」
第三個月：「寶。」
第半年：「豬。」
第一年：「欸你這隻笨鵝。」
第兩年：一種介於狗叫和嬰兒音之間、外人完全聽不懂的聲音。

有天他媽打電話來問：「你們平常都怎麼叫對方？」
我愣了三秒，才想起來——原來他有個正常的名字，而我已經很久很久沒叫過了。

——暱稱土到爆，肉麻到自己都受不了，但那是只屬於我們兩個人的語言，全世界只有一個人聽得懂。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個你早就忘記怎麼叫他本名的另一半，附上你們最新的那個荒謬暱稱，讓對方笑著承認「對啦就是這樣」
variations: 進階版：我們的暱稱多到可以編字典，每一個背後都有一個只有我們懂的故事。外人聽了一頭霧水，我們卻能因為一個詞就笑到不行。
反轉版：吵架的時候最明顯——一喊出對方的本名加全名，兩個人都會愣一下，然後莫名其妙就笑場了。原來連生氣都被暱稱破壞了。

en.content:
The evolution of couple nicknames:

Month one: "Baby."
Month three: "Babe."
Month six: "Dork."
Year one: "Hey, you absolute goose."
Year two: some sound halfway between a puppy noise and baby talk that no outsider could possibly decode.

One day his mom called and asked, "So what do you two actually call each other?"
I froze for three seconds before remembering — oh right, he has a normal name, and I haven't used it in a very, very long time.

— The nicknames are painfully cheesy, embarrassing even to us, but they're a language that belongs to just the two of us. Only one person in the whole world understands it.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner whose real name you've long forgotten how to say, with your latest ridiculous nickname attached, so they laugh and admit 'yeah, that's the one'
variations: Advanced version: we have enough nicknames to fill a dictionary, each with a story only we get. Outsiders are baffled; we crack up over a single word.

Twist version: it's most obvious during a fight — the second one of us uses the other's full legal name, we both freeze, and then somehow burst out laughing. Turns out even our anger got sabotaged by the nicknames.

items.sourceNote: 情侶一起做手作／DIY結果後悔梗，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
上禮拜我們一時興起，決定一起做一個「情侶手作」——網路上看那些影片，兩個人邊做邊笑，成品美得像雜誌。

我們買了材料。

第一步就黏歪了。
第二步材料不夠。
第三步兩個人為了「到底要不要重來」冷戰了半小時。
第四步成品出爐，長得像小學生的美勞作業，還是被狗踩過的那種。

本來是「情侶手作」，現在是「我們到底為什麼要做這個」。

——那個成品我們捨不得丟，就擺在櫃子上。它很醜，但它是我們一起搞砸的，所以特別可愛。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個跟你一起做過某個災難級手作的另一半，附上成品照片，配一句「還記得這個嗎」，保證引發爆笑回憶殺
variations: 進階版：後來朋友來家裡，指著那個東西問「這是什麼」，我們兩個異口同聲說「別問」，然後相視而笑。有些黑歷史，是兩個人的默契。
反轉版：雖然做得超爛，但那個下午我們笑了整整三個小時。成品是垃圾，過程是寶藏——原來重點從來不是做出什麼，是跟誰一起做。

en.content:
Last week, on a whim, we decided to do a 'couples craft project' — you know, the kind in those videos where two people laugh their way through it and the result looks magazine-worthy.

We bought the materials.

Step one: glued it crooked.
Step two: ran out of materials.
Step three: half an hour of cold war over whether to start over.
Step four: the finished product looks like a grade-schooler's art assignment — the kind a dog stepped on.

It started as a 'couples craft.'
Now it's a 'why on earth did we make this.'

— We can't bring ourselves to throw it out, so it sits on the shelf. It's ugly, but it's the thing we ruined together, which makes it strangely lovable.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner you once attempted a disaster-level craft with, attach a photo of the result and 'remember this?' — guaranteed to trigger a laughing wave of nostalgia
variations: Advanced version: later a friend came over, pointed at the thing, and asked 'what is that?' We answered in unison — 'don't ask' — and grinned at each other. Some shameful history is a two-person inside joke.

Twist version: it came out terrible, but that afternoon we laughed for three straight hours. The product was trash, the process was treasure — turns out it was never about what you make, but who you make it with.

items.sourceNote: 情侶「如果我變成一隻蟲你還愛我嗎」荒謬假設問題梗，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
「如果我變成一隻蟲，你還會愛我嗎？」
「會啊。」
「真的？」
「會，我會把你養在陽台的盆栽裡，每天跟你說早安。」
「那如果我變成一顆石頭呢？」
「那就放口袋裡帶你去上班。」
「那如果我變成一朵雲？」
「你可不可以先變回一個正常會回訊息的人——我剛剛問你晚餐吃什麼，問了三次。」

——情侶的深夜對話，永遠會從哲學問題，一路滑坡到晚餐問題。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個半夜總愛丟你一堆荒謬假設題的另一半，配一句「今天想把我變成什麼」，等著看對方認真回你一個更荒謬的答案
variations: 進階版：後來我學乖了，他問「如果我變成一隻蟲」，我直接回「那我就變成另一隻蟲陪你」。他愣了三秒，然後說「……你贏了」。原來這種問題的正確答案，從來不是邏輯，是願不願意跟他一起發瘋。
反轉版：換我問他「如果我變成一隻蟲你還愛我嗎」，他秒回「不會」。我正要生氣，他補一句「因為我會怕，但我會把你放進罐子裡帶著走，一輩子」。土到爆，但我原諒他了。

en.content:
"If I turned into a worm, would you still love me?"
"Yeah."
"Really?"
"Yeah, I'd keep you in the pot on the balcony and say good morning to you every day."
"What if I turned into a rock?"
"Then I'd put you in my pocket and take you to work."
"What if I turned into a cloud?"
"Can you turn back into a person who actually replies to texts first — I've asked you what's for dinner three times now."

— Late-night couple conversations always slide from philosophy straight down to dinner.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who ambushes you with absurd hypotheticals at midnight, with 'what are you turning me into today?' — then wait for them to answer with something even more absurd, dead seriously
variations: Advanced version: eventually I got smart. He asked 'if I turned into a worm—' and I cut in with 'then I'd turn into a worm and keep you company.' He froze for three seconds, then said '...you win.' Turns out the right answer was never logic — it was whether you're willing to be ridiculous alongside them.

Twist version: I asked him 'if I turned into a worm would you still love me,' and he instantly said 'no.' Before I could get mad, he added, 'because I'd be scared — but I'd put you in a jar and carry you around for the rest of my life.' Painfully cheesy. Forgiven.

items.sourceNote: 情侶同居後對『正確做家事方式』的堅持差異，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
「碗不是這樣洗的。」
「我洗得很乾淨啊。」
「你看這個角度，油根本沒沖到。」
「那你來洗。」
「我不是要洗，我是在教你正確的方法。」
「這世界上有一千種洗碗的方法，你偏偏覺得只有你那種是對的。」
「因為只有我那種是對的。」

——同居之後才發現，原來每個人心裡都有一本《正確做家事聖經》，而且每一本都認定對方是異教徒。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個總是嫌你家事做得不夠標準的另一半，配一句「聖經第幾頁說的」，把嚴肅的家務糾紛變成兩個人都笑得出來的梗
variations: 進階版：後來我們達成協議——誰洗碗，誰就是那一餐的洗碗權威，另一個人不准評論。結果他洗碗時我閉嘴，我洗碗時他閉嘴，家裡瞬間和平。原來很多吵架，本質是搶「話事權」。
反轉版：有天我照他的方法洗了一次，他站在旁邊看，看到一半突然說「其實你原本那樣也可以啦」。我：「你三年前不是說不行？」他：「那是三年前的我，比較不成熟。」——原來人是會變的，只要吵夠久。

en.content:
"That's not how you wash dishes."
"They're perfectly clean."
"Look at this angle — the grease never even got rinsed."
"Then you do it."
"I don't want to do it, I'm teaching you the correct way."
"There are a thousand ways to wash a dish, and you've decided only yours is right."
"Because only mine is right."

— After moving in together, you discover everyone carries their own private Bible of Correct Housekeeping — and every edition brands the other person a heretic.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who's always grading your chores, with 'which page of the Bible says that?' — turning a tense household dispute into a joke you can both laugh at
variations: Advanced version: eventually we made a rule — whoever washes the dishes is the dishwashing authority for that meal, and the other isn't allowed to comment. Now I shut up when he washes and he shuts up when I wash, and the apartment is suddenly at peace. Turns out most fights are really about who gets the final say.

Twist version: one day I washed them his way while he watched. Halfway through he suddenly said, 'honestly, your way was fine too.' Me: 'Three years ago you said it wasn't.' Him: 'That was three-years-ago me. Less mature.' Turns out people do change — you just have to argue long enough.

items.sourceNote: 情侶訊息紀錄全是採購清單、沒有情話的梗，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
我們的訊息紀錄，大概長這樣：

「衛生紙沒了。」
「好。」
「洗衣精也快沒。」
「好。」
「牛奶只剩一點。」
「順便買。」

沒有早安，沒有晚安，沒有一句「我想你」。
只有無止盡的採購清單。

有天朋友滑到我們的對話，問：「你們是室友還是情侶？」
我想了想，回她——
「是情侶啊。我們只是早就過了要說『我想你』的階段。現在我想你的方式，是記得幫你買你愛喝的那款、而且一定要冰的、無糖的豆漿。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個跟你訊息紀錄裡全是待辦事項的另一半，配一句「這就是我們的情話」，甜點在於『把日常瑣事記牢，就是最不浪漫的浪漫』
variations: 進階版：後來我特地在採購清單中間插一句「我想你」，他回「衛生紙記得買雙層的」。我以為他沒看到，結果那天他下班買了一束花回來，卡片上寫「我也是，只是不好意思打字」。
反轉版：分開旅行那幾天，訊息突然變得很肉麻，每句話都是想念。回到同居生活的第一天，訊息立刻打回原形——「垃圾今天要倒。」原來距離真的會產生美感，近了就只剩家事。

en.content:
Our text history looks roughly like this:

"Out of toilet paper."
"Okay."
"Almost out of detergent."
"Okay."
"Milk's running low."
"I'll grab it."

No good mornings. No good nights. Not a single 'I miss you.'
Just an endless shopping list.

One day a friend scrolled through our chat and asked, "Are you two roommates or a couple?"
I thought about it, then replied —
"A couple. We just passed the stage of saying 'I miss you' a long time ago. These days, the way I miss you is remembering to buy the exact soy milk you like — the sugar-free one, and it has to be cold."
en.editorNote: Send to the partner whose entire chat thread with you is a to-do list, captioned 'this is our love language' — the sweetness is that remembering the mundane details is the least romantic romance there is
variations: Advanced version: once I slipped an 'I miss you' into the middle of the shopping list. He replied, 'get the two-ply toilet paper.' I figured he hadn't seen it — then he came home from work with a bouquet, card reading 'me too, I was just too shy to type it.'

Twist version: during a few days apart, our texts suddenly turned sappy — every line was longing. First day back to living together, they snapped right back: 'trash goes out today.' Turns out distance really does make things beautiful. Up close, all that's left is chores.

items.sourceNote: 情侶其中一方總是不關櫃子門/抽屜的習慣梗，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
我另一半有一個超能力：他經過的每一扇門，都會神奇地保持開啟。

抽屜、衣櫃、浴室鏡櫃、廚房吊櫃——他打開，然後就……走了。
彷彿「關門」這個動作，會用掉他一整天僅存的體力。

我跟在他後面關門，關了三年，練出一身反射神經。

有天他問我：「你怎麼那麼會找東西，家裡每個櫃子你都好熟。」
我：「因為它們永遠是開的，我一眼就看得到裡面有什麼。」

——愛一個人，就是願意在他身後，默默把他忘記關的每一扇門，一扇一扇關上。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個櫃子門永遠開開的另一半，配一張你家某扇大開的櫃門照片，配一句「又是你」，笑點在於『這個習慣改不掉，但我也懶得跟你計較了』
variations: 進階版：後來我不關了，直接跟他說「這是你的門，你自己關」。結果那些門就一直開著，開到有天他半夜起來喝水，額頭正面撞上他自己沒關的吊櫃。從此他關門關得比誰都勤。
反轉版：有天我發現有一扇櫃門是關好的，愣了一下，感動到差點哭出來。他一臉莫名：「我只是關個門，你反應會不會太大。」——在一起久了，對方做對一件小事，都能讓你感動半天。

en.content:
My partner has a superpower: every door they walk past magically stays open.

Drawers, wardrobe, bathroom mirror cabinet, kitchen cupboards — they open it, and then… they just leave.
As if the act of closing a door would drain the last of their energy for the day.

I've been trailing behind them closing doors for three years. I've developed reflexes.

One day they asked me, "How are you so good at finding things? You know every cabinet in this house."
Me: "Because they're always open. I can see exactly what's inside at a glance."

— Loving someone means being willing to walk behind them and quietly close, one by one, every door they forgot.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner whose cabinet doors are eternally ajar, with a photo of one gaping open in your home and 'you again' — the joke being 'this habit will never change, but I've stopped keeping score'
variations: Advanced version: eventually I stopped closing them and just said, 'that's your door, you close it.' So they stayed open — until the night he got up for water and walked forehead-first into a cupboard he'd left open himself. He's been the most diligent door-closer alive ever since.

Twist version: one day I found a cabinet door actually shut, froze for a second, and nearly cried from the shock of it. He looked baffled: 'I just closed a door, isn't that reaction a bit much?' Turns out after long enough together, your partner doing one small thing right can move you for the rest of the day.

items.sourceNote: 把交往比喻成用一套系統／軟體更新的梗，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
交往，其實很像在用一套系統：

第一年：全新機，什麼功能都新鮮，每天都想打開來玩。
第二年：開始出現小bug，但還在保固內，吵一吵就修好了。
第三年：系統大更新，介面全變了，兩個人重新適應。
第五年：你已經摸透所有隱藏功能，閉著眼睛都能操作。
第十年：偶爾當機，但你捨不得換——因為所有資料都在裡面，換一台等於從頭來過。

——最好的感情不是永遠不出問題，是出了問題，你們願意一起除錯，而不是直接關機走人。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個跟你一起「更新」了好幾年的另一半，看你們現在在第幾版，甜點在於『用得越久越捨不得換，因為裡面裝滿了只有你們懂的資料』
variations: 進階版：交往十年後，我們已經進化到「不需要說明書」的境界——一個眼神、一個嘆氣，對方就知道你要幹嘛。這叫做：從使用者，變成了系統本身。
反轉版：朋友問我要不要「換一台新的、功能更多的」。我看了看身邊這台，說「不用了，這台雖然舊，但我閉著眼睛都會操作，而且它記得我所有的偏好設定」。——感情的價值，從來不在配備，在累積。

en.content:
Being in a relationship is a lot like running a system:

Year one: brand-new device, every feature feels fresh, you want to open it every day.
Year two: small bugs start showing up, but you're still under warranty — a quick fight patches them.
Year three: major update, the whole interface changes, and you both have to re-adapt.
Year five: you know every hidden feature, you can operate it with your eyes closed.
Year ten: it crashes now and then, but you can't bring yourself to replace it — all your data lives in there, and switching means starting over from scratch.

— The best relationship isn't one that never has problems. It's one where, when problems come, you're willing to debug it together instead of just powering off and walking away.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner you've been 'updating' with for years and see which version you're on now — the sweetness is that the longer you use it, the less you want to replace it, because it's full of data only the two of you understand
variations: Advanced version: ten years in, we've reached the 'no manual required' stage — one look, one sigh, and the other already knows what you want. That's called: going from being the user to becoming the system itself.

Twist version: a friend asked if I wanted to 'upgrade to a newer model with more features.' I looked at the one beside me and said, 'no thanks — it's old, but I can operate it with my eyes closed, and it remembers every one of my preference settings.' The value of a relationship was never in the specs. It's in the accumulation.

items.sourceNote: 共用冰箱、貼名字的剩菜被偷吃的信任考驗梗，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
同居之後我才知道，冰箱是這世界上最脆弱的信任契約。

我把上禮拜捨不得吃完的那塊蛋糕，用便利貼寫上「我的，勿動」，貼得端端正正。

三天後，蛋糕不見了，便利貼還在，被貼到旁邊的醬油瓶上。

我：「你吃了？」
他：「我以為那張紙是貼給我看的，意思是『這是要給我的』。」

——在一起久了，連你的字，他都會自動翻譯成對他有利的版本。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個總是偷吃你冰箱食物的另一半，配一張你貼滿便利貼的冰箱照片，笑點在於『防得了外人，防不了枕邊人』
variations: 進階版：後來我學乖了，改把想吃的東西藏在他絕對不會打開的地方——燙青菜的保鮮盒底下。從此天下太平，因為他這輩子沒主動吃過一口青菜。
反轉版：有天我發現冰箱裡多了一塊蛋糕，貼著便利貼「你的，我沒動」。我愣住，原來他也會還。愛情裡最感人的不是分享，是他終於學會了留一口給你。

en.content:
After moving in together, I learned that the fridge is the most fragile trust agreement on earth.

I took the slice of cake I'd been saving all week, wrote 'MINE — do not touch' on a sticky note, and stuck it on perfectly straight.

Three days later the cake was gone. The note was still there — relocated onto the soy sauce bottle next to it.

Me: "You ate it?"
Him: "I thought the note was addressed TO me. Like, 'this is for you.'"

— Be with someone long enough and they'll auto-translate even your handwriting into the version that benefits them.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who keeps raiding your fridge, with a photo of your sticky-note-covered fridge — the joke being 'you can defend against outsiders, but never the person next to you'
variations: Advanced version: eventually I got smart and started hiding the food I wanted under the one thing he'd never open — the container of steamed vegetables. Peace on earth ever since, because that man has never once willingly eaten a vegetable.

Twist version: one day I found an extra slice of cake in the fridge with a note: 'yours, I didn't touch it.' I froze. Turns out he gives back too. The most moving thing in love isn't sharing — it's when they finally learn to save you a bite.

items.sourceNote: 情侶對冷氣溫度／搶棉被的冷戰梗（冷邏輯 vs 熱情緒），重新創作

zh-tw.content:
我們家的冷戰，是真的跟溫度有關。

我覺得26度剛好，他覺得26度是北極。
他把冷氣調到29度，我半夜熱醒；我調回26度，他早上起來鼻子紅得像馴鹿。

最後我們達成史詩級協議：冷氣開26度，他蓋兩床棉被。

於是每晚的畫面就是——我一件短袖睡得香甜，旁邊躺著一個把自己裹成春捲的人，只露出一顆頭，還在發抖。

——所謂磨合，就是兩個人都不改變自己，只是想辦法在同一個房間裡各自舒服。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個跟你溫度感受天差地遠的另一半，配一句「這就是我們家的夏天」，笑點在於『沒有人妥協，但也沒有人離開』
variations: 進階版：後來我們買了那種左右兩邊溫度可以分開調的電熱毯，號稱婚姻救星。結果他那邊開到最高溫，我這邊直接關掉，中間那條線成了兩個國家的邊境。
反轉版：有次他感冒，我默默把冷氣調到29度，一整晚熱到睡不著。他早上說「昨天怎麼沒那麼冷」，我說「大概是天氣變了吧」。有些愛，是說不出口，只能靠偷偷調高兩度來表達。

en.content:
In our house, the cold war is literally about temperature.

I think 72°F is perfect. He thinks 72°F is the Arctic.
He bumps it up to 78°F and I wake up sweating at 2am; I set it back to 72°F and he wakes up with a nose as red as a reindeer's.

Eventually we reached an epic treaty: AC at 72°F, and he sleeps under two comforters.

So every night looks like this — me in a t-shirt sleeping like a baby, next to a man wrapped up like a spring roll, only his head sticking out, still shivering.

— Compromise is when neither person changes, they just find a way to be individually comfortable in the same room.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner whose internal thermostat is the opposite of yours, captioned 'this is summer in our house' — the joke being 'nobody caves, but nobody leaves either'
variations: Advanced version: eventually we bought one of those dual-zone electric blankets billed as the savior of marriages. He cranks his side to max, I switch mine off entirely, and the seam down the middle became the border between two nations.

Twist version: once he had a cold, so I quietly set the AC to 78°F and roasted, sleepless, all night. In the morning he said, 'huh, it wasn't so cold last night.' I said, 'must be the weather.' Some love can't be said out loud — it can only be expressed by secretly nudging the temperature up two degrees.

items.sourceNote: 情侶對洗碗機／碗盤怎麼擺意見不同的梗，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
沒有人跟我說過，決定要不要跟一個人共度餘生，關鍵指標是：他洗完的碗，是怎麼放的。

我的宇宙觀：碗要按大小疊好，杯口朝下，筷子頭尾對齊。
他的宇宙觀：只要每一個碗，都有跟另一個碗，產生某種形式的接觸，就算收好了。

我第一次打開他收的碗櫃，退後三步，像在看一場行為藝術。

現在我們的分工是：他負責洗，我負責在他背後全部重新排一次。
他叫這個「團隊合作」，我叫這個「災後重建」。

——愛情最深的考驗，從來不是生離死別，是你能不能忍住不去重排他收好的碗。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個收納哲學跟你完全相反的另一半，笑點在於『兩個人都覺得自己才是對的，而且永遠不會有結論』
variations: 進階版：後來我試著放手，告訴自己「碗放哪裡不重要，重要的是他願意洗」。我撐了三天。第四天，我趁他洗澡，把整個碗櫃默默重排了一次，然後假裝什麼都沒發生。
反轉版：有天我發現碗全部按大小疊得整整齊齊，杯口朝下。我感動地問他怎麼突然開竅。他說「不是我排的，是我怕你唸，先把它們藏進洗碗機了」。原來成長不是改變，是學會把證據藏好。

en.content:
Nobody warned me that the real metric for deciding whether to spend your life with someone is: how they put away the dishes they've washed.

My worldview: bowls stacked by size, cups face-down, chopsticks aligned head to tail.
His worldview: as long as every bowl is making some form of physical contact with another bowl, the job is done.

The first time I opened his cabinet, I stepped back three paces like I was witnessing performance art.

Our division of labor now: he washes, and I re-arrange all of it behind his back.
He calls this 'teamwork.' I call it 'disaster reconstruction.'

— The deepest test of love was never life and death. It's whether you can resist re-stacking the dishes he already put away.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner whose organizing philosophy is the exact inverse of yours — the joke being 'both of you are certain you're right, and there will never be a verdict'
variations: Advanced version: eventually I tried to let go, telling myself 'where the bowls go doesn't matter, what matters is that he's willing to wash them.' I lasted three days. On day four, while he was in the shower, I silently re-arranged the entire cabinet and pretended nothing happened.

Twist version: one day I found every bowl stacked neatly by size, cups face-down. Moved, I asked what enlightened him. He said, 'I didn't arrange them — I was scared you'd nag, so I just hid them all in the dishwasher.' Turns out growth isn't change. It's learning to hide the evidence.

items.sourceNote: 情侶在超市互傳「家裡還缺什麼」訊息的日常梗，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
所謂長期關係，就是兩個人一輩子互相傳「我在超市，家裡還缺什麼嗎」，直到其中一個人先走為止。

我：「我在超市，要買什麼？」
他：「沒有，都夠。」
（十分鐘後我回到家）
他：「啊你有沒有買衛生紙？」
我：「你剛剛說都夠。」
他：「我剛剛以為還有。」

這個對話，我們結婚以來重播了大概四百次。
家裡的衛生紙，永遠在「還有一點」跟「完全沒有」之間量子疊加。

——愛一個人，就是明知道他等一下一定會補一句「啊還有」，你還是先問了他要不要買什麼。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個永遠在你離開超市後才想起要買什麼的另一半，笑點在於『這個bug永遠修不好，但你們也認命了』
variations: 進階版：後來我學會了，不管他說什麼，我都自動買衛生紙、牛奶、雞蛋這三樣。因為在這個家，這三樣東西的庫存，是一種都市傳說，沒有人真正見過它們同時存在。
反轉版：有次我沒問就直接買了他愛吃的那個牌子的餅乾回家。他愣了一下說「你怎麼知道我想吃這個」。我沒告訴他，其實我在超市站在那排零食前面，想的全是他。

en.content:
A long-term relationship is two people texting each other 'I'm at the store, do we need anything?' for the rest of their lives, until one of them goes first.

Me: "I'm at the store, what do we need?"
Him: "Nothing, we're good."
(Ten minutes later, I'm home)
Him: "Oh, did you get toilet paper?"
Me: "You just said we were good."
Him: "I thought we still had some."

We've re-run this exact conversation about four hundred times since we got married.
The toilet paper in this house exists in a permanent quantum superposition between 'a little left' and 'completely out.'

— Loving someone is knowing they'll definitely add 'oh, and also—' in a minute, and still asking first if they need anything.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who only remembers what they needed after you've left the store — the joke being 'this bug will never get patched, but you've both made peace with it'
variations: Advanced version: eventually I learned to just auto-buy toilet paper, milk, and eggs no matter what he says. Because in this house, having all three in stock at once is an urban legend — nobody has ever actually witnessed it.

Twist version: once I came home with the exact brand of cookies he loves, without asking. He paused and said, 'how did you know I wanted these?' I never told him: standing in that snack aisle, all I was thinking about was him.

items.sourceNote: 情侶太有默契、連紀念日都一起忘記的梗，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
別人的情侶：一個記得紀念日，一個忘記，於是吵架。

我們的情侶：兩個人一起忘記，然後某天晚上同時抬起頭——
「欸……我們上禮拜是不是有個什麼日子？」
「……好像是欸。」
「是幾週年來著？」
「不知道，反正是某個整數。」

最後我們決定，把「一起想起紀念日」的那一天，重新定為新的紀念日。

——真正的默契，不是兩個人都記得，是兩個人一起忘記的時候，都不覺得對方有錯。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個跟你一樣記性差、卻從不因此吵架的另一半，笑點在於『我們爛得剛剛好，剛好爛在同一個點上』
variations: 進階版：後來我們乾脆把手機行事曆的紀念日設成「每個月都提醒一次」，這樣總有一次會記得。結果變成每個月都以為是紀念日，每個月都吃一頓大餐。因禍得福。
反轉版：有年我偷偷記得了，準備給他驚喜，結果打開門發現他也偷偷準備了。我們兩個人站在滿桌菜前面，同時說「你怎麼記得」。原來一起忘記那麼多年，是為了在某一年，一起記得。

en.content:
Other couples: one remembers the anniversary, one forgets, and they fight.

Our couple: both of us forget together, and then one night we look up at the same time —
"Hey… wasn't there some kind of date last week?"
"…Yeah, I think there was."
"Which anniversary was it?"
"No idea. Some round number, definitely."

Eventually we decided to redefine the day we 'remembered the anniversary together' as the new anniversary.

— Real chemistry isn't both of you remembering. It's that when you both forget, neither of you thinks the other did anything wrong.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who's as forgetful as you but never fights about it — the joke being 'we're bad at this in exactly the same spot, and that's perfect'
variations: Advanced version: eventually we just set the calendar to remind us of the anniversary once every single month, figuring at least one of them would land. Now we think every month is the anniversary and go out for a big dinner every time. A blessing in disguise.

Twist version: one year I secretly remembered and planned a surprise — then opened the door to find he'd secretly planned one too. We stood in front of a table full of food and said at the same time, 'how did you remember?' Turns out forgetting together for all those years was just so we could, one year, remember together.

items.sourceNote: 洗澡中被拉開浴簾問零食，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
交往初期的浴室，是有隱私的。
交往三年的浴室，是一個公共論壇。

我正在洗頭，滿頭泡泡、眼睛都睜不開，浴簾突然「唰」地被拉開。
我以為出事了。

「幹嘛！」
「我們家的鹽酥雞是不是吃完了？」
「……你為了問這個拉開浴簾？」
「不然等你洗完我就忘記了啊。」

然後浴簾又「唰」地拉回去。
留我一個人在蓮蓬頭下，思考我們之間到底還剩下什麼界線。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個會在你洗澡洗到一半衝進來問你晚餐吃什麼的另一半，笑點在於『在你面前我已經沒有隱私，但我也不想要有了』
variations: 進階版：後來我學會了反擊。換他洗澡的時候，我也拉開浴簾問「你剛剛是不是把我最後一塊雞排吃掉了」——他在花灑下沉默了三秒，說「我們談談」。原來浴室不只是論壇，還是法庭。
反轉版：有次我真的出事了，滑倒撞到頭，喊了一聲。他衝進來的速度比問零食那次快十倍。原來平常的沒大沒小，是因為他隨時都在聽。

en.content:
Early in a relationship, the bathroom has privacy.
Three years in, the bathroom is a public forum.

I'm mid-shampoo, head full of suds, eyes clamped shut, when the curtain gets yanked open with a loud swish.
I thought something was wrong.

"What?!"
"Are we out of the fried chicken?"
"...You opened the curtain for that?"
"Well I'd forget by the time you're done."

Then the curtain swishes shut again.
Leaving me under the showerhead, wondering what boundaries we have left.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who barges in mid-shower to ask about dinner — the joke being 'I have no privacy left around you, and honestly I don't want any'
variations: Advanced version: eventually I learned to strike back. When it was his turn to shower, I flung the curtain open and asked, 'did you just eat my last chicken cutlet?' He went silent under the spray for three seconds, then said, 'we need to talk.' Turns out the bathroom isn't just a forum — it's also a courtroom.
Twist version: one time I actually slipped, hit my head, and yelped. He burst in ten times faster than the snack-question time. Turns out all that casual barging in meant he was always listening.

items.sourceNote: 「要不要順便買什麼」訊息串直到永遠，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
所謂長久的感情，就是兩個人共用一條永遠不會結束的訊息串：

「我在超市，要順便買什麼嗎？」
「衛生紙。」
「還有嗎？」
「沒了。」

三小時後，另一個人：
「我在全聯，要順便買什麼嗎？」
「衛生紙。」
「我們家是有多會用衛生紙？」

——這條訊息串會一直傳下去，傳到我們其中一個人不在了為止。而且到那一天，家裡大概還是缺衛生紙。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個每次出門都會問你要不要順便買東西的另一半，甜點在於『我人在外面，但我心裡有你要的那包衛生紙』
variations: 進階版：後來我們發明了暗號。他傳「順便」兩個字，我就知道要開始報清單了。有時候我一口氣報八樣，他回一個「……」，然後乖乖全部買回來。所謂承諾，就是那個「……」之後他還是去了。
反轉版：有次我說「沒有要買的」，結果他還是提了一袋我最愛的餅乾回來。我問他幹嘛買，他說「你嘴上說沒有，但你昨天盯著它看了很久」。原來我以為的沒被發現，全被記在心裡。

en.content:
A lasting relationship is two people sharing one text thread that never ends:

"I'm at the store, want me to grab anything?"
"Toilet paper."
"Anything else?"
"Nope."

Three hours later, the other person:
"I'm at the store, want me to grab anything?"
"Toilet paper."
"How much toilet paper does this household go through?"

— This thread will keep going until one of us is gone. And on that day, we'll probably still be out of toilet paper.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who texts 'want anything?' every time they're out — the sweetness being 'I'm out in the world, but I'm carrying your grocery list in my heart'
variations: Advanced version: eventually we developed a code. He'd just text 'grab?' and I'd know to start listing. Sometimes I'd rattle off eight things and he'd reply '...' and then buy every single one. Commitment is the fact that he still went after that '...'.

Twist version: once I said 'nothing to buy,' and he still came home with a bag of my favorite cookies. I asked why. He said, 'you said no, but you stared at them for a while yesterday.' Turns out what I thought went unnoticed was quietly logged.

items.sourceNote: 起司不夠兩個人分而吵架，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
我們感情最危險的一次，不是為了前任，也不是為了錢。
是為了一片起司。

那天晚上一起煎吐司，冰箱只剩一片起司。
「你放你的。」
「不要，你放你的。」
「我不餓，真的。」
「你剛剛才說你很餓。」
「……」

最後那片起司在鍋子上融化到一半，兩個人都不肯先夾，它就這樣焦掉了。
我們看著那片焦掉的起司，突然都笑了。

——有些吵架，不是因為誰想贏，是因為兩個人都想輸給對方。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個會把最後一口好料留給你、結果兩個人推來推去的另一半，甜點在於『我們搶的不是那片起司，是「讓你多吃一點」的權利』
variations: 進階版：後來我們立了規矩——最後一片任何東西，一律對切。結果切的時候又開始吵「你那半比較大」。愛情裡沒有真正的公平，只有兩個都想吃虧的人。
終極版：有次真的只剩一口，我趁他去接電話，默默把那口塞進他等等要吃的碗裡。他回來吃到的時候什麼都沒說，只是把他碗裡的蝦夾了一隻到我碗裡。我們從來沒把這件事講開，但那一餐我們都吃得很飽。

en.content:
The most dangerous moment in our relationship wasn't about an ex, or money.
It was about one slice of cheese.

We were making grilled toast, and the fridge had exactly one slice left.
"You have it."
"No, you have it."
"I'm not hungry, really."
"You literally just said you were starving."
"..."

That slice melted halfway in the pan while neither of us would take it, and it just burned.
We looked at the charred cheese and both started laughing.

— Some fights aren't about who wants to win. They're about two people who both want to lose to the other.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who saves the last good bite for you until you're both pushing it back and forth — the sweetness being 'we're not fighting over the cheese, we're fighting for the right to let you have more'
variations: Advanced version: eventually we made a rule — the last of anything gets cut in half. Then we started fighting over 'your half is bigger.' There's no true fairness in love, just two people who both want the short end.

Ultimate version: once there was truly one bite left, and while he took a call I quietly dropped it into the bowl he'd eat from next. When he got to it he said nothing — just moved a shrimp from his bowl into mine. We never talked about it, but we both left that meal full.

items.sourceNote: 熱戀期不吃乾淨 vs 交往久了計較骨頭上的肉，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
熱戀期吃雞翅，他啃兩口就放下，說「吃不下了」，很優雅。
我覺得他好斯文，愛了。

交往三年吃雞翅，他把我放下的那隻拿起來，對著燈光研究，說：
「這上面明明還有肉，你看，這裡、這裡、還有這裡，加起來至少半口。」

然後他真的把那半口啃乾淨了。

——熱戀是「我要在你面前當個精緻的人」，長久是「我要在你面前當個真實的人」。而真實的人，是不會讓骨頭上的肉白白浪費的。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個在你面前早就不裝了、會把你剩的菜吃光光的另一半，笑點在於『他不是變邋遢了，是終於在你面前放鬆了』
variations: 進階版：現在換我了。我們出去吃合菜，最後一顆獅子頭沒人夾，我們對看一眼，我說「你吃」，他說「一人一半」，然後我們用兩雙筷子把那顆獅子頭夾成兩半，像在拆彈。從精緻到務實，這就是愛情的成長。
反轉版：有次他真的很累，那隻雞翅他一口沒動就睡著了。我把肉啃乾淨，把最完整的一塊留在盤子邊。他半夜醒來吃掉，傳訊息給睡在旁邊的我：「謝謝你幫我留的。」——原來計較骨頭上的肉，也可以是一種溫柔。

en.content:
Early on, he'd nibble a chicken wing twice, set it down, and say 'I'm full,' so elegant.
I thought he was so refined. I was in love.

Three years in, he picks up the wing I set down, holds it to the light, and says:
"There's clearly still meat on this. Look — here, here, and here. That's half a bite, easy."

And then he actually eats that half a bite clean off the bone.

— Dating is 'I want to be a refined person in front of you.' Lasting is 'I want to be a real person in front of you.' And a real person does not let meat go to waste on a bone.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who long ago stopped performing and now finishes everything on your plate — the joke being 'he didn't get sloppy, he finally relaxed around you'
variations: Advanced version: now it's me. We're out for a shared meal, one last meatball nobody will take. We look at each other, I say 'you have it,' he says 'we split it,' and we cut that meatball in half with two pairs of chopsticks like we're defusing a bomb. From refined to practical — that's how love grows up.

Twist version: once he was so tired he fell asleep without touching his wing. I picked it clean and left the best piece on the edge of the plate. He woke at midnight, ate it, and texted the me sleeping right next to him: 'thanks for saving that.' Turns out fussing over meat on a bone can be its own kind of tenderness.

items.sourceNote: 情侶只用貼圖溝通、不打字，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
我們的聊天室，已經三個禮拜沒有出現一個中文字了。

我傳一張「餓了」的貼圖。
他回一張「吃什麼」的貼圖。
我傳一張「都可以」的貼圖。
他回一張「翻白眼」的貼圖。
我傳一張「愛心」的貼圖。
他回一張「愛心」的貼圖。

對話結束。晚餐搞定。感情確認。

——交往久了，語言會退化，但那不是因為沒話說，是因為連貼圖都能懂彼此的意思。有些默契，是不需要字幕的。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個跟你已經可以只用貼圖對話一整天的另一半，甜點在於『我們把一段對話濃縮成六張圖，因為剩下的你都懂』
variations: 進階版：後來我們吵架也只用貼圖。我傳一張「生氣」，他回一張「對不起」，我傳一張「哼」，他回一張「抱抱」，我傳一張「勉強原諒你」。整場冷戰用五張貼圖打完，比講道理快多了，也比講道理有效。
反轉版：有天他突然打了一整段字給我，我嚇到以為出事了。點開一看，是「今天路過我們第一次約會的那家店，想到你，就想跟你說一聲」。原來平常用貼圖，是把打字這件事，留給真正想說的話。

en.content:
Our chat hasn't had a single actual word in it for three weeks.

I send a 'hungry' sticker.
He sends a 'what do you want' sticker.
I send an 'anything' sticker.
He sends an 'eye-roll' sticker.
I send a 'heart' sticker.
He sends a 'heart' sticker.

Conversation over. Dinner sorted. Love confirmed.

— The longer you're together, the more language decays — not because there's nothing to say, but because even stickers carry the whole meaning. Some understanding needs no subtitles.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner you can hold a whole day's conversation with using only stickers — the sweetness being 'we compressed a conversation into six images because you already get the rest'
variations: Advanced version: eventually we fought with only stickers too. I send 'angry,' he sends 'sorry,' I send 'hmph,' he sends 'hug,' I send 'fine, forgiven.' A whole cold war settled in five stickers — faster than arguing, and somehow more effective.

Twist version: one day he suddenly typed out a full paragraph and I panicked, sure something was wrong. I opened it: 'walked past the place we had our first date today, thought of you, just wanted to say so.' Turns out using stickers all the time was how we saved actual typing for the things truly worth saying.

items.sourceNote: 情侶為了某個字的唸法吵架的網路討論，重新創作為台灣情境

zh-tw.content:
我們吵最久的一場架，是關於「蛤蜊」這兩個字到底要怎麼唸。

我唸「ㄍㄜˊ ㄌㄧˋ」，他堅持是「ㄏㄚˊ ㄌㄧˊ」。
我把教育部字典翻給他看，他說字典也會錯。
他打電話問他媽，他媽講出了第三種唸法。

那場架吵了四十分鐘，冷戰兩小時。晚上我們一起去吃蛤蜊絲瓜麵，點餐的時候，兩個人非常有默契地同時說：「那個⋯⋯這個，一份。」

——交往久了才懂，有些爭執根本不需要分出對錯，只需要兩個人一起繞過去。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個曾經跟你為了某個無關緊要的小事吵到天翻地覆、隔天卻完全和好的另一半，笑點在於『我們寧可放棄那個詞，也不願意放棄彼此』
variations: 延伸版：從那天起，「蛤蜊」在我們家變成一個禁字。菜單上看到就用手指，講到就說「那個海邊的」。一段感情的成熟，就是懂得幫某些字建立保護區。
反轉版：三年後某天他突然說：「其實我後來查過了，是你對。」我問他為什麼現在才講。他說：「因為那時候承認的話，那碗麵就不是我們一起繞過去的了，是我輸掉的。」

en.content:
The longest fight we ever had was about how to pronounce 'caramel.'

I say car-a-mel. He insists it's car-mel.
I pulled up the dictionary. He said dictionaries can be wrong.
He called his mom for backup. His mom produced a third pronunciation nobody had ever heard.

Forty minutes of arguing, two hours of silent treatment. That night we went out for dessert, and when we ordered, we both pointed at the menu and said, at exactly the same time, 'that one. One of those.'

— The longer you're together, the more you learn that some arguments don't need a winner. They just need both of you to walk around them.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner you once had a world-ending fight with over something completely meaningless and were fine again by morning — the joke being 'we'd rather give up the word than give up each other'
variations: Extended version: since that day, 'caramel' has been a banned word in our house. On menus we point. In conversation it's 'the brown sticky one.' A mature relationship is knowing which words to build a fence around.

Twist version: three years later he said out of nowhere, 'I looked it up back then. You were right.' I asked why he never said so. He said, 'because if I'd admitted it then, that dessert wouldn't have been something we walked around together. It would've just been me losing.'

items.sourceNote: 另一半找不到就在眼前的東西的經典梗，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
「我的耳機不見了。」
「在客廳桌上。」
「沒有啊。」
「有，就在你正前方。」
「真的沒有，我找過了。」

我走過去，站到他旁邊，伸手，從距離他手肘不到五公分的地方，把耳機拿起來。

他看著我，用一種目睹魔術的表情說：「你怎麼辦到的？」

——這世界上有一種超能力，叫做「東西只要不是自己找的，就會自動現身」。而我，顯然是那個被綁定的召喚道具。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個每次找東西都要喊你、而東西永遠就在他面前的另一半，笑點在於『我不是幫你找到，我只是站過去而已』
variations: 延伸版：後來我發明了一套流程。他喊「找不到」，我先問「你有沒有低頭」。八成的案件在這裡結案。剩下的兩成，答案是「在你手上」。
反轉版：有次換我大喊找不到手機，找了整整十分鐘。他默默走過來，把正在通話中的手機從我耳邊拿下來，看了一眼，再放回去。從那天起我再也沒有嘲笑過他。

en.content:
'I can't find my headphones.'
'They're on the coffee table.'
'They're not.'
'They are. They're directly in front of you.'
'I looked. They're not there.'

I walk over, stand next to him, reach out, and pick them up from a spot roughly two inches from his elbow.

He looks at me like he just watched a magic trick and says, 'how did you DO that?'

— There's a superpower in this world where objects only materialize when someone else looks for them. And I am apparently the summoning item it's bound to.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who calls you every time something goes missing and it is always, always directly in front of them — the joke being 'I didn't find it, I just stood there'
variations: Extended version: I eventually developed a protocol. He yells 'I can't find it,' I ask 'have you looked down.' That closes about eighty percent of cases. For the remaining twenty, the answer is 'it's in your hand.'

Twist version: one day it was me tearing the apartment apart looking for my phone. Ten full minutes. He walked over, took the phone away from my ear mid-call, looked at it, and handed it back. I have never mocked him since.

items.sourceNote: 情侶同居後共同責任分工的討論，改寫為養植物的具體情境

zh-tw.content:
我們決定一起養一盆植物，當作感情的期中考。

第一週：兩個人搶著澆水，它大概喝了一公升。
第三週：「你澆了嗎？」「我以為你澆了。」
第五週：葉子開始變黃。
第六週：我們把它搬到陽台，說是「讓它曬曬太陽」，其實是不想看到它。
第八週：它走了。

我們站在陽台前默哀三秒，然後他說：「還好我們先養這個，沒有先養小孩。」

——原來責任這種東西，兩個人一起扛不會變成一半，只會變成一場「我以為你會」的無限迴圈。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個跟你一起養死過植物、寵物石頭或某個共同計畫的另一半，笑點在於『我們一起失敗，但至少一起』
variations: 延伸版：那盆植物之後，我們又養了一盆。這次我在盆子上貼了兩張貼紙，一張寫「單日他」，一張寫「雙日我」。它現在已經活了兩年，長得比我們的溝通能力還好。
反轉版：我把植物死掉的事講給我媽聽，我媽只回一句：「你們有沒有討論過誰要負責？」我說沒有。她說：「那不是植物死了，是你們的分工死了。」我當場沉默了三秒鐘。

en.content:
We decided to raise a plant together, as a midterm exam for the relationship.

Week 1: we both raced to water it. It drank roughly a liter.
Week 3: 'did you water it?' 'I thought you watered it.'
Week 5: the leaves start going yellow.
Week 6: we move it to the balcony, officially 'so it gets more sun,' actually so we don't have to look at it.
Week 8: it's gone.

We stood at the balcony door for a three-second moment of silence, and then he said, 'good thing we started with this and not a kid.'

— Turns out shared responsibility doesn't split in half. It just becomes an infinite loop of 'I assumed you would.'
en.editorNote: Send to the partner you've killed a plant, a pet rock, or a joint project with — the joke being 'we failed, but at least we failed together'
variations: Extended version: we got a second plant after that. This time I stuck two labels on the pot: 'odd days him,' 'even days me.' It's been alive for two years now and is doing better than our communication skills.
