Twist version: I told my mom the plant died. She just asked, 'did you two ever discuss who was responsible?' I said no. She said, 'then the plant didn't die. Your division of labor did.' I went quiet for a solid three seconds.

items.sourceNote: 另一半拍照技術差的經典抱怨，重新創作並加入情感反轉

zh-tw.content:
我男友幫我拍的照片，有一種非常獨特的美學：

下巴永遠有三層。
地平線一定是斜的。
我閉眼的那張，他會說「這張最自然」。
我認真擺好姿勢的那張，他清楚地拍到了我旁邊的垃圾桶。

我說：「你可不可以拍好看一點？」
他一臉真誠：「可是你本來就長這樣啊，我覺得很好看。」

——他不是技術差，他是真的看不出差別。在他眼裡，那張三層下巴的，跟我修了四十分鐘的那張，是同一個人。而且都很好看。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個拍照技術差到令人絕望、但每一張都是真心覺得你好看的另一半，甜點在於『他的鏡頭沒有濾鏡，因為他的眼睛本來就是』
variations: 延伸版：後來我學會了，出門前先示範一次：「站這裡，蹲下來，鏡頭放低。」他照做，拍出一張還不錯的。他看了看說：「跟剛剛那張差在哪？」我說：「差在腿長十公分。」他說：「喔。可是我又不是為了腿拍你。」
反轉版：整理相簿的時候我發現，他手機裡我的照片有六百多張，幾乎全是我沒發現在被拍的時候——吃麵、發呆、蹲在路邊看貓。沒有一張是我會發限動的角度，但每一張我看起來都很快樂。

en.content:
The photos my boyfriend takes of me have a very distinct aesthetic:

Three chins, every time.
The horizon is always tilted.
The one where my eyes are closed? 'That one's the most natural.'
The one where I actually posed? He got a crisp, well-lit shot of the trash can next to me.

I said, 'could you please make me look good?'
He said, completely sincerely, 'but that's what you look like. I think it's great.'

— He's not bad at photography. He genuinely cannot see the difference. To him, the three-chin shot and the one I edited for forty minutes are the same person. And both of them look great.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner whose photography is hopeless but who means every single shot — the sweetness being 'his camera has no filter because his eyes never did'
variations: Extended version: I eventually started coaching him. 'Stand here. Crouch. Camera lower.' He did it and got a genuinely decent shot. He studied it and asked, 'what's different from the last one?' I said, 'my legs are four inches longer.' He said, 'oh. But I wasn't photographing you for the legs.'

Twist version: going through his phone one day I found over six hundred photos of me, almost all taken when I had no idea — eating noodles, spacing out, crouching on a sidewalk looking at a cat. Not one is an angle I'd ever post. In every single one I look happy.

items.sourceNote: 見家長後被加入對方家族群組的台灣情境，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
見完家長的第三天，我被加進一個叫「○○家族一家親」的群組。

成員四十七人。
我一個都不認識。
每天早上六點半，準時出現一張「早安　祝福您」的花朵圖。

我不敢退、不敢已讀不回、也不敢真的回。於是我發展出一套生存策略：每五天按一次讚，每半個月傳一句「謝謝阿姨❤️」。

他說：「你不用理啊。」
我說：「你不懂，這是我的期中考。」

——被加進對方的家族群組，是一段感情從「你們兩個」正式變成「我們一家」的分界線。有點恐怖，但仔細想想，也蠻甜的。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個把你拉進他家族群組、卻完全不知道那有多可怕的另一半，笑點在於『他覺得只是加個群，我覺得是入學考試』
variations: 延伸版：半年後我在群組裡的地位大幅提升——因為我是唯一會回長輩貼圖的人。現在阿姨們有事會直接標我，跳過他。他有天很委屈地說：「這是我家欸。」我說：「不好意思，現在是我的了。」
反轉版：我原本一直很怕那個群組，直到有天他阿嬤傳了一張我們兩個的合照，配字「這是我們家的孩子」。我盯著那個「們」字看了很久，然後把整段對話截圖存起來。那天之後，六點半的花朵圖我都會認真看完。

en.content:
Three days after meeting his parents, I got added to a group chat called 'The [Family Name] Fam ❤️'.

Forty-seven members.
I know none of them.
Every morning at 6:30 sharp, a flower graphic that says 'GOOD MORNING BLESSINGS TO YOU'.

I can't leave. I can't read and ignore. I can't actually reply. So I developed a survival strategy: react to something every five days, and once every two weeks type 'thank you Auntie ❤️'.

He said, 'you can just ignore it.'
I said, 'you don't understand. This is my midterm.'

— Getting added to your partner's family group chat is the official line where a relationship stops being 'you two' and becomes 'the family.' Mildly terrifying. Also, if you think about it, kind of sweet.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who added you to their family group chat with no idea what he'd done — the joke being 'he thinks it's a group chat, I think it's an entrance exam'
variations: Extended version: six months in, my standing in that chat had skyrocketed, purely because I'm the only person who replies to the aunts. They now tag me directly and skip him entirely. He complained once: 'that's MY family.' I said, 'sorry. It's mine now.'

Twist version: I was scared of that group chat right up until his grandma posted a photo of the two of us captioned 'this is our family's kid.' I stared at the word 'our' for a long time, then screenshotted the whole thing. Since that day I read every 6:30 a.m. flower graphic properly.

items.sourceNote: 情侶把寵物當小孩、用娃娃音對話的梗，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
我們家的貓叫「阿肥」。

但我們從來不叫牠阿肥。

我叫牠「寶寶」，他叫牠「兒子」，牠自己大概以為牠叫「欸欸欸不要抓沙發」。

最詭異的是我們對牠說話的聲音——那個又尖又軟、句尾自動上揚的娃娃音，我們只對牠用。對彼此，我們是正常人；一轉頭對牠，兩個成年人瞬間退化。

有一天我朋友來我家，聽到我用那個聲音問「馬麻的乖寶寶今天有沒有想馬麻～」，她沉默三秒，說：「你剛剛那個是誰的聲音。」

——養寵物的情侶都有一套只有他們和那隻動物聽得懂的語言。旁人聽了雞皮疙瘩，但那其實是一個家庭正在成形的聲音。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個跟你一起把寵物寵上天、還會用娃娃音對話的另一半，笑點在於『我們對彼此是成年人，一轉頭對牠就退化成幼稚園』
variations: 延伸版：後來我們發現一件恐怖的事——我們對彼此，也開始不小心用那個聲音了。某天他問我「寶寶要不要喝水～」，我愣了一下，才發現那句話本來是要對貓說的，只是他看著我。我們兩個都笑了，但沒有一個人打算改。
反轉版：吵架冷戰的時候，唯一的破冰方式就是那隻貓。只要有一個人先用娃娃音對牠說話，另一個人就會忍不住接話，接著接著，兩個人就對著一隻貓和好了。牠可能是這個家最重要的翻譯官。

en.content:
Our cat's name is Meatball.

But we never call him Meatball.

I call him 'baby,' he calls him 'son,' and the cat probably thinks his name is 'HEY-HEY-NO-not-the-couch.'

The weirdest part is the voice we use on him — that high, soft, sing-song baby voice with the automatic upward lilt at the end of every sentence. We use it only on him. To each other, we're normal adults. Turn to the cat, and two grown people instantly regress.

A friend came over once and heard me ask, in that voice, 'does mama's good baby miss his mama today~' She went silent for three seconds and said: 'whose voice was that just now.'

— Every couple with a pet develops a private language only they and that one animal understand. It gives outsiders goosebumps. But it's actually the sound of a family taking shape.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner you spoil that pet with, the one who also does the baby voice — the joke being 'we're adults to each other and preschoolers the second we turn to the cat'
variations: Extended version: eventually we noticed something horrifying — we'd started slipping into that voice with each other. One day he asked 'does baby want some water~' and I froze, realizing the sentence was meant for the cat, except he was looking at me. We both laughed. Neither of us planned to stop.

Twist version: during a silent-treatment standoff, the only icebreaker is that cat. The moment one of us uses the baby voice on him, the other can't help chiming in, and one thing leads to another, and two people make up over a single cat. He might be the most important translator in this house.

items.sourceNote: 看電影時一直問「他是誰」「他們不是死了嗎」的解說需求梗，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
跟他看電影的標準流程：

開場五分鐘：「他是好人還是壞人？」
二十分鐘：「等等，那個女的是他姊姊還是前女友？」
四十分鐘：「他們不是死了嗎？」
「那是回憶。」
「哪一段是回憶？」
「剛剛整段都是回憶。」
「所以他到底死了沒？」

最後十分鐘他終於安靜了。
我轉頭一看——他睡著了。
電影演到最關鍵的反轉，他錯過了。
隔天他跟同事說：「那部超好看，你一定要去看。」

——有一種人，看電影需要另一個人全程當即時字幕兼客服。而你，就是被指定的那一位。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個看電影從頭問到尾、問完還睡著的另一半，笑點在於『我不是在陪你看電影，我是你的專屬解說員』
variations: 延伸版：後來我學乖了，每次開播前先問他：「你要現在問，還是等等一起問？」他說：「我盡量不問。」開場第三分鐘，「欸他是誰？」我把爆米花遞給他，這是我們之間新的和平協議——嘴巴塞滿就問不了了。
反轉版：直到有一次換我看一部我完全看不懂的燒腦片，換我一直問，他一題一題耐心回答，還幫我倒帶。那天我才發現，原來當那個『被問的人』，其實有點爽——因為代表對方相信你什麼都懂。

en.content:
The standard procedure for watching a movie with him:

Five minutes in: 'Is he a good guy or a bad guy?'
Twenty minutes: 'Wait, is that woman his sister or his ex?'
Forty minutes: 'Didn't they die already?'
'That's a flashback.'
'Which part is the flashback?'
'That whole last stretch was a flashback.'
'So is he dead or not?'

The last ten minutes, he finally goes quiet.
I turn to look — he's asleep.
The movie hits its biggest twist, and he misses it.
The next day he tells a coworker, 'that movie was amazing, you have to see it.'

— Some people need another person to serve as live subtitles and customer support for the entire film. And you are the one designated for the job.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who narrates questions through the whole movie and then falls asleep — the joke being 'I'm not watching a movie with you, I'm your personal commentator'
variations: Extended version: eventually I got smart and asked before every movie: 'do you want to ask now, or ask later all at once?' He said, 'I'll try not to ask.' Minute three: 'hey, who's that?' I handed him the popcorn. That's our new peace treaty — a full mouth can't ask questions.

Twist version: until one day I watched a mind-bending film I couldn't follow at all, and now I was the one asking, and he answered each question patiently and even rewound for me. That's when I realized being the one who gets asked is kind of nice — because it means the other person thinks you understand everything.

items.sourceNote: 情侶開車搶控制車上音樂／aux 線主導權的梗，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
車上音樂的主導權，是一段感情裡最血腥的戰場。

他開車，理論上他選歌。
但我坐副駕，手機連著藍牙。
於是就形成一種微妙的恐怖平衡：

他放一首，我聽三十秒，「下一首。」
我放一首，他聽十秒，「這什麼？」
「好聽啊。」
「這是在唸經。」

最後我們達成一個誰都不滿意的協議——放一個我們都能忍受、但都不是最愛的歌單。名字叫「和平」。裡面全是十年前的老歌，因為只有那個年代，我們的品味還沒分岔。

——所謂契合，不是喜歡一樣的東西，是願意為對方，把最愛的那首歌跳過。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個跟你搶車上音樂、最後被迫聽老歌的另一半，笑點在於『我們的和平歌單，是兩個人品味的最大公因數』
variations: 延伸版：那個叫「和平」的歌單，後來變成我們最珍貴的東西。因為每一首，都是我們一起在某段路上聽過的。現在只要前奏一響，我們就知道那是哪一趟旅行、那天天氣如何、誰在副駕睡著。品味的妥協，最後長成了共同的記憶。
反轉版：有一次他一個人開車去接我，我上車時發現他在放我最愛、他一直嫌棄的那個歌手。他假裝沒事，「隨便放的。」我沒拆穿他。原來所謂愛，是他一個人的時候，偷偷聽你喜歡的歌，試著懂你為什麼喜歡。

en.content:
Control of the car music is the bloodiest battlefield in a relationship.

He's driving, so in theory he picks the songs.
But I'm in the passenger seat, phone connected to Bluetooth.
So a delicate balance of terror forms:

He plays one, I listen thirty seconds, 'next.'
I play one, he listens ten seconds, 'what is this?'
'It's good.'
'It sounds like chanting.'

Eventually we reach an agreement neither of us likes — a playlist we can both tolerate but neither truly loves. It's called 'Peace.' It's all songs from ten years ago, because that's the only era before our tastes forked apart.

— Compatibility isn't liking the same things. It's being willing to skip your favorite song for someone else.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner you fight the aux war with, the one you both settle for old songs with — the joke being 'our peace playlist is the greatest common denominator of two people's taste'
variations: Extended version: that playlist called 'Peace' became our most precious thing, because every song is one we heard together on some stretch of road. Now the moment an intro plays, we know which trip it was, what the weather was like, who fell asleep in the passenger seat. A compromise in taste grew into a shared memory.

Twist version: once he drove alone to pick me up, and when I got in I found him playing the artist I love and he'd always mocked. He played it cool, 'just something random.' I didn't call him out. Turns out love is him secretly listening to your music when he's alone, trying to understand why you like it.

items.sourceNote: 一方喊「我好了」之後又做十件事才真的出門的梗，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
「我好了，可以走了。」

這句話從我嘴裡說出來的那一刻，離真正出門，平均還有十二分鐘。

因為說完「我好了」之後，我還要：
找鑰匙、上廁所、換一件外套、把換下來的外套掛好、看一下瓦斯關了沒、回去確認一次真的關了、餵貓、找手機（手機在我手上）、再上一次廁所。

他早就穿好鞋，站在門口，像一尊等待風化的雕像。
他不催了，因為催也沒用。他學會了一件事：我說「我好了」，是一種情緒，不是一種事實。

——在一起久了你會明白，有些「好了」是宣言，有些「好了」是預告片，而正片還要再等等。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個總是先在門口等你、等到快變雕像的另一半，或那個嘴上說好了卻永遠還有事的自己，笑點在於『我說好了不是承諾，是一種心情』
variations: 延伸版：後來他發明了一套換算公式——聽到我第一次喊「我好了」，他不動；聽到第二次「真的好了」，他開始穿鞋；聽到第三次「欸我們走了啦」，他才確定這次是真的。三次確認制，是他用無數次在門口罰站換來的智慧。
反轉版：直到有一天換他說「我好了」，然後也開始東摸西摸找東西，我站在門口等他，忽然一點都不生氣，只覺得——啊，原來被等的人是這種感覺，一點都不趕，因為知道有人願意等。從那天起我們兩個都遲到，但沒人再催誰。

en.content:
'I'm ready, we can go.'

The moment those words leave my mouth, we are, on average, still twelve minutes from actually leaving.

Because after saying 'I'm ready,' I still have to:
find my keys, use the bathroom, change my jacket, hang up the jacket I just took off, check whether the gas is off, go back to confirm it's really off, feed the cat, look for my phone (it's in my hand), and use the bathroom one more time.

He's had his shoes on for ages, standing by the door like a statue waiting to erode.
He's stopped rushing me, because rushing doesn't work. He's learned one thing: when I say 'I'm ready,' it's an emotion, not a fact.

— Stay together long enough and you learn that some 'I'm ready's are declarations, some are trailers, and the feature presentation is still a while off.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who's always waiting at the door until he nearly turns to stone, or to the self who says 'ready' but always has one more thing — the joke being 'my ready isn't a promise, it's a mood'
variations: Extended version: eventually he invented a conversion formula — first 'I'm ready,' he doesn't move; second 'okay REALLY ready,' he starts putting on his shoes; third 'come on, let's GO,' he's finally sure it's real this time. The three-confirmation system is wisdom he earned standing at the door countless times.

Twist version: until one day he was the one who said 'I'm ready' and then started puttering around looking for things, and I stood waiting by the door, and suddenly I wasn't annoyed at all — oh, so this is what being the one who waits feels like, no rush, because you know someone's willing to wait. Since that day we're both late, but neither of us rushes the other.

items.sourceNote: 兩個人共撐一把傘、結果兩邊都淋濕的梗，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
下雨天，一把傘，兩個人。

他把傘往我這邊移，我把傘往他那邊推。
來回三次，達成一個結論：兩個人都淋濕了。

傘中間那塊乾的地方，成了無人認領的三不管地帶。誰都不肯站進去，因為站進去就代表對方在淋雨。

回到家，兩個人一邊的肩膀都是濕的——他右肩，我左肩，剛好對稱。
他說：「早知道各撐各的。」
我說：「那多沒意思。」

——一把傘遮不住兩個人，但兩個人都想遮住對方。淋濕的那半邊肩膀，是這個世界上最溫柔的浪費。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個跟你共撐一把傘、寧可自己淋濕也要把傘給你的另一半，笑點兼甜點在於『我們都淋濕了，但我們都覺得值得』
variations: 延伸版：後來我買了一把大到誇張的傘，想說這下總不會淋濕了吧。結果我們還是各往對方那邊推，還是各濕一邊。原來濕的不是因為傘太小，是因為兩個人都太想把好的留給對方。傘再大也治不好這種病，而我也不想治。
反轉版：有一次雨很大，他乾脆把傘整個塞到我手上，自己衝進雨裡去攔計程車。等他回來全身濕透，我把傘往他頭上舉，他笑著說「來不及了啦」。那天我才懂，有些人愛你的方式，就是替你先淋那場雨。

en.content:
A rainy day, one umbrella, two people.

He tilts the umbrella toward me, I push it back toward him.
Three rounds of this, and we reach a conclusion: we're both wet.

The dry patch in the middle becomes unclaimed no-man's-land. Neither of us will stand in it, because standing there means the other one's getting rained on.

Back home, one shoulder each is soaked — his right, my left, perfectly symmetrical.
He says, 'we should've just each brought our own.'
I say, 'where's the fun in that.'

— One umbrella can't cover two people, but two people both want to cover the other. That soaked half of your shoulder is the gentlest waste in this world.
en.editorNote: Send to the partner who shares one umbrella with you and would rather get wet than let you — the joke, and the sweetness, being 'we both got soaked and we both think it was worth it'
variations: Extended version: later I bought a ridiculously huge umbrella, figuring now surely no one gets wet. We still pushed it toward each other, still ended up wet on one side each. Turns out the wetness wasn't from the umbrella being small — it's from two people both wanting to give the good half away. No umbrella cures that disease, and I don't want to cure it.

Twist version: once it was pouring and he just shoved the whole umbrella into my hands and dashed into the rain to flag a taxi. When he came back soaked through, I held the umbrella up over his head and he laughed, 'too late now.' That day I understood — some people love you by taking the rain first, in your place.
